title: padRows allocates a fresh multi-MB buffer every frame on the GPU-compose upload hot path
desc: uploadTexture() always calls padRows(), which allocates a brand-new Uint8Array(paddedRowBytes * height) and copies row-by-row whenever rowBytes !== align(rowBytes,256). Two of the four per-frame uploads always hit this branch: the world window (rowBytes = WIN_W*4 = 703*4 = 2812, padded to 2816) and the overlay (rowBytes = VIEW_W*8 = 575*8 = 4600, padded to 4608). That is ~2816*519 + ~4608*391 ≈ 3.3MB of fresh allocation plus two full memcpy loops every single composed frame, feeding GC pressure on the render path. The light and LUT uploads happen to be 256-aligned so they pass through, masking the problem.
evidence: padRows: `const padded = new Uint8Array(paddedRowBytes * height); for (let row...) padded.set(bytes.subarray(...), row*paddedRowBytes);` — called unconditionally from uploadTexture each frame for winBytes (WIN_W*4=2812≠2816) and overlay.half (VIEW_W*8=4600≠4608).
reco: Allocate two reusable padded scratch buffers (one sized WIN_H*align(WIN_W*4,256), one VIEW_H*align(VIEW_W*8,256)) as instance fields and have padRows fill into a caller-supplied buffer, mirroring how the rest of this class reuses winBytes/lightData/overlay.half. Alternatively size WIN_W so WIN_W*4 is already 256-aligned. This removes the only per-frame heap churn in the compose path.
verify: Verified in WebGpuLiveCompose.ts. VIEW_W=575/VIEW_H=391 (config/constants.ts), WIN_W=703, WIN_H=519. padRows (lines 152-160) is called unconditionally from uploadTexture (line 814) and allocates `new Uint8Array(paddedRowBytes*height)` plus a row-by-row memcpy whenever rowBytes != align(rowBytes,256). World window upload (line 641): rowBytes=703*4=2812, padded to 2816 -> allocates. Overlay upload (line 686): rowBytes=575*8=4600, padded to 4608 -> allocates. Light (LIGHT_W*16=4608) and LUT (256*4=1024) are 256-aligned so they pass through (return same view). So 2 of 4 uploads allocate every composed frame (~1.46MB + ~1.80MB = ~3.3MB) + two memcpy loops. Real per-frame GC pressure on the render path. Note: WebGPU writeTexture genuinely requires bytesPerRow aligned to 256, so a staging copy for non-aligned rows is unavoidable; the correct fix is a persistent reused scratch buffer, not eliminating the copy. Still a legitimate high-impact perf finding.

title: Per-frame O(objects×markers) and O(objects×links) Array.find scans in the render loop
desc: Every animation frame the marker-positioning loop iterates this.markers (one entry per visible object/light) and for each entry calls this.doc.objects.find(...) and this.doc.lights.find(...). drawCanvas does the same for every link (find fromId, find toId over doc.objects). Both are O(n·m) per frame where n = object/light count. For a large authored level (hundreds of objects/links) this is quadratic work on the rAF hot path, with no early-out, when the data is already keyed by id.
evidence: loop(): `for (const [id, el] of this.markers) { const obj = this.doc.objects.find((o) => o.id === id); const light = obj ? null : this.doc.lights.find((l) => l.id === id); ... }`. drawCanvas(): `for (const l of this.doc.links) { const from = this.doc.objects.find((o) => o.id === l.fromId); const to = this.doc.objects.find((o) => o.id === l.toId); ... }`.
reco: Build a Map<string, EditorObject|EditorLight> once per doc revision (or iterate doc.objects/doc.lights directly and look the marker up from this.markers, which is already a Map). Store the record reference on the marker (Map<id,{el,ref}>) so the per-frame loop is O(visible). Resolve link endpoints from the same id→record map.
verify: Verified at lines 9264-9283 (marker loop), 9434-9462 (link wires), 9465-9467 (link-in-progress). loop is the per-frame rAF callback (defined 9200, re-requests rAF at 9202). this.markers is a Map<string,HTMLDivElement> keyed by id (line 972), yet the loop calls this.doc.objects.find / this.doc.lights.find for each marker; drawCanvas does two this.doc.objects.find per link. Genuinely O(n*m) per frame on the hot path with data already keyed by id. High is defensible for large authored levels; could be argued medium for typical small levels, but the finding holds.

title: Document mood `ambient` is floored to 0 on import/share (data loss + pitch-black playtest)
desc: `mood.ambient` is the ambient LIGHT LEVEL, a small float validated in [0.02, 0.6] by the Builder UI (Builder.ts:10294) and applied directly as `ctx.params.global.ambient` during playtest (compile.ts:52-53). But `sanitizeImportedDoc` sanitizes it with `clampInt(raw.mood.ambient, 0, 0xffffff)`, which `Math.floor`s the value as if it were a 24-bit color. Any in-range ambient (e.g. 0.3) becomes `Math.floor(0.3)` = 0. Because EVERY ingestion path (localStorage library load via loadDocLibrary->sanitizeImportedDoc, share-code decode, JSON import, and even migrateSandboxSave) routes through this sanitizer, a saved/shared document's authored ambient is destroyed the next time it is opened, and the compiled playtest then sets global ambient to 0 -- a pitch-black level.
evidence: `ambient: num(raw.mood.ambient) ? clampInt(raw.mood.ambient, 0, 0xffffff) : null,` where `clampInt = (value, min, max) => Math.max(min, Math.min(max, Math.floor(value)))`. The authoring range is `min: 0.02, max: 0.6` (inspectorSchemas.ts:109-111) and Builder.ts:10294 rejects anything outside `0.02..0.6`.
reco: Sanitize ambient as a clamped float in the real range, mirroring the UI: `ambient: num(raw.mood.ambient) ? clampNum(raw.mood.ambient, 0.02, 0.6) : null`. Add a round-trip test (capture a doc with ambient 0.3, run through sanitizeImportedDoc, assert it survives).
verify: Verified in document.ts:693 `ambient: ... clampInt(raw.mood.ambient, 0, 0xffffff)` and clampInt at 454-455 does `Math.floor`. UI (Builder.ts:10293-10301) stores ambient as a float Number(raw) validated to [0.02,0.6]; compile.ts:52-53 applies it directly as ctx.params.global.ambient (the Sim 'Ambient Light' slider range is 0.02..0.5). Any authored value like 0.3 floors to 0 on re-ingestion. Confirmed every ingestion path routes through sanitizeImportedDoc: loadDocLibrary (line 492, localStorage), shareCodeToDoc (line 778), Builder JSON import (4043), migrateSandboxSave (750). Number.isFinite(0) is true and 0!==null, so compile sets ambient=0 -> pitch-black playtest. Real data-loss bug; high is correct.

title: FNV-1a string hash copy-pasted across 4+ files; Builder's copy is a comment-acknowledged clone of Levels' seed fold
desc: The exact byte-identical FNV-1a-over-a-string loop (seed 0x811c9dc5, prime 0x01000193) is independently re-implemented as a private function in at least four files: Levels.hashString (level id -> expedition seed), Builder.hashLevelId, AssetPreview.fnv1a, and twice in sprites.ts (spriteContentSig fold + spritePhase). core/rng.ts already exports hashSeed() (the canonical FNV fold) and world/virtual/hash.ts exports fnv1aBytes(). The Builder copy's own comment reads 'Tiny FNV-1a over the level id, matching Levels' expedition seed fold' — i.e. the two MUST stay byte-identical for campaign seed reproducibility, yet they are physically separate copies with no shared source. If one is ever 'optimized' or tweaked, Builder playtest seeds silently diverge from real expedition seeds.
evidence: Levels.ts hashString: `let h = 0x811c9dc5; for(...) { h ^= s.charCodeAt(i); h = Math.imul(h, 0x01000193); } return h>>>0;`. Builder.ts hashLevelId is the same body with comment 'matching Levels' expedition seed fold'. AssetPreview.ts fnv1a and sprites.ts spritePhase are again the same body.
reco: Extract one shared `fnv1aString(s: string, seed = 0x811c9dc5): number` into core (next to hashSeed in core/rng.ts, a foundation module). Have Levels, Builder, AssetPreview and sprites import it. This removes the silent-divergence risk on the seed-reproducibility path specifically.
verify: Verified all cited sites: Levels.ts:2249 hashString, Builder.ts:319 hashLevelId (with the explicit comment 'matching Levels' expedition seed fold'), AssetPreview.ts:219 fnv1a, sprites.ts:365 spriteContentSig fold and 407 spritePhase — all byte-identical (0x811c9dc5 / 0x01000193). Canonical core/rng.ts exports hashSeed (same constants) and world/virtual/hash.ts exports fnv1aBytes. Builder<->Levels seed reproducibility hinge on two physically-separate copies. Real coupling risk; high is fair.

title: Corrupt level-blob RLE throws out of enterLevel mid-transition (progression-lock)
desc: enterLevel() restores a previously-saved level by calling restoreLevel() (line 1657), which calls rleDecode(blob.rle, savedTypes) (line 1544). rleDecode does `atob(rle)` (src/core/rle.ts:22), and atob THROWS InvalidCharacterError on malformed base64. enterLevel is called from the in-game descent/ascend/waystone transitions (lines 534, 562, 589, 886) with NO try/catch — only the resume path at line 1494 wraps it. By the time the throw happens, the curtain is shown (showTransitionCurtain), this._transitioning is set true (line 1640), the cached blob has been deleted (line 1645), and in restoreLevel ctx.world has already been reassigned to a fresh World (line 1534) and ctx.enemies cleared. The exception escapes uncaught, leaving the run wedged: transition half-applied, curtain up, no recovery — a hard progression-lock, violating the fail-open design rule.
evidence: restoreLevel: `rleDecode(blob.rle, savedTypes)` (1544) → rle.ts: `const bin = atob(rle)` (22) which throws on bad input. enterLevel call sites 534/562/589/886 have no surrounding try/catch (contrast the resume path 1493-1497). _transitioning=true and ctx.world swap happen before the throw point.
reco: Wrap the restoreLevel()/createLevel() body of enterLevel (or rleDecode itself) in try/catch; on failure, fall back to createLevel (regenerate pristine) and emit a toast, OR retire the save. At minimum, reset _transitioning and lift the curtain in a finally so a decode failure can't strand the run. Fail-open is an explicit project rule.
verify: Verified end to end. restoreLevel (Levels.ts:1532) does ctx.world = new World() (1534) and ctx.enemies.length=0 (1535) BEFORE rleDecode(blob.rle, savedTypes) (1544); rleDecode calls atob(rle) (rle.ts:22) which throws InvalidCharacterError on non-base64 input. enterLevel sets _transitioning=true (1640) and shows the curtain (1642) before calling restoreLevel (1657). _transitioning is only reset in finishTransitionWithCurtain at the END of enterLevel (1712) or curtain callbacks (336/912) — none run if restoreLevel throws, so update() early-returns forever (525) with the curtain up: a hard progression lock, violating fail-open. The transition call sites (534 well-fall, 562 portal-descend, 589 vault-arch, 886 debug) have NO try/catch; only the resume path (1493-1497) is wrapped. Reachable for a save whose CURRENT level blob is fine but a deeper unentered level's blob is corrupt — resume succeeds, then the descent into that level detonates. Confirmed at high.

title: Expedition save-shape validator never inspects per-level blob contents
desc: isExpeditionSaveShape validates the top-level save (v===1, currentId, expeditionSeed, score, player fields) and checks `Array.isArray(save.levels)` — but never validates any element of save.levels. Each SavedLevelBlob is then stored verbatim into savedBlobs (line 1475) and later fed to restoreLevel, which trusts blob.rle (string for atob), blob.life/blob.charge (index/value pairs), blob.explored (base64), blob.enemies, etc. A save that is structurally valid at the top level but has a missing/garbage rle, a non-array life list, or out-of-range indices passes validation and detonates later inside a transition (see the related enterLevel finding). This is the root cause that turns a minor corruption into an uncaught mid-transition throw.
evidence: `Array.isArray(save.levels)` (1424) is the ONLY check on levels; no per-blob shape guard. Contrast the careful field-by-field player validation immediately following (1427-1435).
reco: Add a per-blob validator (typeof blob.id==='string', typeof blob.rle==='string', Array.isArray(blob.life/charge/enemies/pickups/...)) and reject/retire the whole save if any blob fails — validate at load time, where retireSavedExpedition already exists, rather than crashing at transition time.
verify: isExpeditionSaveShape (1414-1436) validates v===1, currentId, expeditionSeed, score, loadout, and does a careful field-by-field player check, but for levels it does ONLY Array.isArray(save.levels) (1424) — zero per-blob validation. Each blob is stored verbatim into savedBlobs (1475) and later trusted by restoreLevel for blob.rle (atob), blob.life/charge (index/value pairs), blob.explored (base64), blob.enemies. This is the root cause that lets a top-level-valid save carry a garbage rle that detonates mid-transition (finding 0). High is defensible given the hard-lock consequence and the explicit fail-open rule, though note it requires a tampered/partially-corrupted localStorage save rather than a normal-play condition.

title: Restored wand card ids are unvalidated; compileWand crashes on an unknown id
desc: loadLoadout() and restoreRuntimeState() push saved card strings straight into w.cards (w.cards.push(...saved.cards)) and this.collection.push(...data.collection) with no check that each entry is a real CardId or null. compileWand() then does `const def = CARD_DEFS[id]; if (def.kind === 'modifier')` with no guard, so an id not present in CARD_DEFS makes def undefined and `def.kind` throws a TypeError. program() compiles lazily on fire, and the throw is not caught, so the wand throws every cast attempt. The expedition save-shape guard (isExpeditionSaveShape) does not inspect loadout.wands[].cards or wands.collection at all, so a stale save (a card later removed/renamed — the codebase has a documented history of card remaps) or a hand-edited save loads cleanly and then crashes wand firing.
evidence: WandSystem.restoreRuntimeState: `w.cards.push(...saved.cards)` then padding; collection.push(...data.collection). compiler.compileWand line 103-104: `const def = CARD_DEFS[id]; if (def.kind === 'modifier')` — no `if (!def) continue`. ALL_CARD_IDS exists (cards.ts:257) and could drive validation but isn't used here.
reco: Filter saved cards/collection through a known-id check on restore (keep only ALL_CARD_IDS.includes(id) or null), AND make compileWand defensively skip unknown ids: `const def = CARD_DEFS[id]; if (!def) continue;` inside the loop. Either alone fixes the crash; doing both is belt-and-suspenders for save-ABI drift.
verify: Verified compiler.ts:103-104 does `const def = CARD_DEFS[id]; if (def.kind === 'modifier')` with no `!def` guard; an id absent from CARD_DEFS makes def undefined and `def.kind` throws TypeError. restoreRuntimeState (WandSystem.ts:763-764) and loadLoadout (724) do `w.cards.push(...saved.cards)` with zero validation, and isExpeditionSaveShape (Levels.ts:1414-1436) never inspects loadout.wands[].cards or collection. Compilation is lazy: fire() (215) -> program() (914-921) -> compileWand(), with NO try/catch, so every cast attempt throws once a bad id is in a slot. buildWandSentenceView (sentenceView.ts:309) also calls compileWand, so the wand-editor UI throws on render too. The restore is wrapped in try/catch (Levels.ts:1503) but the bad data is stored and only detonates later on fire — outside that catch. ALL_CARD_IDS (cards.ts:257) exists and could drive validation but isn't used on the load path. Real crash-on-fire from a stale/hand-edited save. High is defensible; the practical trigger requires a removed/renamed card or hand-edit (no card has actually been removed yet, and saves are gated by genVersion + review-save checks), so medium would also be reasonable, but the impact (every cast throws, soft-lock combat) keeps it at high.

title: Post-FX TSL pipeline duplicated verbatim between DataTexture and StorageTexture source variants
desc: buildPostOutputNode (DataTexture source) and buildStoragePostOutputNode (StorageTexture source) are ~60 lines of identical bloom/aberration/grain/hurt-vignette TSL that differ only in how the source is sampled (texture(this.texture,...) vs storageSampleRgb(storageTexture,...)). The same split exists for buildBaseOutputNode vs buildStorageBaseOutputNode. Any tuning change to bloom taps, grain hash, chromatic aberration, or hurt mix must be made in two places and will silently drift between the WebGL-data path and the GPU-compose path if only one is edited.
evidence: Both functions compute the same `tap1/tap2`, `c0..c6` bright-color taps, `grainHash`, `lumaWeight`, `hurtMix`, `lensed`, and `post` with identical magic constants (0.28/0.12/0.06, 12.9898/78.233/43758.5453, 0.18/0.55, etc.); only the `sampleRgb`/`base` source differs.
reco: Extract a single post-node builder parameterized by a `sampleRgb(uv)` closure (and a `base` sampler), then call it from both the DataTexture and StorageTexture sites. The base-output nodes can collapse the same way. This is pure dedup with no behavior change.
verify: Verified WebGpuRenderBackend.ts lines 337-397 (buildPostOutputNode, DataTexture source) vs 399-457 (buildStoragePostOutputNode, StorageTexture source). The two bodies are byte-for-byte identical post-FX TSL apart from how the source is sampled: line 350 `texture(source,p).rgb` / line 341 sampleRgb via texture(this.texture) vs line 410 `sampleRgb(p)` / line 401 sampleRgb via storageSampleRgb. Same tap1/tap2, c0..c6, identical magic constants 0.28/0.12/0.06, 12.9898/78.233/43758.5453, 0.18/0.55, grainHash, lumaWeight, hurtMix, lensed, post. The smaller base-node split (buildBaseOutputNode 323-326 vs buildStorageBaseOutputNode 332-335) also exists. Genuine drift hazard; medium is fair.

title: Gauge-rescue rebuilds full-grid wizard/reachable masks O(features × passes) times per level
desc: The gauge-rescue block recomputes wizardMask() and reachableMask() inside rescueAt() (up to twice per call: once after the nearestWiz tunnel, once after the spawn-direct fallback), and rescueAt is invoked once per cut-off mechanism, rune vault, key, waystone, and cauldron. wizardMask itself calls fitsOf() (which allocates the rubble mask + hRun + fits) and allocates seen + two Int32Array queues sized WIDTH*HEIGHT (1600*1064 = ~1.7M each); reachableMask allocates three more. On a tight seed with many cut-off features this is dozens of full-grid BFS passes plus tens of MB of short-lived typed-array churn, all synchronous behind the level-load curtain.
evidence: Inside rescueAt: `wiz = wizardMask({...}); cell = reachableMask({...}); if (pass()) return true;` then again after the fallback tunnelTo (lines 1018-1024). recordRescue→rescueAt is called for every door/valve/hands-on/cell-reach mechanism, every runeVault, every key pickup, every waystone, and the cauldron (lines 1051-1132). The pipeline already has a DEV timer that warns when generateLevel crosses 400ms (lines 1148-1156).
reco: Recompute the masks once per rescue iteration only when the world actually changed, or batch all cut-off features and run a single mask rebuild after carving all of them (re-validating afterward), rather than rebuilding inside each per-feature attempt. At minimum, reuse preallocated scratch buffers across mask builds instead of allocating fresh Int32Array(W*H) every call.
verify: Verified in validate.ts: wizardMask allocates fits (which itself allocates blocks+hRun+fits = 3 full-grid Uint8Array) plus seen + qx/qy Int32Array (each WIDTH*HEIGHT=1600*1064=1,702,400, Int32 = ~6.8MB each); reachableMask allocates seen+qx+qy. CaveGenerator.ts rescueAt (1011-1025) recomputes BOTH masks at 1018-1019 and AGAIN at 1022-1023, so up to 2 full re-derivations per call. recordRescue/rescueAt is invoked per cut-off door/valve/hands-on/cell-reach mechanism, each runeVault, each key pickup, each waystone, and the cauldron (1051-1132). Early-continue paths mean it's per CUT-OFF feature, but on a tight seed that is genuinely dozens of full-grid BFS passes + tens of MB of churn synchronously during level load. Accurate; medium fits.

title: Large copy-pasted stamp-floor-and-connect boilerplate across every arena/vault block
desc: placeStructures is a single 1633-line function in which the same pattern is hand-inlined a dozen times: carvePocket a room, then a `for dx ... { const Y = cy+11; if (inBounds && types===Empty) { types=Stone; colors=stoneColor(); } }` floor strip, then connectToCaves. The mechanism vault, Wave E chamber, Kiln, Sump, branch dais, and vault hoard each repeat this verbatim with only the radius and offsets changed. The same is true of the 'stamp a metal casing rectangle' loops (rune vault, sump, kiln tank, live-circuit vault). This duplication is why the file is enormous and why the gauge/apron/skin fix comments had to be repeated; a single `carveRoomWithFloor(cx, cy, rx, ry)` and `stampMetalBox(...)` helper (analogous to the already-extracted carvePocket/carveRect/tunnelTo in connect.ts) would collapse hundreds of lines and make the Metal-preserving invariant enforced in one place instead of ~15.
evidence: e.g. lines 620-627, 646-653, 849-856, 1450-1458 are byte-for-byte the same floor-strip loop differing only in the dx bound and center variable; the metal-casing rectangle appears at 717-731, 1244-1255, 1172-1191, 1078-1085 with copy-pasted `packRGB(96,102,112)`.
reco: Extract the room-floor and metal-box stamping into shared helpers in world/connect.ts (or a new world/stamps.ts) and call them from each block; this also centralizes the 'skip Cell.Metal' guard.
verify: Verified: file is 1632 lines, placeStructures starts at line 58 (single ~1575-line function). The floor-strip loop `for dx ... const Y = center+11; if Empty → Stone/stoneColor()` appears verbatim at 620-627, 646-653, 849-856, 1450-1458 (4 `const Y = X+11` instances plus offset variants); grep shows 28 stone-stamp type/color pairs and 8 copies of metal-casing `packRGB(96,102,112)` (lines 359,959,997,1181,1248,1254,1335,1341). Duplication and the repeated Metal-preserving invariant are real. Medium is borderline-high for pure duplication (no behavioral bug), but defensible given the scale and the safety-critical Metal-skip repeated ~15x. Keeping medium.

title: Single-chunk cancel strands the caller promise and leaks the worker's canceled set
desc: On `generateChunk`, the worker checks `if (canceled.has(msg.req.jobId)) return;` and returns WITHOUT posting any response and WITHOUT removing the id from `canceled`. The matching TsWorkerBackend stores `{resolve,reject}` in pendingChunks and never settles it, so that promise hangs forever. The `cancel` handler also unconditionally `canceled.add(jobId)` and posts `canceled` even for jobs that already completed (line 27-30), so the set grows without bound across the worker's lifetime (window jobs delete their id, but stray/late cancels and any cancelled single-chunk jobs never do).
evidence: Worker generateChunk: `if (canceled.has(msg.req.jobId)) return;` (no postMessage, no delete). Worker cancel: `canceled.add(msg.jobId); worker.postMessage({kind:'canceled', jobId})` with no bound. TsWorkerBackend.generateChunk leaves the resolver in pendingChunks until a chunk/error/canceled message arrives.
reco: In the generateChunk cancel branch, post `{kind:'canceled', jobId}` and `canceled.delete(jobId)` so the backend's rejectJob settles the pending promise; bound the canceled set (e.g. delete the id after handling, or only track in-flight window jobs).
verify: Verified worldgen.worker.ts:33-39: generateChunk does `if (canceled.has(msg.req.jobId)) return;` with no postMessage and no delete of the id. TsWorkerBackend.generateChunk (lines 48-54) stores {resolve,reject} in pendingChunks and only settles on a chunk/error/canceled message, so that promise hangs forever for a pre-canceled single chunk. The window path DOES post 'canceled' and delete (lines 51-54, 72), but single-chunk cancels never delete and the cancel handler unconditionally adds (line 28) even for completed jobs, so the canceled set grows unbounded. Both claims accurate; medium appropriate.

title: Per-frame O(objects) find() for every marker and link wire makes the editor loop O(n^2)
desc: The requestAnimationFrame loop positions DOM markers by re-resolving each marker id with this.doc.objects.find(...) and this.doc.lights.find(...) every frame (line 9265-9266). drawCanvas (also every frame) re-resolves both endpoints of every link with this.doc.objects.find(...) twice per link (9435-9436), plus a find for the in-progress link wire (9466). Marker count and link count both scale with the document, and each find is a linear scan over doc.objects, so the loop is O(markers*objects) + O(links*objects) per frame on top of an already heavy draw.
evidence: loop(): `for (const [id, el] of this.markers) { const obj = this.doc.objects.find((o) => o.id === id); const light = obj ? null : this.doc.lights.find((l) => l.id === id); ...}`. drawCanvas(): `for (const l of this.doc.links) { const from = this.doc.objects.find((o) => o.id === l.fromId); const to = this.doc.objects.find((o) => o.id === l.toId); ...}`. There are 20 `this.doc.objects.find` call sites in the file; the marker map already keys by id but stores only the element.
reco: Build a Map<string, EditorObject> (and Map<string, EditorLight>) once per doc-mutation (syncMarkers already rebuilds on change) and reuse it in the per-frame loop and the link-wire draw. Or store the resolved object/light reference as the marker map value instead of re-finding by id each frame.
verify: Verified at lines 9264-9266 (marker glue loop), 9435-9436 (per-link endpoint resolution), 9466 (in-progress wire). The `markers` map is `Map<string, HTMLDivElement>` (line 972) storing only the element, so each frame resolves ids via this.doc.objects.find / this.doc.lights.find — linear scans. The loop is a per-frame requestAnimationFrame (line 9200-9202, gated only by isOpen). So author-mode editing is O(markers*objects + links*objects) per frame. Real and accurately described. Downgrading from high to medium: this is editor-authoring-only (not the gameplay sim hot path), object/link counts in a single level document are modest, and the marker loop already early-exits non-visible markers; it's a real but bounded inefficiency, not a high-severity problem.

title: loadSelectedDocument and openDocumentAsset skip sanitizeImportedDoc used by every other load path
desc: importDocumentFile, importShareCode, and offerDraft all pass loaded data through sanitizeImportedDoc(...) before adopting it. The two paths that read from the local document library / asset store instead do a raw `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(saved)) as EditorDocument` with no validation. A library entry written by an older/buggier build, a partially-corrupted localStorage value, or a hand-edited entry will be adopted unsanitized, then replaceDocument -> applyDocTerrain decodes doc.world and syncAll reads doc.objects/lights/links, where missing or malformed fields can throw and leave the editor in a half-replaced state.
evidence: loadSelectedDocument: `const doc = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(saved)) as EditorDocument;` then `this.replaceDocument(doc, ...)`. openDocumentAsset: `const doc = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(latest.payload)) as EditorDocument;`. Compare importDocumentFile: `const doc = parsed === null ? null : sanitizeImportedDoc(parsed); if (!doc) { this.status('NOT A BUILDER DOCUMENT', true); return; }`.
reco: Run library/asset-store documents through sanitizeImportedDoc(...) (or at least a shape guard) before replaceDocument, and surface a clear error instead of throwing if it returns null.
verify: Verified: loadSelectedDocument line 3866 does `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(saved)) as EditorDocument` then replaceDocument, no sanitize. openDocumentAsset line 12201 does the same with latest.payload. Meanwhile the import paths DO sanitize: line 4043 `sanitizeImportedDoc(parsed)` and line 8719 `sanitizeImportedDoc(draft.doc)` for drafts. So library/asset-store load paths bypass the validation every external-input path uses. A corrupted or older-schema localStorage entry would be adopted unvalidated. Real inconsistency and medium severity is fair (untrusted-ish persisted data, though same-origin localStorage is lower risk than file/share-code import).

title: Document terrain RLE is not length-validated before acceptance (prefab path is, document path isn't)
desc: `sanitizeWorldLayer` accepts any RLE string that `rleDecode` doesn't throw on, decoding into a fixed `new Uint8Array(WIDTH*HEIGHT)` scratch. `rleDecode` (core/rle.ts:21-30) does not bound-check `pos`: runs summing to MORE than WIDTH*HEIGHT are silently truncated by `TypedArray.fill`'s clamp, and runs summing to LESS leave trailing cells as Empty. So a corrupt or hostile share code / imported JSON with a mismatched run total is accepted as a valid-looking but wrong terrain layer rather than rejected. The prefab importer guards exactly this with `if (rleLength(p.rle) !== w * h) return null;` (prefablib.ts:646) plus a per-cell `< CELL_COUNT` check; the document importer has no equivalent.
evidence: Document path: `try { rleDecode(layer.rle, new Uint8Array(WIDTH * HEIGHT)); } catch { return null; }` then returns `{ rle: layer.rle, ... }` with no decoded-length check. Prefab path: `if (rleLength(p.rle) !== w * h) return null;` plus `for (const cell of cells) if (cell >= CELL_COUNT) return null;`.
reco: Add the same decoded-length guard (and ideally the per-cell `< CELL_COUNT` check) to `sanitizeWorldLayer` that prefablib.sanitizePrefab already uses, so a malformed terrain layer is rejected rather than silently mis-decoded.
verify: Verified sanitizeWorldLayer (document.ts:563-580) only does `try { rleDecode(layer.rle, new Uint8Array(WIDTH*HEIGHT)); } catch { return null; }` then returns the raw rle, with no length or per-cell check. rleDecode (rle.ts:21-30) uses into.fill(t,pos,pos+run) which clamps without throwing, so a mismatched run total is silently truncated/padded. The prefab path (prefablib.ts:646-651) guards with `rleLength(p.rle) !== w*h` AND a per-cell `>= CELL_COUNT` reject. The document path has neither (it also misses the CELL_COUNT bound, slightly worse than stated). Real asymmetry; medium is reasonable for an import-of-untrusted-data robustness gap.

title: Importer dedups IDs per-family, so an object and a light can share an ID that validation then reports as a playtest-blocking duplicate
desc: `sanitizeImportedDoc` uses three independent `Set`s for ID uniqueness -- `objectIds`, `linkIds`, `lightIds` (and `procIds`). So `uniqueId` guarantees uniqueness only WITHIN a record family. But `validateDocument` pools all three families into one set and reports any cross-family collision as `builder.id.duplicate`, which is in `PLAYTEST_BLOCKING_CODES` (validate.ts:66-74). A shared/imported document whose object and light happen to share an ID (e.g. both 'x' from a hand-edited or third-party file) survives sanitization but is then permanently un-playtestable with a 'duplicate id' error the user cannot easily resolve from the UI.
evidence: document.ts: `const objectIds = new Set<string>();` ... `const linkIds = new Set<string>();` ... `const lightIds = new Set<string>();` -- each `uniqueId(..., set)` call uses only its own set. validate.ts:686-695 pools `...objects.map(o=>o.id), ...lights.map(l=>l.id), ...links.map(l=>l.id)` into one `ids` set and pushes `builder.id.duplicate` on any repeat.
reco: Share a single `usedIds` Set across objects, links, and lights in `sanitizeImportedDoc` so the importer enforces the same global uniqueness the validator requires.
verify: Verified sanitizeImportedDoc uses separate sets objectIds/linkIds/lightIds/procIds (document.ts:664,669,676,683) and uniqueId (513-520) only dedups within the passed set. The validator (validate.ts:686-695) pools objects+lights+links into ONE `ids` set and pushes 'builder.id.duplicate' on any repeat, which IS in PLAYTEST_BLOCKING_CODES (66-74). So an imported/hand-edited doc where an object and a light share an id passes sanitization but is permanently un-playtestable. Confirmed via isPlaytestBlockingIssue/playtestBlockingIssues (97,104) gating compileAndPlaytest (compile.ts:43-44). Medium is fair.

title: WaveDirector is entirely dead code; ARCHITECTURE.md still lists waveCtl.update in the frame order
desc: The WaveDirector class is constructed in Game.ts (ctx.waveCtl = new WaveDirector(ctx)) and assigned to Ctx, but neither WaveDirector.update() nor WaveDirector.start() is ever invoked. The descent (Levels) replaced wave survival — Game.tick (lines 419-447) never calls ctx.waveCtl, and a repo-wide grep for 'waveCtl.' only matches ARCHITECTURE.md. The ctx.waves *state* is still poked by Enemies/Player for kill counting, but the entire WaveDirector implementation (spawn logic, intermission countdown, +25 HP wave-clear) is unreachable. Worse, ARCHITECTURE.md line 167 documents the frame order as '...enemyCtl.update -> rigidBodies.update -> waveCtl.update -> ...', which is now false and will mislead anyone reasoning about the tick.
evidence: Grep for 'waveCtl\.' returns only 'ARCHITECTURE.md:167: ...rigidBodies.update -> waveCtl.update ->'. Game.tick never references ctx.waveCtl. WaveDirector.start is only referenced inside WaveDirector.ts itself (update() calls start()), and update() is never called.
reco: Delete WaveDirector and the waveCtl field on Ctx (or, if ctx.waves kill-tracking is still wanted, keep only createWaveState and drop the class). Update ARCHITECTURE.md's frame-order list to match the real Game.tick sequence so the documented contract stops contradicting the code.
verify: Verified: WaveDirector is constructed at Game.ts:170 (ctx.waveCtl = new WaveDirector(ctx)) and declared on Ctx (types.ts:2168), but a src-wide grep for 'waveCtl.' returns zero call sites — Game.tick (lines 419-447) never invokes ctx.waveCtl.update/start. The tick comment at 434-435 confirms 'The descent replaced wave survival'. update() calls start() internally but update() itself is never called, so the whole class is unreachable from the running game (only console.test.ts stubs it). ARCHITECTURE.md:167 still documents '...rigidBodies.update -> waveCtl.update ->' which is stale/false. Real dead-code + stale-doc finding. Downgraded high->medium: it is genuinely dead and misleads the documented frame order, but it causes no runtime bug, and ctx.waves state is still used for kill counting, so it is not a pure orphan.

title: serializeLevel persists whole Mechanism objects including fields the type marks 'transient, not persisted' (incl. live RigidBody refs)
desc: serializeLevel stores `mechanisms: rt.mechanisms` (the live runtime objects), which is then JSON.stringified by writeExpeditionSave. The Mechanism type explicitly documents several fields as transient/not-persisted: dispBodies (RigidBody[] — '...transient, not persisted'), dissolve (in-progress retraction cells), seqPrev/fuseT/dispCoolT. dispBodies holds live RigidBody references (which carry an onTerrainHit function and a free-form data record); JSON.stringify silently strips the function but serializes a detached snapshot. On restore, blob.mechanisms is used verbatim as the runtime list (restoreLevel line 1591), so a dispenser would come back with dispBodies full of phantom bodies that don't exist in ctx.rigidBodies — dispense()'s cap check (bodies.length >= cap) would then count phantoms and throttle/stop real emissions, and ctx.rigidBodies.remove(phantom) is a no-op. dissolve being persisted mid-open restores a door whose retraction queue points at cells already cleared. Currently dispensers only exist in the non-saveable physics-test arena, so the dispBodies path is latent, but the pattern is wrong and the dissolve/seqPrev fields do leak into real campaign saves.
evidence: serializeLevel: `mechanisms: rt.mechanisms` (no field stripping). types.ts:1527 `dispBodies?: RigidBody[];` commented '(transient, not persisted)'; types.ts:1471 dissolve, 1502 prevWant, 1525 dispCoolT all transient. restoreLevel:1591 `mechanisms: blob.mechanisms` consumes them back verbatim.
reco: Serialize a sanitized projection of each mechanism (id, kind, x, y, w, h, state, targetId, logic, pressed, broken, threshold, zone, material, oneShot, seqDone/seqFired, plug breakFrac/reading, etc.) and explicitly omit transient runtime fields (dispBodies, dissolve, fuseT, dispCoolT, prevWant, closeT). At minimum drop dispBodies before stringify so live body references never enter the save.
verify: Verified serializeLevel (Levels.ts:1395) stores 'mechanisms: rt.mechanisms' with no field stripping and restoreLevel (1591) consumes 'mechanisms: blob.mechanisms' verbatim. types.ts confirms the cited transient fields: dispBodies (1527, RigidBody[] '(transient, not persisted)'), dissolve (1471), prevWant (1503), dispCoolT (1525), seqPrev (1460). dispense() (Mechanisms.ts:1284-1290) does count dispBodies.length against cap and ctx.rigidBodies.remove(old) on overflow, so phantom snapshot bodies would throttle real emissions on restore. The author correctly hedges that dispensers only exist in the non-saveable physics-test arena (physicsArena.ts; built fresh on entry at Levels.ts:1698), so the dispBodies path is latent — but dissolve/seqPrev/closeT etc. DO serialize into real campaign saves, and a door persisted mid-open restores a dissolve queue pointing at already-cleared cells. Pattern is structurally wrong and partially live. Medium is fair.

title: screenshot command draws the WebGL canvas outside the rAF callback, intermittently capturing a cleared frame
desc: captureScreenshot awaits a single requestAnimationFrame, then performs g.drawImage(source, 0, 0) after the promise resolves — i.e. in a later microtask, no longer inside the rAF callback. The renderer runs without preserveDrawingBuffer (confirmed in CLAUDE.md probe gotchas and docs/GPU-COMPOSE-PLAN.md), so the GL drawing buffer is only readable synchronously inside the rAF callback; once it returns the buffer may be cleared. The downstream blank-detection (line 919) then sometimes reports 'Screenshot canvas appears blank', making the command flaky rather than deterministically wrong.
evidence: `await new Promise<void>((resolve) => w.requestAnimationFrame(() => resolve()));` (line 884) followed by `g.drawImage(source, 0, 0);` (line 894) — the drawImage executes after the rAF callback has already returned. docs/WEBGPU-BENCHMARK-LEDGER.md and the verify-*.mjs scripts explicitly note 'drawImage of a no-preserveDrawingBuffer WebGL canvas intermittently catches a cleared frame' and sample inside rAF to avoid it.
reco: Perform the drawImage (and ideally the toDataURL/getImageData) synchronously inside the requestAnimationFrame callback, resolving the promise with the captured result, instead of resolving the rAF promise first and drawing afterward.
verify: captureScreenshot (commands.ts 881-928): line 884 awaits a Promise that resolves inside the rAF callback, then line 894 g.drawImage(source,0,0) runs AFTER the await — in a microtask after the rAF callback returned, not inside it. With no preserveDrawingBuffer (per CLAUDE.md probe gotchas), the GL drawing buffer is only reliably readable synchronously inside the rAF callback, so this is exactly the documented intermittently-cleared-frame pattern, and the blank-detection at 919 then reports 'canvas-blank'. Real flakiness; fail-soft so not data-corrupting, but it makes the tool unreliable. Medium stands.

title: Colossus footfall edge-detector reads render-owned `prevG`, never set for colossus → fires every grounded frame
desc: The Kiln Colossus AI gates its screen-shake + `hollowKnock` footfall feedback on a landing edge: `if (e.grounded && !e.prevG) { this.shakeAt(...); ctx.audio.hollowKnock(); }`. But `e.prevG` is written in only one place in the whole codebase — the render layer (`EnemySprites.ts`), and only for kinds `slime`/`acidslime` (line 52) and `bomber` (line 305). It is never written for `colossus` in either the sim or the renderer. So a marching colossus has `prevG === false` permanently, and `e.grounded && !e.prevG` is true on every grounded frame. The footfall shake and hollow-knock sound therefore play continuously while the colossus stands on the ground, instead of once per landing — a feel bug (audio spam / constant rumble) on the run's final boss. It also depends on render-owned state leaking into the simulation, which inverts the events-outward/calls-inward contract.
evidence: Enemies.ts:1216 `if (e.grounded && !e.prevG) { this.shakeAt(e.x, e.y, 0.02, 0.05); ctx.audio.hollowKnock(); }`. Grep for `.prevG =` shows assignments only at EnemySprites.ts:52 (slime/acidslime) and EnemySprites.ts:305 (bomber); no colossus path ever sets prevG=true.
reco: Have the colossus AI own its own landing-edge flag (e.g. set `e.prevG = e.grounded` at the end of the colossus branch, mirroring what the sprite code does for slimes), or introduce a dedicated `e.colossusPrevG` updated in the sim. Better still, stop the sim from reading a render-layer field at all: track the previous grounded state inside `Enemies.update` so the footfall fires exactly once per landing regardless of whether the renderer ran.
verify: Verified at Enemies.ts:1216 `if (e.grounded && !e.prevG) { this.shakeAt(...); ctx.audio.hollowKnock(); }`. Grep for `.prevG =` shows the ONLY writers are EnemySprites.ts:52 (slime/acidslime) and EnemySprites.ts:305 (bomber). The colossus render branch (EnemySprites.ts:438+) never sets prevG, and the colossus sim branch (Enemies.ts:1182-1260) never sets it either. Enemies.ts:1189 sets e.grounded=true while on the floor. So e.prevG stays false permanently → the edge condition fires every grounded frame, spamming shakeAt + hollowKnock continuously on the final boss. Real feel bug; also leaks render-owned animation state into the sim's gating. Medium is fair.

title: Infuser conjures unbounded real material from a fixed 2-cell payment
desc: The Infuser card pays exactly 2 cells out of the flask per cast (WandSystem.markProjectile: flask.count -= 2, then INFUSED.set(p, material)), but the projectile then sheds 2 real grid cells of that material EVERY frame for its entire life with no budget. A bolt (life 180) therefore materializes up to ~360 real cells of, e.g., water/oil into the world from a 2-cell payment. INFUSED is never decremented or deleted. This contradicts the project's stated design pillar that material is conserved ('every stored cell is real; siphoning deletes exactly the cells that pour/throw later return').
evidence: WandSystem.ts:606-610: `INFUSED.set(p, flask.material); flask.count = Math.max(0, flask.count - 2); if (flask.count === 0) flask.material = null;`. Projectiles.ts:609-623: `const infuseMat = INFUSED.get(p); ... for (let d = 0; d < 2; d++) { ... world.replaceCellAt(ti, infuseMat, ...) }` — runs unconditionally every frame with no budget counter, unlike shedTrail which uses waterTrailBudget/oilTrailBudget.
reco: Give INFUSED a per-projectile cell budget mirroring shedTrail (e.g. store {material, budget} and decrement per placed cell, deleting the mark at 0), or charge the flask proportionally. As-is the infuser is an infinite material fountain that breaks conservation. If this over-generation is intentional, the design comment claiming the flask 'pays' for the trail should be corrected.
verify: Verified in Projectiles.ts:609-622 and WandSystem.ts:603-610. INFUSED.set(p, material) is paired with flask.count -= 2 ONCE at cast (markProjectile). The per-frame update loop reads INFUSED and unconditionally sheds up to 2 real cells (replaceCellAt) every frame for the projectile's whole life with no budget counter — unlike the adjacent shedTrail which uses waterTrailBudget/oilTrailBudget. grep confirms INFUSED is only set (WandSystem:606) and read (Projectiles:609), never decremented/deleted. A wisp (life 240) or bolt (life 180) materializes hundreds of cells from a 2-cell payment, directly violating the documented conservation pillar (docs/design-panel-raw.md:327: 'conservation: you cannot dupe matter'). Medium is fair: real material spawn from fixed payment, but it is a combat trail effect gated by mana+slot, not a storable-resource dupe.

title: Spells class doc claims firePlayerSpell is the play-mode dispatch, but wands replaced it; method is play-mode dead
desc: The Spells class doc says it owns 'the play-mode per-frame casting dispatch (firePlayerSpell)'. In the real game, play-mode firing goes through ctx.wands.fire() (Player.ts:1766), and firePlayerSpell is only ever called from builder/gallery.ts (spell-preview choreography). The 80-line, 16-branch method duplicates projectile-spawn logic that is now authoritatively in WandSystem.castActionAt. The doc actively misleads a maintainer into thinking this is the live combat path.
evidence: Player.ts:1766 `if (player.firing) ctx.wands.fire(ctx);` is the play path. Grep shows firePlayerSpell callers are only gallery.ts:1688/1694/1731. Spells.ts:20-21 doc: '...the play-mode per-frame casting dispatch, and the build-mode one-shot casts'.
reco: Update the class doc to state firePlayerSpell is now a gallery-preview-only path (the legacy fixed-spell dispatch) and that live play casting is owned by WandSystem. Consider extracting the shared per-type spawn parameters (life/velocity/gravity-bias) into one table consumed by both firePlayerSpell and castActionAt to kill the duplication.
verify: Verified. Spells.ts:18-22 class doc claims firePlayerSpell is 'the play-mode per-frame casting dispatch'. The live play path is Player.ts:1766 `if (player.firing) ctx.wands.fire(ctx);` (comment there even says wands replaced legacy ctx.spells dispatch). grep shows firePlayerSpell's only callers are gallery.ts:1688/1694/1731 (builder spell-preview) plus the types.ts:1205 interface decl. The 80-line, 16-branch method (Spells.ts:200-279) is dead in play mode and duplicates WandSystem.castActionAt logic. The doc actively misleads. Documentation-vs-reality drift; medium is acceptable since it would mislead a maintainer into editing the wrong combat path.

title: escapeHtml re-implemented byte-identically in 4 builder panels (canonical one already exported from Fields.ts)
desc: The same 3-replace `escapeHtml` (&,<,>) is copy-pasted verbatim into gallery.ts, prefabPanel.ts, spritePanel.ts, and virtualWorldPanel.ts, plus AssetPreview.ts as `esc`. Meanwhile src/ui/editor/Fields.ts already exports a more complete `escapeHtml` (escapes &,<,>,",') and `escapeAttr`. Beyond the duplication, the 3-replace copies are subtly weaker than the exported one: they do not escape quotes, so any of these used in an HTML attribute context would under-escape (no active attribute-context use was found today, so this is latent, not a live bug).
evidence: gallery.ts/prefabPanel.ts/spritePanel.ts: `function escapeHtml(s){ return s.replace(/&/g,'&').replace(/</g,'<').replace(/>/g,'>'); }` — identical. Fields.ts:189 exports a 5-char version.
reco: Delete the four+ local copies and import `escapeHtml` from ui/editor/Fields.ts (or hoist it to core/strings.ts, which already houses shared string helpers and is a foundation module importable anywhere). Standardizing on the 5-char version also closes the attribute-escaping gap for free.
verify: Verified gallery.ts:1963, prefabPanel.ts:292, spritePanel.ts:138, virtualWorldPanel.ts:1686 are the identical 3-replace escapeHtml; AssetPreview.ts:228 esc is the 4-replace (adds quote). Fields.ts:189 exports the full 5-char escapeHtml + escapeAttr. Duplication and the latent under-escaping note both hold. Medium is appropriate.

title: Dead module: src/builder/spritePanel.ts (SpritePanel class) is never imported anywhere
desc: The entire 140-line spritePanel.ts module — exporting `SpritePanelHooks` and `class SpritePanel` — is not imported by any file in src/, tests/, or scripts/. It is orphaned scaffolding (it also carries its own copy of the duplicated escapeHtml). A grep for `spritePanel` / `from '.../spritePanel'` across the repo returns no importer.
evidence: `grep -rln "from.*spritePanel|spritePanel'" src tests scripts` returns nothing; class SpritePanel occurs only at its declaration site.
reco: Delete src/builder/spritePanel.ts (and confirm the live sprite UI lives in spritePanel's intended replacement). If it is a planned panel, wire it in or move it out of src/ until used.
verify: spritePanel.ts exports class SpritePanel + SpritePanelHooks; grep shows no importer. Builder.ts has a method named wireSpritePanel/renderSpritePalette (its own inline implementation) and never imports the spritePanel module — the name collision is coincidental, not a use. Module is dead scaffolding.

title: Dead module: src/builder/assetDependencyGraph.ts (buildAssetDependencyGraph) never imported
desc: buildAssetDependencyGraph and its three types (AssetGraphNode/Edge/AssetDependencyGraph) are exported but referenced nowhere outside this file — the 54-line module is fully dead. Note a SEPARATE, live dependency-graph feature exists (builder/assets/AssetDatabase has dependency tracking; types/ContentDependencyKind), so this is a stranded earlier/parallel implementation, not the one in use.
evidence: `grep -rln assetDependencyGraph src tests scripts` (excluding the file) returns nothing; buildAssetDependencyGraph occurs once (its declaration).
reco: Delete the module. If asset dependency graphing is desired, consolidate on the AssetDatabase path rather than keeping a second unused graph builder.
verify: buildAssetDependencyGraph and all three types (AssetGraphNode/Edge/AssetDependencyGraph) are referenced only inside assetDependencyGraph.ts itself; no external importer across src/tests/scripts. Fully dead 54-line module.

title: Dead leftover: populationForDepth() superseded by populationForLevel() but still exported
desc: populationForDepth() (a flat depth-based enemy-count curve) is exported but called nowhere — its only occurrence in the whole repo is its own declaration. The live function is populationForLevel() directly below it (biome-weighted population), which does NOT call populationForDepth. This is a stale earlier design left behind after the population system moved to biome weighting; it is misleading because a reader assumes both are part of the spawn pipeline.
evidence: `grep -rn populationForDepth src tests scripts` => only worldgraph.ts:50. populationForLevel (lines 66-82) computes its own total from `24 + depth*6` and biome foes, independently.
reco: Delete populationForDepth(); it is dead and risks being 'maintained' as if it shaped spawns.
verify: populationForDepth (worldgraph.ts:50) has a single occurrence (its own declaration). The live function populationForLevel (66-82) computes its own total (24+depth*6) from biome foes and never calls populationForDepth. Stale superseded function; confirmed dead.

title: Dead save-migration path: migrateSandboxSave() is never called
desc: migrateSandboxSave(name, save) converts a v1 sandbox raw-grid save into a v2 EditorDocument, but nothing in src/, tests/, or scripts/ calls it. Per CLAUDE.md the three authoring/save families are deliberately separate, so a v1-sandbox -> Builder-document bridge is a real feature; this being dead means either an intended 'open my old sandbox save in the Builder' path was never wired up, or it is abandoned. Either way it is untested dead code on a data-migration path (silent-rot risk: nobody will notice if it breaks).
evidence: `grep -rn migrateSandboxSave` => only the declaration in document.ts; no caller and no test exercises it.
reco: Either wire it into the Builder import/open flow with a test, or delete it. Dead migration code on a save format is a liability because it looks load-bearing.
verify: migrateSandboxSave (document.ts:736) is exported but has only its declaration occurrence; no caller, no test. It is a real v1-sandbox->v2-document migration bridge left unwired. Untested dead data-migration path; medium fair.

title: Expedition save-shape guard checks typeof but not finiteness — NaN player state loads
desc: isExpeditionSaveShape() validates player.x/y/hp/maxHp/levit/maxLevit with `typeof ... === 'number'` but does NOT call Number.isFinite on them (it does for expeditionSeed and score). NaN and Infinity are typeof 'number', so a corrupt/hand-edited save passes the gate, and tryResumeExpedition then assigns p.maxHp/hp/maxLevit/levit and p.x/p.y directly from the save. A NaN player position propagates into physics, camera.snapTo, and every per-frame distance check; NaN hp breaks combat/UI. This is the exact corrupted-save case the guard exists to catch.
evidence: Lines 1428-1433 use bare `typeof player.x === 'number'` etc. Lines 1481-1483 `p.maxHp = save.player.maxHp; p.hp = save.player.hp; ...` and line 1499 `p.x = save.player.x; p.y = save.player.y;` copy raw, no clamp/finite filter.
reco: Add Number.isFinite() to the player numeric fields in isExpeditionSaveShape (mirroring expeditionSeed/score), or sanitize on assignment with the existing finiteNumber()/nonNegativeInt() helpers already defined in this file.
verify: Verified Levels.ts:1428-1433 gate player.x/y/hp/maxHp/levit/maxLevit with bare `typeof ... === 'number'`, while expeditionSeed (1420-1421) and score (1422-1423) additionally call Number.isFinite. Lines 1480-1483 and 1499-1500 copy save.player.* raw into p with no clamp/finite filter, then camera.snapTo(p.x,p.y). The surrounding try/catch (1503) does NOT rescue this because NaN/Infinity propagate silently rather than throwing. Reachability: NaN can't survive JSON (stringify->null, parse rejects literal NaN), but Infinity IS reachable through valid JSON — `JSON.parse('1e999')` yields Infinity, which is typeof 'number' and passes the gate. The guard's stated job is catching corrupt saves, so this is a real gap. Medium is appropriate.

lines: 35, 110-159; consumers e.g. src/builder/PreviewRuntime.ts:748, src/world/prefabs/place.ts:250, src/builder/prefablib.ts:467, src/builder/gallery.ts:842
title: chargeScanTiles 'covered' caching silently drops charges written via raw world.charge[i] after a tile is scanned
desc: World keeps a sparse activeCharges Set so the electrical sim doesn't full-scan the window each substep. To recover charges written outside setChargeAt (save loads, prefab placement), rebuildActiveChargesInBounds() scans tiles and records them in chargeScanTiles so each 64x64 tile is scanned at most once. The contract is 'all positive charge must go through setChargeAt OR be discovered by the one-time tile scan'. But once a tile is marked covered, any later RAW positive write (world.charge[i] = v) into that tile is NEVER rediscovered: it is not in activeCharges and chargeTrackingCovers() reports the tile as already done. electrical.ts only iterates activeCharges, so such a charge never spreads or decays. The codebase has dozens of raw world.charge[i]= writes (prefab place.ts:250, prefablib.ts:467, PreviewRuntime.writeCell:748, gallery.ts:842) and the safety depends entirely on those happening before the containing tile is ever scanned.
evidence: World.ts:125 `if (this.chargeScanTiles.has(key)) continue;` and World.ts:137 `this.chargeScanTiles.add(key);` permanently mark a tile covered; electrical.ts:17 `for (const ci of w.activeCharges)` is the only enumeration source. Raw positive writes like place.ts:250 `world.charge[i] = v;` bypass setChargeAt and add nothing to activeCharges.
reco: Either (a) make setChargeAt the only sanctioned charge mutator and convert the remaining raw positive writes (PreviewRuntime.writeCell, prefab/gallery/prefablib placement) to it, or (b) invalidate the relevant chargeScanTiles entries whenever the grid is repopulated/stamped (clear the covering tile key on a raw region write). Document the invariant in World.ts so future raw writes don't silently regress charge propagation.
verify: Verified the mechanism: World.ts:125 skips any tile already in chargeScanTiles and 137 permanently adds it; electrical.ts:17 enumerates ONLY w.activeCharges and only rebuilds when !chargeTrackingCovers (line 16). So a RAW positive write (world.charge[i]=v) into an already-scanned tile is never rediscovered. The cited consumers all exist and bypass setChargeAt: place.ts:250, prefablib.ts:467, PreviewRuntime.ts:748 (writeCell), gallery.ts:842. The contract description is accurate. However 'medium' overstates real impact: place.ts placePrefabs is called from CaveGenerator (worldgen), i.e. charge is written into a level BEFORE the sim/camera ever reaches it, and tiles are scanned on first encounter as the sim window moves — so in normal play the write reliably precedes the scan. Only the Builder/gallery/PreviewRuntime authoring paths (disposable runtimes) could realistically write into an already-scanned tile. Real latent edge case, but low severity in practice.

title: World.set() is unused dead code with footgun semantics (does not clear life/charge unlike replaceCellAt)
desc: The public method set(x,y,t,color) writes type+color and deletes the colorOverride but deliberately leaves life and charge untouched. There are no callers anywhere in src/, tests, or scripts (production cell placement goes through replaceCellAt/clearCellAt/swap or raw array writes). It is therefore dead code, and its divergent semantics from replaceCellAt (which also zeroes life and clears charge) make it a latent footgun: a future caller using set() to 'place a fresh cell' would inherit stale life/charge from whatever occupied the index, e.g. leaving a conductor charged or a fresh cell with a leftover fire countdown.
evidence: World.ts:65-70 sets types[i]/colors[i] and colorOverrides.delete(i) only; compare replaceCellAt (88-94) which also sets life[i]=0 and clearChargeAt(i). Grep for `world.set(`/`.world.set(` across src returns zero matches.
reco: Delete set(), or if it must stay, either make it clear life/charge to match replaceCellAt or rename/comment it explicitly as 'type+color only, preserves life/charge' so the divergence is intentional and visible.
verify: World.ts:64-70 set(x,y,t,color) writes types[i]/colors[i] and colorOverrides.delete(i) only, leaving life and charge untouched — versus replaceCellAt (88-94) which also zeroes life[i] and clearChargeAt(i). Grep for world.set(/.world.set(/w.set( across src, tests, and scripts returns zero matches, confirming it is unused. The divergent semantics (stale life/charge inherited from the prior occupant) make it a latent footgun. Genuinely dead code with a real semantic trap; low severity is correct.

title: Play-mode gold magnet does a full fixed-disc scan every substep with no early-out, scaled up ~2.6x by the goldmagnet perk
desc: runHarvesterField runs once per processFrame, i.e. up to 6x per render frame. In play mode it unconditionally iterates a precomputed disc of radius 30 (~2827 cells) or 48 with the Gold Sense perk (~7238 cells), doing inBounds + idx + types read for every cell regardless of whether any gold exists. With the perk that is ~7238 x 6 ~= 43k array probes/frame, every frame, for the entire level — even on levels with zero gold. It is faithful to the original (which scanned rad=30 each substep) but the perk roughly 2.6x'd the constant cost, and there is no 'any gold in window' guard to skip the scan. Also idx(px,py) is computed twice per gold hit (lines 51 and 53).
evidence: harvester.ts:48 `for (const { dx, dy } of diskOffsets(rad))` with rad = perks.goldmagnet ? 48 : 30 (line 45), inside the per-substep runHarvesterField (called at Simulation.ts:54). No precondition gating the loop on gold presence.
reco: Gate the scan behind a cheap 'does the active window contain any Gold' check (a per-window gold counter maintained on swap/clear, or sampling), or run the magnet at tick rate rather than per substep. At minimum hoist the duplicate idx() into one local.
verify: harvester.ts:45 rad = perks.goldmagnet ? 48 : 30; line 48 iterates diskOffsets(rad) unconditionally with no 'any gold in window' guard, inside runHarvesterField which runs per substep (up to 6x/frame). Double idx compute confirmed: w.idx(px,py) at line 51 (condition) and again at line 53. The ~2827 vs ~7238 cell counts and the ~2.6x perk scaling are accurate. Noted as faithful to original (scanned rad=30 each substep); a micro-opt, low severity is right.

title: In-place material transmutations bypass clearCellAt/replaceCellAt and leave stale life on the destination cell
desc: Many handler transmutations write types[i]/colors[i] (and sometimes life[i]) directly instead of using replaceCellAt/clearCellAt. Where the new material does not also set life, the previous occupant's life value persists. E.g. handleWater's toxic dilution (62-64) turns Toxic into Water by writing only types+colors, so any leftover life on that index carries into the new Water cell; handleAcid's self-consume (312-314) sets the source to Empty via raw writes, leaving stale life/charge on the now-Empty cell. This is faithful to the original port (the prototype did the same raw writes), and life is harmless on most of these materials, so impact is low — but it is an inconsistency with the World's own clearCellAt/replaceCellAt primitives that exist precisely to keep the transient planes coherent.
evidence: liquids.ts:62-64 writes only `w.types[ni]=Cell.Water; w.colors[ni]=waterColor();` (no life reset) versus World.replaceCellAt (World.ts:88-94) which zeroes life and clears charge. acid self-consume liquids.ts:313-314 `w.types[ci]=Cell.Empty; w.colors[ci]=EMPTY_COLOR;` leaves life[ci]/charge[ci] intact rather than calling clearCellAt.
reco: Where a handler fully replaces a cell's identity, prefer world.replaceCellAt/clearCellAt (or explicitly zero life) so the transient planes never carry over. If kept as raw writes for parity, add a one-line note that life/charge intentionally persist, since it reads as an oversight next to the safe primitives.
verify: Verified each cited transmutation does raw type/color writes bypassing replaceCellAt/clearCellAt: water->toxic dilution liquids.ts:62-64 writes only types[ni]+colors[ni] (no life reset); nitrogen evaporation 198-201 sets life explicitly so that one is fine; acid->steam 308-310 sets life=25 (fine) but the acid self-consume 312-314 sets types[ci]=Empty + colors[ci]=EMPTY_COLOR leaving life[ci]/charge[ci] intact instead of clearCellAt; lava->stone 347-351 writes types[ci]/colors[ci] for Stone with no life reset. The finding itself flags this as faithful-to-port and mostly harmless (life unused on these materials), with the acid self-consume being the one genuine inconsistency since Empty cells should have cleared transient planes. Low severity is correct.

title: In-place transmutation of conductor cells leaves stale electrical charge (and life) instead of using replaceCellAt
desc: When a charged conductor cell is transmuted in place, the handlers write the new material with raw `w.types[i] = ...; w.colors[i] = ...` and never clear the cell's `charge`/`life`. The World class provides `replaceCellAt(i, t, color)` specifically to clear this transient metadata (it zeroes life and calls clearChargeAt), and these sites bypass it. Acid (a conductor, see isConductor in CellType.ts) corroding into Empty at lines 312-314 leaves a now-Empty cell still in `world.activeCharges` with charge>0; the electrical grid (electrical.ts lines 32-39) spreads charge OUT of a source cell regardless of the source's own type, so the empty hole keeps re-energizing conductor neighbors for several frames until decay. Lava (also a conductor) hitting water becomes Stone at line 347 keeping its charge similarly. handleSand's charge-fusion to Glass (powders.ts:23-26) leaves charge on the non-conductor Glass.
evidence: handleAcid: `const ci = w.idx(x, y); w.types[ci] = Cell.Empty; w.colors[ci] = EMPTY_COLOR; return;` — no clearChargeAt, while World.replaceCellAt exists and does exactly that. Acid is in isConductor(); charge can be deposited on it by Lightning.ts/Projectiles.ts setChargeAt at grid positions.
reco: Replace the raw `w.types[i]=/w.colors[i]=` transmutation writes for conductor cells (acid->empty in handleAcid, lava->stone in handleLava, and the sand->glass charge path in handleSand) with `w.replaceCellAt(i, type, color)` so charge and life are cleared in lockstep. This keeps activeCharges consistent and matches the established invariant that transmutation clears transient metadata.
verify: Verified in liquids.ts: handleAcid corrosion exit (312-314) does raw `w.types[ci]=Cell.Empty; w.colors[ci]=EMPTY_COLOR; return;` with no clearChargeAt; handleLava water->Stone (346-352) likewise raw; powders.ts handleSand charge-fusion to Glass (23-26) raw. World.replaceCellAt (World.ts:88-94) exists and zeroes life + clearChargeAt for exactly this. Acid/Blood/Lava are conductors (CellType.ts isConductor 101-110) and charge IS deposited on them in real play (Projectiles.ts:212 chargeNearby gates on isConductor; Lightning.ts:48 setChargeAt at grid cells). electrical.ts (17-44) iterates activeCharges and spreads from any charged source regardless of source type (trySpread at 27-31 only checks the NEIGHBOR is a conductor), so a now-Empty/Stone/Glass cell with stale charge keeps re-energizing conductor neighbors until it decays (~1/update). Sibling files (newMaterials.ts handleSnow/Coal/Ash) correctly use replaceCellAt/clearCellAt, confirming the inconsistency. Real but transient/cosmetic (charge decays, no hard-lock) — low is right.

title: Vine climbRate/hangRate and liquid viscosity material params are dead dials exposed as live inspector sliders
desc: MATERIAL_PARAMS defines `Vines: { climbRate: 0.15, hangRate: 0.25 }` and `viscosity` on Slime and the three Elixirs, but no handler reads them: handleVines (vines.ts) hardcodes its growth/hang/climb branch thresholds as `roll < 0.42 / 0.74` and never references ctx.params.materials[Cell.Vines].climbRate/hangRate; handleViscousLiquid uses `flowRate`, never `viscosity`. Because the Inspector (ui/Inspector.ts) generically renders a slider for every numeric param key, these surface as editable dials that visibly do nothing when dragged — actively misleading for tuning. (Verified against alchemists-descent.html: the original prototype also defined-but-never-used these, so this is inherited vestigial config, not a port regression.)
evidence: params.ts line 42: `[Cell.Vines]: { name: 'Vines', flammability: 0.45, climbRate: 0.15, hangRate: 0.25 }` — grep shows climbRate/hangRate/viscosity referenced only in params.ts, core/types.ts, and a comment in RigidBodies.ts; never in sim/elements/*.
reco: Either wire these params into handleVines/handleViscousLiquid (replace the magic 0.42/0.74 splits with hangRate/climbRate-derived thresholds, and gate viscous flow on viscosity), or delete the dead fields from MATERIAL_PARAMS and the optional keys from MaterialParams so the inspector stops offering inert sliders. Document the choice given they are intentionally-mutable tuning data.
verify: Confirmed: params.ts:42 Vines has climbRate/hangRate, lines 55/58-60 Slime+Elixirs have viscosity. Grep across src shows these keys appear ONLY in params.ts (data), core/types.ts (optional interface fields), and an unrelated WATER_DRAG comment in RigidBodies.ts — never read by any sim/elements handler. handleVines (vines.ts:88-106) hardcodes roll<0.42 / roll<0.74 and never reads climbRate/hangRate; handleViscousLiquid (liquids.ts:117) uses flowRate, never viscosity. Inspector.ts:92-105 renders a slider for EVERY numeric profile key except 'name', so these become draggable dials that do nothing. Vestigial config, low/maintainability — accurate.

title: Redundant moved-stamp writes after swap() in handleEmber
desc: handleEmber calls `w.swap(...)` and then immediately sets `w.moved[w.idx(tx, ty)] = w.movedTick` on the destination cell. World.swap already stamps `moved[a]` and `moved[b]` with movedTick (World.ts:187-188), so these two extra writes are dead/redundant. Only the line-78 stamp (after an in-place steam conversion, not a swap) is meaningful. The redundant writes suggest the author was unsure of swap's contract and are a small maintenance hazard (they imply swap might not stamp moved).
evidence: Lines 104-106: `w.swap(x, y, tx, ty); w.moved[w.idx(tx, ty)] = w.movedTick; return;` — swap() at World.ts:170-200 already does `this.moved[a] = this.movedTick; this.moved[b] = this.movedTick;`.
reco: Drop the two post-swap `w.moved[...] = w.movedTick` writes in handleEmber (keep the line-78 one, which follows an in-place conversion). Relies on the documented invariant that swap is the single cell-movement primitive that stamps the moved epoch.
verify: Confirmed in thermal.ts handleEmber: lines 104-105 `w.swap(x,y,tx,ty); w.moved[w.idx(tx,ty)]=w.movedTick;` and 108-110 same for the straight-down case. World.swap (World.ts:187-188) already stamps moved[a] and moved[b] with movedTick, so the post-swap stamp on the destination is redundant. The line-78 stamp (after an in-place steam conversion, not a swap) is the only meaningful one, as the finding states. Trivial dead writes; low/maintainability is right.

title: Steam-to-water condensation on a solid ceiling does not clear cell life before returning
desc: In handleGas, when steam under a solid ceiling condenses it does `w.types[ci] = Cell.Water; w.colors[ci] = waterColor(); w.life[ci] = 0; return;` — it sets life to 0 explicitly here, but the other in-place gas conversions (life<=0 -> Water/Empty at lines 30-38) do NOT reset life and rely on the fact that life is already <=0. The inconsistency (one path zeroes life, the sibling path leaves the negative/zero life) is a small smell; Water created with a leftover non-zero life would be harmless today (water ignores life) but is fragile if water ever gains a life-driven behavior. Using replaceCellAt here would make all three exits uniform.
evidence: Line 25 sets `w.life[ci] = 0` for the ceiling-condensation path; lines 31-36 convert to Water/Empty without touching life. Asymmetric handling of the same Steam->Water transition.
reco: Route all three Steam->Water/Empty conversions in handleGas through World.replaceCellAt so life is consistently cleared, removing the special-case `w.life[ci] = 0` and the asymmetry.
verify: Confirmed in gas.ts: ceiling-condensation path (20-28) sets w.life[ci]=0 explicitly; the life<=0 sibling conversions (30-38) convert to Water/Empty without resetting life. The asymmetry is real but genuinely harmless today (life is already <=0 in that branch by the guard, and water handlers never read life — verified handleWater ignores life). Borderline noise; accurate as a low/maintainability smell.

title: Asymmetric / cardinal neighbor-offset constant tables are copy-pasted across four element files
desc: The same neighbor-offset constants are re-declared in multiple files: CARDINAL_OFFSETS ([1,0],[-1,0],[0,1],[0,-1]) appears in liquids.ts, thermal.ts, gas.ts; the asymmetric ignition list ([1,0],[-1,0],[0,1],[-1,-1]) appears as IGNITION_OFFSETS in powders.ts and liquids.ts AND as FIRE_REACTION_OFFSETS in thermal.ts (identical values); newMaterials.ts has its own CARDINAL_DIRS with a different element order. The asymmetric list in particular is load-bearing magic, so having three independent copies risks one being 'fixed' to symmetric without the others. This is duplication that should be a single shared, commented module so the asymmetry is documented in exactly one place.
evidence: thermal.ts:22-27 FIRE_REACTION_OFFSETS and powders.ts:6-11 / liquids.ts:25-30 IGNITION_OFFSETS are byte-identical [[1,0],[-1,0],[0,1],[-1,-1]]; CARDINAL_OFFSETS duplicated in liquids/thermal/gas.
reco: Hoist the offset tables into a single foundation module (e.g. sim/neighborOffsets.ts) with a comment explaining that the [-1,-1] diagonal asymmetry is a preserved port constant, and import from there. Do NOT merge the asymmetric ignition list with the symmetric cardinal list.
verify: Substance confirmed with one factual correction. The asymmetric ignition list [[1,0],[-1,0],[0,1],[-1,-1]] is byte-identical and triplicated: IGNITION_OFFSETS in powders.ts:6-11 and liquids.ts:25-30, plus FIRE_REACTION_OFFSETS in thermal.ts:22-27 — this is load-bearing magic (invariant 4), so the 'one fixed to symmetric, others not' hazard is real. CARDINAL_OFFSETS is duplicated in liquids.ts:19-24 and thermal.ts:16-21. newMaterials.ts:10 CARDINAL_DIRS uses a different element order. CORRECTION: the finding claims CARDINAL_OFFSETS is also in gas.ts — it is NOT (gas.ts uses inline neighbor checks, no such constant). Minor evidence error; core duplication/maintainability point holds. Low is right.

title: spawnSmoke source position is inconsistent between fire reactions (x,y vs tx,ty)
desc: Within handleFire's reaction loop, most fuel ignitions call `spawnSmoke(ctx, x, y)` using the FIRE cell's own coordinates, but the Toxic branch calls `spawnSmoke(ctx, tx, ty)` using the burning neighbor's coordinates (line 208). spawnSmoke emits smoke one cell above the given position, so smoke from a toxic ignition appears above the toxic cell while smoke from wood/vine/moss ignition appears above the fire cell. This is an inconsistency rather than a correctness bug, but it makes the smoke origin non-obvious and is the kind of copy-paste drift worth normalizing.
evidence: Lines 170/176/182/191/197 use `spawnSmoke(ctx, x, y)`; line 208 (Toxic) uses `spawnSmoke(ctx, tx, ty)`.
reco: Pick one convention (smoke at the igniting fuel's position, tx/ty, is arguably more correct) and apply it to all fuel branches, or document why toxic differs.
verify: Confirmed in thermal.ts handleFire reaction loop: Wood (170), Vines (176), Fungus (182), Glowshroom (191), Moss (197) all call spawnSmoke(ctx, x, y) with the FIRE cell's coords; the Toxic branch (208) uses spawnSmoke(ctx, tx, ty) with the burning neighbor's coords. spawnSmoke (258-268) emits one cell above the given position, so the toxic smoke origin differs. Pure cosmetic copy-paste drift; low/maintainability is accurate.

title: GPU compose silently truncates shockwaves to 8 and lenses to 4 while the CPU loop processes all of them
desc: The GPU compose path caps active distortions at MAX_WAVES=8 and MAX_LENSES=4 (wCount/lCount use Math.min). The CPU reference path in FrameComposer.composeTerrainCpu iterates ctx.shockwaves and lenses unbounded (wavesLen = ctx.shockwaves.length, lensLen = lenses.length). When more than 8 simultaneous shockwaves (e.g. a cluster of explosions) or 5+ black holes exist, the default (GPU) path and the CPU fallback produce visibly different refraction/ring-glow. Since gpuCompose is on by default but flips to CPU on any GPU failure, the same scene can render two different ways. The COMPOSE_PAD doc comment reasons about offset magnitude but never mentions the count truncation.
evidence: GPU: const wCount = Math.min(waves.length, MAX_WAVES); ... const lCount = Math.min(lenses.length, MAX_LENSES); CPU: const wavesLen = ctx.shockwaves.length; const lensLen = lenses.length; with a full for-loop over each.
reco: Either cap the CPU loop to the same MAX_WAVES/MAX_LENSES so both paths agree, or (better, since lens count is tiny and waves matter for parity) document this as an accepted deviation in docs/PORTING.md and assert in the parity probe that scenes stay under the caps. Truncation should at least prefer the strongest/nearest distortions rather than array order.
verify: Verified: ComposeShader.ts L47-48 MAX_WAVES=8/MAX_LENSES=4, L609/L617 use Math.min to cap; FrameComposer.composeTerrainCpu L276-277 uses unbounded ctx.shockwaves.length / lenses.length and the L288/L313 loops iterate all of them. WebGpuLiveCompose also caps at MAX_WAVES, so only the CPU path is unbounded — a real parity gap. explosion.ts L18 pushes one uncapped shockwave per blast, so >8 simultaneous waves is realistic in a cluster. Downgrading severity to low: the divergence is purely cosmetic (refraction/ring-glow), affects no game state, and the two paths are not used simultaneously in one session — a user only sees one path unless a mid-session GPU failure flips them. The 'two different ways' claim overstates real-world impact.

title: Per-frame heap allocations in the CPU compose path (activeBackdropLayers objects + lenses array)
desc: compose() builds a fresh lenses array of object literals every frame, and composeTerrainCpu() builds activeBackdropLayers as a new Array<{...}> pushing up to 5 object literals each frame. Both run in the per-frame render path. This is the CPU fallback path (not the default GPU path), so impact is bounded, but on machines without WebGL2 / after a GPU-compose failure this is steady GC pressure in the hottest loop's setup. The lenses array is additionally allocated even on the GPU path.
evidence: const lenses: CompositorLens[] = []; ... lenses.push({ cx, cy, R, K }); and const activeBackdropLayers: Array<{pixels;width;opacity;xSamples;ySamples}> = []; rebuilt every compose().
reco: Hoist both into reusable instance-field scratch buffers (a fixed-size pooled lens array with a count, and a preallocated activeBackdropLayers of length 5 reset in place), mirroring the pattern already used for backdropSampleX/Y.
verify: Verified: compose() L144 builds a fresh lenses[] of object literals every frame (pushed at L147) and it is allocated before the GPU/CPU branch and passed to beginGpuCompose, so it is allocated even on the default GPU path. composeTerrainCpu L241-247 builds activeBackdropLayers as a new Array and L267 pushes up to N object literals per frame. Both are per-frame setup allocations. Severity low is correct — bounded (a handful of objects), and the heavier activeBackdropLayers is CPU-fallback-only.

title: Water-shimmer neighbor check can read out of bounds / diverge from the shader under distortion
desc: The liquid surface-shimmer branch guards on the UNdistorted wy (wy > 0) but indexes the neighbor via the DISTORTED lookup: types[ci - WIDTH] where ci = lookupX + lookupY*WIDTH. When a shockwave or lens pulls lookupY to 0 while wy > 0, ci - WIDTH is negative and reads outside the typed array (returns undefined, compared === Cell.Empty → false). The GPU shader guards both wy > 0 AND ly > 0, so the two paths can disagree at the very top of a distorted water surface. Functionally harmless (OOB read yields undefined, not a crash) but it is an unintended read and a parity gap.
evidence: CPU: (type === Water||Healium||Teleportium) && wy > 0 && types[ci - WIDTH] === Cell.Empty — ci uses lookupY. GPU: ... && wy > 0 && ly > 0 && (texelFetch(uWin, ivec2(lx, ly-1)).a & 0x7Fu) == Empty.
reco: Guard the CPU neighbor read with lookupY > 0 (not wy > 0) to match the shader and avoid the negative index, or use the undistorted wy/wx for the surface check on both paths.
verify: Verified at FrameComposer L412: guard is (Water||Healium||Teleportium) && wy>0 && types[ci-WIDTH]===Cell.Empty where ci = lookupX+lookupY*WIDTH (distorted), but the wy>0 guard is undistorted. ComposeShader L272-274 guards both wy>0 AND ly>0 before texelFetch(lx,ly-1). When distortion clamps lookupY to 0 while wy>0, CPU reads ci-WIDTH = lookupX-WIDTH (negative) -> undefined, !==Empty. Real OOB read and parity gap, but harmless (no crash, yields false) and only at a distorted water surface touching the top edge. Low severity correct.

title: Sprite lighting vignette ignores the live postFx.vignette setting that the terrain loop honors
desc: Lighting.sample() (used by every sprite/rigid-body/ragdoll draw) applies the baked vignette table directly (vg = this.vignette[...] = 1 - 0.52*r^2), with no rescale by ctx.state.postFx.vignette. The terrain compose loop (and the GPU shader) explicitly rescale the baked table by postFx.vignette/0.52 so the setting is live-tunable.
