Result: turning the vignette slider down brightens terrain corners but leaves sprites darkened by the fixed 0.52 amount, so a sprite in a corner is lit inconsistently with the rock behind it.
evidence: FrameComposer: const vigScale = ctx.state.postFx.vignette / 0.52; ... vg = 1 - vigScale*(1 - vignette[i]). Lighting.sample: if (...) vg = this.vignette[fy*VIEW_W+fx]; (no scale).
reco: If sprites should track the live vignette, apply the same vigScale rescale in sample(); if the fixed vignette on sprites is intentional, add a one-line comment to that effect so it does not read as an oversight.
verify: Verified: Lighting.sample L125 sets vg = this.vignette[...] directly (baked 1 - 0.52*r^2 per L102), no rescale by postFx.vignette. FrameComposer L231 computes vigScale = postFx.vignette/0.52 and L370 applies vg = 1 - vigScale*(1-vignette[i]); GPU shader uses uVignette uniform. So the live vignette slider tunes terrain corners but not sprites/ragdoll/rigid-body draws that go through sample(). Real inconsistency. Low/maintainability is fair.

title: Redundant syncSettings() call in Renderer.render()
desc: render() calls this.backend.syncSettings(ctx.state.render) twice around the fallback checks. The first call (line 394) runs on the possibly-about-to-be-replaced backend; the fallback helpers may swap this.backend; the second call (line 397) then syncs whatever backend is now active. The first call is dead work in every case (if no fallback, line 397 still runs; if fallback, the first synced a backend that gets discarded).
evidence: this.backend.syncSettings(...); this.fallBackFromFailedWebGpu(...); this.fallBackFromLostWebGpu(...); this.backend.syncSettings(...); this.backend.render(ctx);
reco: Drop the first syncSettings call; keep only the one after the fallback swaps so the active backend is synced exactly once per frame.
verify: Verified: Renderer.render() calls backend.syncSettings at L394 and again at L397 (cited 393-398 is off by one — the method signature is at 393). syncSettings (L145-153) only assigns requestedBackend + featureFlags, no side effects. Fallback methods (L338/L349) branch on backend.initializationFailed/deviceLost — backend state, not fed by syncSettings — and replace this.backend with a new WebGLRenderBackend already constructed from settings (L341-346). So the first call is dead in both branches. Real but genuinely trivial (two field assignments, no allocation) and self-labeled ai-slop; borderline noise. Low is right.

title: Repeated O(n) bodies.find('player-corpse') scans every frame across four modules
desc: ctx.rigidBodies.bodies is linearly scanned for the player-corpse tag in multiple places each frame: drawRigidBodies skips it with a per-body tag compare, drawPlayerRagdoll does bodies.find, Camera.update does bodies.find on death, and Lighting.build does bodies.find on death. The body list is small so this is not hot, but the corpse identity is known when it is spawned and could be cached on a Ctx field, removing the scans and the scattered tag-string coupling.
evidence: const corpse = ctx.rigidBodies.bodies.find((b) => b.tag === 'player-corpse'); appears in FrameComposer (x2 incl. the skip), Camera.ts, and Lighting.ts.
reco: Expose the player-corpse body via a cached reference (e.g. ctx.rigidBodies.playerCorpse) set on spawn/cleared on transition, and read it in all four sites instead of re-scanning by tag string.
verify: Verified via grep: bodies.find(b => b.tag === 'player-corpse') in FrameComposer L594, Camera.ts L62, Lighting.ts L638, plus the per-body skip compare at FrameComposer L525-526. Tag string set at Player.ts L833. Claim of repeated scans across modules is accurate. Note the Camera and Lighting finds are guarded by death/play-mode conditions so they do not run every frame in the common case; only the L525 skip iterates always. Small list, so low/perf is correct — it is a cleanliness/caching suggestion, not a hot-path problem.

title: Per-pixel tuple allocation in cauldron bowl draw via spread of steel()
desc: The cauldron renderer calls this.setPx(x, y, ...steel(v)) inside its wall/base/rim loops; steel() returns a fresh 3-element array on every pixel, so each drawn cauldron pixel allocates and spreads a throwaway array. The cauldron is small and culled off-screen, so impact is minor, but it is an avoidable allocation in a draw loop and an inconsistent pattern versus the rest of the file which passes r,g,b scalars directly.
evidence: const steel = (v) => [v*0.86, v*0.93, v]; ... this.setPx(x, y, ...steel(v)); repeated per pixel across walls, base, rim, handles, feet.
reco: Inline the three channel computations as locals (sr = v*0.86, sg = v*0.93, sb = v) and call setPx(x, y, sr, sg, sb), avoiding the array allocation + spread per pixel.
verify: Verified: FrameComposer L943 steel = (v) => [v*0.86, v*0.93, v] returns a fresh tuple; spread into setPx at L954, L960, L963-964, L968-970, L974, allocating a throwaway array per drawn pixel across walls/base/rim/handles/feet. The brew loop at L986 passes r,g,b scalars directly, confirming the inconsistency. Cauldron is small and off-screen-culled, so low/perf is correct — avoidable but minor.

title: Dead arithmetic / unused `sw` in golem arm rendering (`* 0` term)
desc: Inside the golem arm loop, `const sw = dy <= 7 ? armSwing : Math.round(armSwing * 0.5);` is declared but its only use is `(dy <= 7 ? -sw : 0) * 0`, which is multiplied by zero and therefore contributes nothing to the pixel position. The effective left-arm x is `-6 - (dy <= 7 ? Math.round(armSwing * 0.6) : 0)`. The `sw` variable and the `* 0` sub-expression are leftover scaffolding that compute a value that is discarded.
evidence: `const sw = dy <= 7 ? armSwing : Math.round(armSwing * 0.5);` then `P(-6 + (dy <= 7 ? -sw : 0) * 0 - (dy <= 7 ? Math.round(armSwing * 0.6) : 0), dy + B, ...SD);` — `sw` appears nowhere else in the function.
reco: Delete the `sw` declaration and the `(dy <= 7 ? -sw : 0) * 0 ` term, leaving `P(-6 - (dy <= 7 ? Math.round(armSwing * 0.6) : 0), dy + B, ...SD)`. Visual output is unchanged. If a swing on this arm was actually intended, replace the dead term with the real offset and add a test/note.
verify: Verified EnemySprites.ts lines 406-408. Line 407 declares `const sw = dy<=7 ? armSwing : Math.round(armSwing*0.5);`. Its only use on line 408 is `(dy<=7 ? -sw : 0) * 0` -- multiplied by zero, so it contributes nothing to the x position; the effective term is `-6 - (dy<=7 ? Math.round(armSwing*0.6) : 0)`. `sw` appears nowhere else in the else-branch (fists at 414-415 recompute Math.round(armSwing*0.6) directly). Dead variable + dead `*0` sub-expression, leftover scaffolding. Low / ai-slop is accurate.

title: blackhole projectile draw relies on non-null assertion of optional vortexRad with NaN-swallowing fallback
desc: The blackhole branch computes `const drawRad = Math.max(2, Math.floor(p.vortexRad! / 6));` using a non-null assertion on the optional Projectile.vortexRad. If a blackhole projectile is ever created without vortexRad, the result is NaN; Math.max(2, NaN) is NaN and the subsequent `for (let dy = -NaN-1; ...)` loops never execute, so it fails silently (no draw) rather than crashing. This matches the established `pr.vortexRad!` usage in FrameComposer, so it is a consistency-with-existing-code issue rather than a new defect, but the `!` hides an invariant that is not locally enforced.
evidence: `const drawRad = Math.max(2, Math.floor(p.vortexRad! / 6));` — vortexRad is declared `vortexRad?: number` in core/types.ts.
reco: Either narrow with a guard (`if (p.vortexRad === undefined) continue;`) before the blackhole branch, or default it (`p.vortexRad ?? <radius>`), so the optionality is handled explicitly instead of via `!` plus accidental NaN-loop suppression.
verify: Verified FxSprites.ts line 155 `const drawRad = Math.max(2, Math.floor(p.vortexRad!/6));` and types.ts:358 declares `vortexRad?: number`. If a blackhole lacks vortexRad, NaN propagates and the for-loops (156-157) never execute -> silent no-draw, no crash. FrameComposer.ts:147 uses the same `pr.vortexRad!` pattern, so this is consistency-with-existing-code, not a new defect -- as the finding itself states. Technically correct type-safety nit but borderline trivial; low is appropriate.

title: WebGpuLiveCompose hardcodes LIGHT_W/LIGHT_H while the CPU compose derives them from the live LightField
desc: The light texture is sized from module-level constants LIGHT_W=(VIEW_W>>1)+1 / LIGHT_H=(VIEW_H>>1)+1, and uploadLight() iterates exactly LIGHT_W*LIGHT_H reading lightR[i]/lightG[i]/lightB[i] from the passed LightField. The CPU ComposeShader instead sizes its buffer from `light.LW * light.LH` (the field's own dimensions). Today Lighting sets LW/LH to the same formula so the values agree, but the WebGPU path silently assumes the LightField matches these constants; a future change to LW/LH (or a differently-sized field) would read past/short of the array in uploadLight with no assertion.
evidence: WebGpuLiveCompose: `const LIGHT_W = (VIEW_W >> 1) + 1;` and `for (let i = 0...; i < LIGHT_W * LIGHT_H; i++) { this.lightData[offset] = lightR[i]; ... }`. ComposeShader.ts: `this.lightData = new Float32Array(light.LW * light.LH * 4);`.
reco: Either assert `light.LW === LIGHT_W && light.LH === LIGHT_H` once in initialize/beginFrame, or drive uploadLight() off light.LW/light.LH like the CPU path so the two compose backends share the same source of truth for light-field dimensions.
verify: Verified WebGpuLiveCompose.ts: LIGHT_W/LIGHT_H are module constants (lines 32-33) = (VIEW_W>>1)+1 / (VIEW_H>>1)+1, and uploadLight (line 1017) iterates exactly LIGHT_W*LIGHT_H reading lightR/G/B[i] from the passed LightField. ComposeShader.ts instead sizes (line 481) and iterates (line 850) from light.LW*light.LH. Lighting.ts:88-89 sets LW/LH to the identical formula and sizes lightR/G/B arrays as LW*LH (lines 90-92), so today they agree, but the WebGPU path hardcodes the assumption with no assertion; a future LW/LH change or differently-sized field would over/under-read in uploadLight. Accurate low-severity maintainability/consistency observation.

title: generateLevel is a ~700-line god-function carrying the entire post-skeleton pipeline
desc: generateLevel inlines bedrock, exit well, gold tells, waystone placement+fallback, cauldron, the entire D1 onboarding seal/plug logic, prefab+machine passes, secrets, structures, the id-shift merge, campaign dressing, mineral vugs, the large gauge-rescue subsystem, and sump repair. It declares many nested closures (stampBrazier, trySeal, tryPlug, rescueAt, nearestWiz, metalOnLine, wizNear/wizNearCount/cellNear, recordRescue, rescueSpellLab, inSpellLab) that close over mutable let-bound masks. The single function is hard to test in isolation and hard to reason about for stream-order/determinism, which is exactly the property this file most needs to keep auditable.
evidence: The function body spans lines 474-1179 with the gauge-rescue alone (941-1138) being ~200 lines of nested closures over `let wiz`/`let cell`. The D1 onboarding block (738-861) is itself two self-contained sub-generators.
reco: Extract cohesive stages into named module functions (placeExitWell, placeWaystones, placeOnboarding, gaugeRescue) taking explicit (ctx, rng, graph, ...) like the existing connect.ts/secrets.ts split. The rng is already threaded explicitly, so this is a mechanical move that preserves stream order while making each stage unit-testable.
verify: Verified generateLevel spans 474-1179 (~705 lines). It inlines onboarding (D1 block 738-861 with self-contained trySeal/tryPlug sub-generators), campaign dressing, mineral vugs, and the ~200-line gauge-rescue (941-1138) with many nested closures (wizNear/wizNearCount/cellNear/metalOnLine/nearestWiz/rescueAt/recordRescue/rescueSpellLab/inSpellLab) closing over mutable `let wiz`/`let cell`. Description is accurate. This is a maintainability/code-smell observation with no functional defect; given the file's stated need to stay auditable/deterministic it is a legitimate note, but downgraded to low as pure style/structure with no correctness or perf impact.

title: Per-frontier array-of-arrays allocation in the rim-light distance BFS
desc: The distance-from-air BFS allocates a fresh 4-element array of 2-element neighbor arrays for every frontier cell on every BFS level. Across a full level the open-cell frontier is on the order of 10^5-10^6 cells over 13 levels, so this produces millions of tiny ephemeral arrays at generation time. It is correct, just needless GC pressure during the synchronous gen hitch.
evidence: `for (const [fx2, fy2] of frontier) { for (const [dx, dy] of [[1,0],[-1,0],[0,1],[0,-1]]) { ... } }` — the inner neighbor literal is reconstructed each iteration of the outer for-of.
reco: Hoist the 4 neighbor offsets to a module-level constant (or unroll the four directions inline) so the BFS does zero per-cell allocation. The frontier arrays themselves could also be flat Int32Array index queues.
verify: Verified CaveGenerator.ts 125-144: distance-from-air BFS uses `for (const [fx2,fy2] of frontier) for (const [dx,dy] of [[1,0],[-1,0],[0,1],[0,-1]])`. Both the frontier tuples and the inner 4-element neighbor literal are allocated/reconstructed per iteration, producing many short-lived arrays at gen time. Correct but needless GC pressure; low is right.

title: connectToCaves JSDoc says default radius 11 but the parameter default is 12
desc: The doc comment states "`radius` defaults to 11" and builds its 9.62-radius justification around that, but the actual signature uses `radius = 12`. Since this radius math is explicitly called out as load-bearing (fit-island fragmentation), the contradiction is a real trap for the next person tuning connectivity.
evidence: Comment line ~71: "`radius` defaults to 11: the player's collision box is 9x17..." vs signature line 88: `radius = 12,`.
reco: Update the comment to say 12 (or change the default to 11 if 11 was the intended/tested value). Verify against the golden/findability suite either way.
verify: Verified connect.ts: JSDoc line 71 states '`radius` defaults to 11' and builds the 9.62-radius / fit-island fragmentation justification on 11, but the signature at line 88 is `radius = 12`. Since the comment explicitly flags this radius as load-bearing, the doc/code mismatch is a real trap. Low.

title: Secret connector can dead-end in rock, leaving an undiscoverable sealed chamber
desc: The connector dig loop breaks either when open space is detected ahead (`reached`) or when it runs out of bounds. If it exits on the bounds condition without ever reaching open space, the chamber + connector are fully enclosed by rock, the last-5-steps reseal still writes breach skin, and the three 'tell' cells are placed in solid wall — so the secret has no adjacency to any open/reachable cell and can never be found or breached. Because the connector aims at an open region centroid this is rare, but stampSecrets still counts it toward placedCount, so a buried-and-lost secret reads as a successful placement.
evidence: Loop at lines 181-212 breaks on `if (ix < 4 || ix >= W - 4 || iy < 4 || iy >= H - 8) break;` (no reach) as well as `if (reached) break;`. The reseal + tell + payload then run unconditionally (lines 214-282) and `placedCount++` at line 282. Secrets are not covered by validateFindability (treated as buried treasure).
reco: Track whether the connector actually `reached` open space; if not, either skip the reseal/tell (leaving the bore open so the chamber connects) or roll back the chamber and retry, and only increment placedCount on a connector that terminated via `reached`.
verify: Verified secrets.ts: connector loop 181-212 breaks on bounds (`if (ix<4||ix>=W-4||iy<4||iy>=H-8) break;`) OR `reached`. If it exits on bounds without ever setting `reached`, the chamber+connector are sealed; reseal (217-231) only reskins the last 5 dug steps, and tell/payload/`placedCount++` (233-282) run unconditionally. Confirmed in validate.ts that validateFindability (300-415) loops mechanisms/runeVaults/pickups/waystones/refuge/spellLab/vaultArch/cauldron but has NO secrets loop, so a buried-and-lost secret is never audited yet still counts as placed. Rare (connector aims at an open centroid) — low is correct.

title: Non-deterministic generation colors mean restored levels never match the original session's terrain tint
desc: Every material color factory draws from Math.random() (e.g. sandColor/stoneColor/goldColor and the branchy crystalColor/rawOreColor/catalystColor). generateCaves/generateLevel write these into world.colors while cell TYPES come from the seeded Rng. The save system regenerates pristine worlds from seed and overlays only player-changed cells (Levels.restoreLevel), so unchanged terrain is recolored differently on every load. This is intentional per the save comment (types are the ABI, colors are cosmetic) and the golden test deliberately hashes only types — but it does mean 'replay the seed' is not pixel-deterministic, which is a subtle footgun for anyone who later tries to diff/snapshot rendered output or persist a color-sensitive feature off the seed alone.
evidence: `const rand = (n) => Math.floor(Math.random() * n)` (line 20) feeds nearly every factory; tests/gen-golden.test.ts line ~20 notes "Colors are randomized paint (Math.random) and deliberately not hashed"; Levels.ts line 147 comment: "colors regenerate from the seed + a diff recolor pass."
reco: No change required for current behavior, but document this boundary prominently at the top of colors.ts ("types are seeded/deterministic; colors are Math.random and intentionally NOT seed-stable") so the invariant is discoverable, and keep any future color-dependent persistence out of the seed-only path.
verify: Verified colors.ts: `rand = (n)=>Math.floor(Math.random()*n)` (line 20) feeds nearly every factory (sand/water/stone/gold/crystal/rawOre/catalyst etc.), so terrain TINT is non-deterministic while TYPES come from the seeded Rng. Confirmed gen-golden.test.ts:19 ('Colors are randomized paint (Math.random) and deliberately not hashed') and Levels.ts:147 ('colors regenerate from the seed + a diff recolor pass'). The observation is accurate, but the finding itself concedes this is INTENTIONAL by-design (types = ABI, colors = cosmetic). It is an informational footgun note for anyone later persisting a color-sensitive feature off the seed, not a defect. Confirmed at low/informational.

title: tunnelTo hard-clamps carve rows to y >= 26, so a rescue target above that row is silently pulled down
desc: tunnelTo clamps every carve step to `y = clamp(y, 26, HEIGHT - 12)` regardless of the caller's minY. Generation uses minY=2, and connectToCaves/gauge-rescue can legitimately target features in the upper gallery band (the baseline 'upper gallery artery' clamps as low as row 32, and crevasse/vault skeletons carve from y≈14-24). A connector or rescue whose target sits between the world top and row 26 will have its tunnel floor clamped to 26 and may stop 3 cells short of the target without ever joining it, since the while-loop exits when |y-ty|<=3 only if ty>=23.
evidence: Line 185: `y = Math.floor(clamp(y, 26, HEIGHT - 12));` is a hardcoded 26, not derived from a minY parameter; the loop termination is `Math.abs(y - ty) > 3` (line 180). carveSpawnChamber and several skeletons place open space well above row 26.
reco: Thread the caller's minY (or a small constant like minY+12) into tunnelTo's y clamp instead of the literal 26, or assert/clamp targets into the reachable band so a high target can't produce a tunnel that never closes the gap.
verify: Verified connect.ts tunnelTo: line 185 `y = Math.floor(clamp(y, 26, HEIGHT-12));` is a hardcoded 26, not derived from any minY param; loop termination is `Math.abs(x-tx)>3 || Math.abs(y-ty)>3` (180). Confirmed MIN_Y=2 (CaveGenerator.ts:87) and crevasse tunnels carve open space from `y: minY+14` (~16) and noise/shelves above row 26, so high targets exist. A target with ty<23 can never satisfy |y-ty|<=3 once y is clamped to 26. Real latent bug. Practical impact is narrow: the gauge-rescue caller uses a swept rect with up:25 (huge vertical clearance softens the shortfall) and aims at wizard-fit cells whose feet-row inherently sits low; other tunnelTo/connectToCaves callers target portal/spawn/fit cells well below row 26. Low is appropriate.

title: extractRegionGraph swallows all errors and returns an empty graph with no signal
desc: extractRegionGraph wraps extract() in a bare try/catch that on ANY throw returns a degenerate empty graph (no regions, no edges, spawnRegion/exitRegion = -1). This is the documented fail-open behavior, but it swallows programmer errors (e.g. an OOB introduced by a future edit) completely silently — placement then quietly degrades (no main path, secrets fall back to 'any region', connectToCaves finds no main-path region and returns no tunnel) with zero diagnostic even in DEV.
evidence: `try { return extract(...); } catch { return { ...empty graph... }; }` — the catch binding is omitted and nothing is logged.
reco: Keep the fail-open return, but log the caught error under import.meta.env.DEV (console.error) so a real bug in extract() is not masked as 'this level just has no regions'.
verify: Verified regions.ts 59-75: `try { return extract(...); } catch { return {... empty graph, spawnRegion:-1, exitRegion:-1 ...}; }` with omitted catch binding and no logging. This is the documented fail-open behavior (doc 46-51, and aligns with the project's hard 'fail-open / never hard-lock progression' rule), so the swallow itself is by-design — but the finding's narrower point is valid: there is no DEV-only diagnostic, so a future programmer error (OOB) silently degrades placement with zero signal. Reasonable low observability nit; not a hard bug.

title: Brazier vault puzzle is nearly unreachable due to biased mechRoll formula
desc: The mechanism-gated treasure vault picks its trigger with `mechRoll = (vaultIdx + (rng.next() < 0.5 ? 0 : 1)) % 3`. vaultIdx is only 0 or 1, and the bit is 0 or 1, so vault #0 can only yield mechRoll 0 or 1 (plate/lever) and vault #1 only 1 or 2 (lever/brazier). The brazier path (mechRoll===2) therefore appears ONLY on the second vault, which itself spawns only ~50% of the time (`1 + (rng.next()<0.5?1:0)` vaults), so the brazier puzzle shows up on roughly 1 in 4 levels at best, while the plate puzzle never appears on the second vault. The three archetypes are clearly meant to rotate evenly.
evidence: `for (let vaultIdx = 0; vaultIdx < 1 + (rng.next() < 0.5 ? 1 : 0); vaultIdx++)` then `const mechRoll = (vaultIdx + (rng.next() < 0.5 ? 0 : 1)) % 3;` — sum range is {0,1,2} but never uniformly covers all three for a given vaultIdx.
reco: Roll the archetype with a uniform `rng.int(3)` (or `Math.floor(rng.next()*3)`) per vault if even rotation is intended; if the bias is deliberate, add a comment saying so. Note this also changes the rng stream, so it must be done as a deliberate gen change (re-record golden hashes / bump GEN_VERSION).
verify: Read structures.ts:604-657. Loop bound is `1 + (rng<0.5?1:0)` → vaultIdx ∈ {0,1}. mechRoll = (vaultIdx + {0,1}) % 3. So vaultIdx=0 yields {0,1} (plate/lever), vaultIdx=1 yields {1,2} (lever/brazier). Brazier (===2) only on the second vault, which itself spawns ~50% of the time. Math is exactly as described; the three archetypes do not rotate evenly. Accurate low-severity distribution/maintainability observation.

title: applyBiomeExtras set() omits the life/charge reset its sibling pass performs
desc: The `set` helper in applyBiomeExtras writes only `types` and `colors`, leaving `life` and `charge` untouched, whereas the parallel applyCampaignDressing.setWall (lines 310-320) explicitly resets `life=0; charge=0` and the fungus/moss passes in this same function bother to set `w.life=0` afterward. Today this is benign because applyBiomeExtras only overwrites cells it has confirmed are Empty or Wall (which generation leaves at life=0/charge=0), but it is a latent footgun: any future change that lets these passes overwrite a cell carrying stale life (e.g. a decaying fire) or charge would silently inherit it onto crystal/gold/coal/snow/ash, producing materials that behave oddly in the sim.
evidence: `const set = (x, y, t, color) => { const i = w.idx(x, y); w.types[i] = t; w.colors[i] = color; };` — no life/charge reset, unlike setWall which does `world.life[i] = 0; world.charge[i] = 0;`.
reco: Reset `w.life[i]=0; w.charge[i]=0` inside set() for consistency with setWall and the fungus/moss callers, removing the redundant per-caller life resets.
verify: Read biomeExtras.ts:715-719: `set` writes only types/colors. Compared to applyCampaignDressing.setWall (310-320) which sets life[i]=0 and charge[i]=0. The difference is real. Correctly flagged as benign-today/latent-footgun (the passes only overwrite Empty/Wall cells which gen leaves at life=0/charge=0). Low severity is right.

title: Doc comment overstates RawOre cache rate in fillMineralVugs (~19% vs actual 16%)
desc: The function-level docstring says vug fill is '~19% a hidden RawOre cache', but the inline comment at line 1052 and the actual roll thresholds give RawOre for roll in [0.8, 0.96) = 16% and Crystal for [0.96, 1.0) = 4%. The two comments contradict each other and the code; the 19% figure is stale.
evidence: Docstring: 'mostly solid stone/coal ... ~19% a hidden RawOre cache ... a rare crystal geode.' Code: `roll < 0.58 ? Cell.Stone : roll < 0.8 ? Cell.Coal : roll < 0.96 ? Cell.RawOre : Cell.Crystal` → RawOre band is 0.80..0.96 = 16%.
reco: Fix the docstring to '~16%' to match the code and the line-1052 comment.
verify: Read biomeExtras.ts:1002-1054. Docstring line 1005 says '~19% a hidden RawOre cache'; inline comment line 1052 says '~16%'; code line 1054 `roll < 0.8 ? Coal : roll < 0.96 ? RawOre : Crystal` gives RawOre band 0.80..0.96 = 16% and Crystal 4%. The 19% docstring figure is stale and contradicts both the inline comment and the code. Confirmed.

title: Stale-named local 'w' in stampVeins shadows the conventional world alias
desc: Inside stampVeins, `const w = rng.next() < 0.22 ? 1 : 0;` names a vein half-width flag `w`, while every other function in this file uses `w` as the World alias (see applyBiomeExtras line 710 `const w = ctx.world`). It does not actually collide here (applyCampaignDressing's world alias is `world`), but it is a readability trap: a reader scanning the vein loop sees `world.inBounds(x, yy)` and `w` two lines apart and must realize `w` is not the world. A single-letter name reused for an unrelated concept in a file that elsewhere binds it to World invites a real bug on the next edit.
evidence: `const w = rng.next() < 0.22 ? 1 : 0; for (let dy = -w; dy <= w; dy++) { const yy = y + dy; if (!world.inBounds(x, yy) ...` — `w` (width) and `world` coexist.
reco: Rename the local to `halfW` or `vw`.
verify: Read biomeExtras.ts:364-391. Line 381 `const w = rng.next() < 0.22 ? 1 : 0;` (vein half-width) coexists with `world.inBounds`/`world.types` two lines later (384-385). applyCampaignDressing uses `world` as the World alias, so there is no actual collision here (the finding admits this). applyBiomeExtras separately binds `w = ctx.world` (line 710). Pure readability nit, accurately described and self-qualified as non-colliding. Low/noise-ish but factually correct.

title: physicsArena module-level savedAmbient is global mutable state shared across runtimes
desc: savedAmbient is a module-level `let` capturing the player's ambient on first entry, restored via a self-unsubscribing levelChanged handler. The current call order (Levels.ts emits levelChanged at 1695 then calls buildPhysicsArena at 1698) makes the one-shot pattern self-correcting in normal play, but module-global mutable state tied to a one-shot event handler is fragile: any future code path that stamps the arena without a subsequent levelChanged (or that constructs a second Ctx/runtime, e.g. a test harness or the Builder preview) would either leak the handler or restore the wrong runtime's ambient. The value belongs on the runtime/Ctx, not in module scope.
evidence: `let savedAmbient: number | null = null;` at module top; `if (savedAmbient === null) savedAmbient = ctx.params.global.ambient;` and the restore closure `() => { ctx.params.global.ambient = savedAmbient; savedAmbient = null; restore(); }`.
reco: Store the saved ambient on the LevelRuntime (or stash it in runtime state) and restore from there, so the lifetime is scoped to the runtime rather than the module.
verify: Read physicsArena.ts:1-63. `let savedAmbient: number | null = null;` at module scope (line 27); captured at 45 `if (savedAmbient===null) savedAmbient = ctx.params.global.ambient;`; restored via self-unsubscribing `ctx.events.on('levelChanged', () => {...; savedAmbient=null; restore();})` (59-63). Module-global mutable state tied to a one-shot handler is correctly characterized as fragile under a second Ctx/runtime or an arena stamp without a following levelChanged. Low severity is appropriate (works in normal play; this is a latent-fragility/architecture observation, not an active bug).

title: Spell-lab and other blocks read def.depth==1 / branch with no explicit mutual-exclusion guard
desc: The D1 Spell Lab is gated on `def.depth === 1` and the Gilded-Vault branch hoard on `def.branch`; these two large stamping blocks would overlap badly (both anchor off spawn/refuge and stamp dais/lab geometry) if a level were ever both depth 1 and a branch. Today worldgraph.ts guarantees the only branch is depth 4 and the only depth-1 level is non-branch, so it is safe — but the safety is implicit in a separate config file with no assertion or comment in structures.ts. A future content edit adding a depth-1 branch would produce overlapping geometry with no warning.
evidence: `if (def.depth === 1) { ... spellLab ... }` (line 382) and `if (def.branch) { ... vaultArch/vaultHoard ... }` (line 1411) are independent ifs with no `&& !def.branch` / `&& def.depth !== 1` cross-guard; mutual exclusion lives only in config/worldgraph.ts.
reco: Either add `&& !def.branch` to the depth===1 guard (cheap insurance) or a brief comment documenting that worldgraph guarantees no depth-1 branch exists.
verify: Read structures.ts:382 `if (def.depth === 1)` (Spell Lab) and 1411 `if (def.branch)` (vaultArch/hoard) — two independent ifs, no `&& !def.branch` cross-guard, both anchor off spawn/refuge. Verified config/worldgraph.ts: d1 has depth:1 and no branch; the only `branch: true` level has depth:4. So mutual exclusion is real but lives entirely in config with no assertion/comment in structures.ts. Accurate low-severity implicit-safety/maintainability note; a future depth-1 branch would overlap geometry silently.

title: ChunkCache is dead code with no def/seed versioning — a cache-invalidation trap if ever wired in
desc: ChunkCache (an LRU keyed only by `${cx},${cy}`) is never instantiated anywhere in src/, tests/, or scripts/ — it is referenced only in planning docs. The live consumers (Levels.createVirtualRuntimeFromChunks, builder/virtualWorldPanel) keep their own maps and never use it. Beyond being dead, the cache key omits any def identity (seed, generation params, def id). If it were adopted, calling backend.updateDef() with a new seed/params would leave previously-cached chunks indistinguishable from fresh ones, serving stale terrain for the same (cx,cy). The eviction scan is also O(n) per insertion (full Map scan to find the min touch tick).
evidence: `private readonly chunks = new Map<string, VirtualChunk>()` keyed via `chunkKey(cx,cy) => "${cx},${cy}"`; no seed/def in key. `evict()` does `for (const [key, touched] of this.touches)` to find oldest on every set. Grep shows zero `new ChunkCache(` in the repo.
reco: Either delete ChunkCache and its export (it is unused), or before adopting it, fold the def's seed/version into the cache key and clear() on updateDef. If kept as LRU, replace the O(n) eviction scan with insertion-order Map semantics (delete+set on touch, evict via the first key from .keys()).
verify: Verified ChunkCache.ts: LRU keyed only by `${cx},${cy}` (chunkKey, line 62-64), no seed/def in key; evict() is O(n) full-Map scan per insertion (lines 45-58). Grep across repo confirms zero `new ChunkCache(` — only the barrel re-export (index.ts:8) and two planning-doc mentions. Live consumers keep their own maps: virtualWorldPanel uses `new Map<string, CachedPreviewChunk>` (line 305) and Levels uses its own maps; neither imports ChunkCache. The seed-in-key concern is genuinely latent since updateDef() exists on the backend. All factual claims hold, but this is pure dead code with hypothetical risk — medium is overstated; dropping to low.

title: Cluster of dead exported helpers in coords.ts and hash.ts
desc: Several exported helpers are unused across the entire repo (src, tests, scripts): coords.ts `worldToChunk`, `localInChunk`, `chunkRect`, `chunksForRect`; hash.ts `fnv1aBytes`, `hashCoord3`, `hash3i`, `signedUnitHash`. (The PixelSceneStamper `chunkRect` matches are a local variable, not the exported function.) These look like a speculative API surface built ahead of need and re-exported via index.ts, adding maintenance/tree-shaking noise and implying coordinate conversions that the generator actually does inline.
evidence: Grep for each symbol across the repo returns only its own definition and the barrel re-export; e.g. `worldToChunk`/`localInChunk`/`chunksForRect`/`hash3i`/`signedUnitHash`/`fnv1aBytes` have no call sites. The generator instead inlines `Math.floor(x/tileSize)` and uses `unitHash2i`/`fnv1aByteArrays`.
reco: Delete the unused exports (or move any genuinely-shared coordinate math into a single used path). If retained intentionally as a public API, add a comment marking them as the intended chunk-addressing API and cover them with a test so they are not silently broken.
verify: Verified via grep for `\b(symbol)\s*\(` invocation pattern: worldToChunk, localInChunk, chunksForRect, hashCoord3, hash3i, signedUnitHash, fnv1aBytes each appear ONLY at their own definition line — zero call sites. Confirmed exported chunkRect (coords.ts:44) is also dead: the chunkRect references in PixelSceneStamper.ts:42-55 are a local object literal, not the imported function (PixelSceneStamper imports only WorldRect and rectsOverlap from coords). The generator inlines Math.floor and uses unitHash2i/signedUnitHash2i/fnv1aByteArrays instead. All re-exported via index.ts barrel. Accurate dead-code/ai-slop finding; low is right.

title: 36-bit hash constant 0x5c13faced is silently truncated to 32 bits by XOR
desc: `organicNoise(def.seed ^ 0x5c13faced, wx, wy)` in dressSurfaceTerrain uses a 9-hex-digit (36-bit) literal. JS `^` coerces operands to Int32, so the high nibble is dropped and the effective operand is 0xc13faced. The result is still deterministic and seam-stable, so it does not break the gen-golden contract, but it is clearly a typo (every other seed-mix constant in this file is exactly 8 hex digits) and reduces the intended independence of this surface-cover noise field from the others.
evidence: Confirmed numerically: `(0 ^ 0x5c13faced) >>> 0 === (0 ^ 0xc13faced) >>> 0` (both 0xc13faced). Surrounding constants (e.g. 0x4f1bbcdc, 0x7f4a7c15, 0x2f7c6d31) are all 32-bit.
reco: Replace with an intended 32-bit constant (e.g. 0x5c13face or another unused 8-digit value). Because this changes generated output, treat it as a deliberate generation change: re-record gen-golden hashes and bump GEN_VERSION per the hard-invariant workflow, or leave as-is and add a comment noting the truncation is benign.
verify: Verified ChunkGenerator.ts:724 `organicNoise(def.seed ^ 0x5c13faced, wx, wy)`. Numerically confirmed via node: `(0 ^ 0x5c13faced) >>> 0` === `(0 ^ 0xc13faced) >>> 0` === 0xc13faced — the high nibble (5) is dropped by Int32 coercion. Grep confirms 0x5c13faced is the ONLY 9+-hex-digit literal in the file; every other seed-mix constant is 8 digits. Harmless (still deterministic, gen-golden unaffected) but a clear typo reducing intended field independence. Low is correct.

title: toTransferableChunk assumes plane TypedArrays own their whole buffer (offset 0, exact length)
desc: toTransferableChunk transfers `chunk.types.buffer` etc. and fromTransferableChunk reconstructs with `new Uint8Array(input.types)` (whole buffer, offset 0). This is correct only because the generator allocates each plane as a standalone `new Uint8Array(size*size)`. If a VirtualChunk were ever built from a subarray/view (e.g. a sliced cache region, or a chunk read back from a pooled buffer), `.buffer` would transfer the wrong/oversized backing store and the reconstruction would ignore byteOffset, silently corrupting cells. It also detaches the source buffers, so a chunk transferred twice (e.g. from a cache) yields zero-length planes on the second send.
evidence: `const buffer = chunk.types.buffer as ArrayBuffer; out.types = buffer; transfer.push(buffer);` and `types: input.types ? new Uint8Array(input.types) : new Uint8Array(n)` — neither uses byteOffset/byteLength.
reco: Either transfer `chunk.types.buffer.slice(byteOffset, byteOffset+byteLength)` / pass byteOffset+byteLength through the wire type, or document and assert (in dev) that planes are full-buffer-owning. This becomes load-bearing the moment ChunkCache or any buffer pooling is introduced.
verify: Verified transfer.ts:55-85 transfers `chunk.types.buffer` (whole buffer) and fromTransferableChunk:103-117 reconstructs with `new Uint8Array(input.types)` ignoring byteOffset/byteLength. Confirmed the generator allocates each plane as a standalone full-length buffer (ChunkGenerator.ts:97-98,109-110 `new Uint8Array(size*size)` etc.), so the code is correct TODAY only because of that allocation pattern — exactly as the finding states. The double-transfer detachment (a chunk sent twice yields zero-length planes) is real since postMessage transfer detaches the buffer. Latent fragility, not a live bug; low is appropriate.

title: materializeChunks computes window bounds via four Math.min/max spread scans (large-window stack hazard + GC)
desc: Window bounds are computed with `Math.min(...chunks.map(c=>c.cx))` four times, each allocating an intermediate array and spreading it as function arguments. For the current small windows this is negligible, but `Math.min(...bigArray)` risks 'Maximum call stack size exceeded' for very large windows and allocates four throwaway arrays per materialize. Since materialization runs on level entry (a latency-sensitive transition), a single linear pass is preferable.
evidence: `const cx0 = Math.min(...chunks.map((chunk) => chunk.cx));` repeated for cy0/cx1/cy1 — four map() allocations plus four argument-spreads.
reco: Compute min/max cx/cy in a single `for` loop over chunks (no spread, no intermediate arrays). Cheap and removes the large-window stack hazard.
verify: Verified WindowMaterializer.ts:88-91: cx0/cy0/cx1/cy1 each computed via `Math.min(...chunks.map(c=>c.cx))` / Math.max — four map() allocations plus four argument-spreads. The `Math.min(...bigArray)` stack-overflow risk is real in principle but windows are small in practice, and a single linear pass is trivially preferable. Accurate micro-perf/cleanliness note; low severity is correct (genuinely negligible at current window sizes).

title: Untagged worker 'error' rejects ALL in-flight jobs and never re-creates the worker
desc: When the worker posts `{kind:'error'}` without a jobId, the backend calls rejectAll(), failing every concurrent init/chunk/window promise; the global worker 'error' listener also rejectAll() on any uncaught error. A transient failure in one chunk that escapes untagged thus tears down unrelated jobs. After rejectAll the worker is not nulled, and ensureWorker() early-returns `if (this.worker) return;`, so a wedged worker is reused for subsequent calls with no recovery path.
evidence: `if (message.jobId !== undefined) this.rejectJob(...) else this.rejectAll(error)`; `this.worker.addEventListener('error', (event) => this.rejectAll(...))`; `ensureWorker(){ if (this.worker) return; ... }`.
reco: Scope errors to a jobId wherever the worker can attribute them (its catch already computes jobId); reserve rejectAll for genuinely fatal conditions and, on fatal/onerror, set this.worker=null so ensureWorker() can recreate it.
verify: Verified TsWorkerBackend: handleMessage error branch (lines 128-132) calls rejectAll() when jobId is undefined; the global worker 'error' listener (lines 87-89) also rejectAll(); ensureWorker (lines 80-81) early-returns `if (this.worker) return` and never nulls the worker on error, so a wedged worker is reused with no recovery. One inaccuracy in the finding: chunk errors are NOT untagged — the worker catch (worldgen.worker.ts:81) tags jobId via `'req' in msg ? msg.req.jobId`, so generateChunk/generateWindow errors carry a jobId; only init/updateDef errors (no req/jobId) and the global 'error' event are untagged → rejectAll. The 'transient failure in one chunk that escapes untagged' framing is therefore slightly wrong, but the substantive claims (untagged error tears down all jobs; no worker recovery) hold. Low is appropriate.

title: Author-mode light preview rebuilds the entire light array + per-light objects every frame
desc: In author session mode the loop sets state.editorLights by running doc.lights.filter(...).map((l,n)=>({...toAuthoredLight(l,n), flicker:0})) unconditionally every frame whenever any light exists. This allocates a fresh filtered array plus a brand-new AuthoredLight object per light each frame, even when nothing about the lights changed — steady GC pressure in the editor render loop proportional to light count.
evidence: `state.editorLights = this.doc.lights.length > 0 ? this.doc.lights.filter((l) => !l.hidden && !this.mutedLightIds.has(l.id) && (this.soloLightId === null || l.id === this.soloLightId)).map((l, n) => ({ ...toAuthoredLight(l, n), flicker: 0 })) : null;`
reco: Cache the mapped array and only rebuild it when a documentRevision / mute-set / solo change is detected (a dirty flag already exists for several caches). The light field consumer can read the cached array each frame.
verify: Verified lines 9246-9257. In the non-live/author branch the rebuild .filter(...).map((l,n)=>({...toAuthoredLight(l,n),flicker:0})) runs every frame whenever doc.lights.length>0, with no dirty check — fresh filtered array + new AuthoredLight object per light each frame. Allocation claim is accurate. However it is gated by this.lightPreviewOn at line 9238 (else editorLights=null), so only fires when light preview is enabled; the per-frame GC churn is proportional to light count but small in absolute terms. Downgrading from medium to low: real but minor editor-only allocation, not a runtime/gameplay path.

title: Global window 'mousemove' handler runs (allocates + writes cursor) even while the Builder is closed
desc: wirePointer() attaches an inline window 'mousemove' listener that is never removed and has no `if (!this.isOpen) return` guard (unlike onKeyDown at 9027). For every mouse move anywhere on the page — for the life of the app, including after the Builder is closed — it allocates a new position object (mouseToWorld), allocates this.lastMouseClient = {x,y}, calls syncWandLightPreview(), hitGizmoAt() (getBoundingClientRect), and writes this.overlay.style.cursor. Most heavy work short-circuits when the overlay rect is 0, but the allocations and the cursor style write still happen on a permanent global listener.
evidence: `window.addEventListener('mousemove', (e) => { const pos = this.mouseToWorld(e); this.lastMouseClient = { x: e.clientX, y: e.clientY }; this.lastMouse = pos; this.syncWandLightPreview(); const hoverGizmo = this.gizmoDrag ? null : this.hitGizmoAt(e); ... this.overlay.style.cursor = this.canvasCursor(hoverGizmo); ... });` — no isOpen guard, listener never removed.
reco: Add `if (!this.isOpen) return;` as the first line (matching onKeyDown), and/or store the handler and removeEventListener it in close(). The mouseup handler at 4982 similarly lacks an isOpen guard.
verify: Verified lines 4924-4931. The window 'mousemove' listener has no isOpen guard (contrast loop at 9201 and onKeyDown at 9027 which both early-return on !isOpen). No removeEventListener('mousemove') exists anywhere in the file, so it is permanent. syncWandLightPreview (8606-8608) and hitGizmoAt (4517-4518) both short-circuit on disabled/zero-rect, so the residual cost is just the two small object allocations and a cursor-style write per global mouse move. Real but very low impact; downgrading medium->low.

title: Unreachable gizmoDrag branch inside the Escape key handler
desc: onKeyDown returns early at the top (lines 9028-9033) whenever this.gizmoDrag is truthy, handling Escape there via cancelGizmoDrag(). The later Escape branch's first clause `if (this.gizmoDrag) { this.cancelGizmoDrag(); }` (9090-9091) can therefore never execute — this.gizmoDrag is guaranteed null by the time control reaches line 9088. Dead/misleading code that implies a code path that does not exist.
evidence: Top of onKeyDown: `if (this.gizmoDrag) { e.preventDefault(); e.stopImmediatePropagation(); if (e.code === 'Escape') this.cancelGizmoDrag(); else this.status(...); return; }`. Later, inside `else if (e.code === 'Escape')`: `if (this.gizmoDrag) { this.cancelGizmoDrag(); } else if (this.settling || ...)`.
reco: Remove the redundant `if (this.gizmoDrag)` clause from the Escape ladder (start it at `if (this.settling || this.settleSnap)`), since the top-of-function guard already owns gizmo-drag Escape.
verify: Verified. onKeyDown top guard at 9028-9033 returns early whenever this.gizmoDrag is truthy (handling Escape via cancelGizmoDrag). Therefore at line 9090 inside the later 'else if (e.code===Escape)' block, this.gizmoDrag is guaranteed falsy and the 'if (this.gizmoDrag){this.cancelGizmoDrag()}' clause is unreachable dead code. Correctly identified; low/ai-slop is right.

title: RMB eyedrop force-switches the active tool to Paint and opens the material panel
desc: eyedrop() (bound to RMB, advertised as 'RMB anywhere eyedrops') calls selectMaterial(), which when the current tool is not a terrain tool calls setTool('paint') and openSidePanel('mat'), and overwrites the status with 'ARMED: ...' before eyedrop sets 'PICKED: ...'. So right-clicking to sample a material while using Select/Link/Light yanks the user into the Paint tool and pops a panel — a surprising side effect for a 'pick the color under the cursor' gesture. selectMaterial conflates two concerns (arm material vs. switch-to-paint).
evidence: `private eyedrop(...) { ... this.selectMaterial(t); ... }` and `private selectMaterial(id) { this.armMaterial(id); const isTerrainTool = ...; if (!isTerrainTool) this.setTool('paint'); this.openSidePanel('mat'); this.status('ARMED: ' + ...); }`.
reco: Have eyedrop call armMaterial() (which only arms the swatch/sandbox state) instead of selectMaterial(), so sampling never changes the active tool or opens a panel. Reserve the auto-switch-to-paint behavior for explicit palette clicks.
verify: Verified. eyedrop (5386-5394) calls this.selectMaterial(t). selectMaterial (3412-3421) does setTool('paint') when the current tool isn't a terrain tool, calls openSidePanel('mat'), and sets status 'ARMED:' (later overwritten by eyedrop's 'PICKED:'). So RMB-eyedrop while in Select/Link/Light forces the Paint tool and pops the material panel — an accurate description of a surprising side effect. Low/maintainability is appropriate (behavioral nuance, not a correctness bug).

title: Marquee selection of lights ignores per-light hidden/locked flags
desc: commitMarquee gates light inclusion only on the lights layer family (`!this.layerHidden.has('lights') && !this.layerLocked.has('lights')`), not on the per-light flags. Everywhere else lights are gated via lightSelectable(), which additionally checks `!light.hidden && !light.locked`. As a result a marquee can add an individually hidden or locked light to selectedIds, making it appear selected in the inspector/markers, while subsequent operations (drag targets at 4919, align/rotate, pruneSelection) silently skip it — an inconsistency between what looks selected and what acts selected.
evidence: `if (!this.layerHidden.has('lights') && !this.layerLocked.has('lights')) { for (const l of this.doc.lights) { if (l.x >= x0 && l.x <= x1 && l.y >= y0 && l.y <= y1) ids.push(l.id); } }` — no `this.lightSelectable(l)` check, unlike the objects loop just above which uses layerSelectableObj(o).
reco: Replace the family-only guard with a per-light `if (this.lightSelectable(l) && l.x >= x0 ...)` check so marquee selection matches every other selection path.
verify: Verified. commitMarquee (5099-5103) gates lights only on layerHidden/layerLocked('lights'), omitting the per-light flags. lightSelectable (8237-8239) is the canonical gate and additionally checks !light.hidden && !light.locked; the drag loop at 4918-4920 and the object marquee loop at 5092 both use the *Selectable helpers. So a marquee can add an individually hidden/locked light to selectedIds while later ops skip it — a genuine looks-selected vs acts-selected inconsistency. Low/bug is right.

title: clientToWorld returns the shared internal lastMouse object on a zero-size overlay
desc: When the overlay rect has zero width/height (Builder hidden, or laid out before first paint), clientToWorld returns `this.lastMouse` directly — the live internal field, not a copy. Callers treat the return value as a fresh {x,y} and some keep references or mutate derived state from it; handing back the mutable internal object risks aliasing bugs if any caller ever writes to the returned point. It is currently benign because the immediate callers reassign or only read, but it is a latent foot-gun in a method named like a pure transform.
evidence: `const rect = this.overlay.getBoundingClientRect(); if (rect.width <= 0 || rect.height <= 0) return this.lastMouse;`
reco: Return a fresh object: `return { x: this.lastMouse.x, y: this.lastMouse.y };` so the transform never leaks its internal state.
verify: Verified lines 4478-4491. On a zero-size overlay clientToWorld returns this.lastMouse (the live internal field) rather than a copy, while the normal path returns a fresh object literal. A caller that mutates the returned point would alias internal state. Currently benign (callers read or reassign), so this is a latent foot-gun, not an active bug; low is appropriate.

title: Redundant snap() recomputation in place() for slab kinds
desc: place() computes `let px = this.snap(x), py = this.snap(y)` and then, for door/runeDoor/valve/plug, immediately discards those and recomputes `px = this.snap(x) - Math.floor(...)` / `py = this.snap(y) - ...`, calling this.snap(x)/this.snap(y) a second (and effectively third) time on the same inputs. Minor wasted work and the local name `px`/`py` shadows the module-level `px` pixel-draw helper (line 490), hurting readability.
evidence: `let px = this.snap(x), py = this.snap(y); if (kind === 'door' || kind === 'runeDoor' || kind === 'valve' || kind === 'plug') { px = this.snap(x) - Math.floor(((params.w as number) ?? 3) / 2); py = this.snap(y) - Math.floor(((params.h as number) ?? 13) / 2); }`
reco: Compute `const sx = this.snap(x), sy = this.snap(y)` once and derive px/py from sx/sy; rename the locals (e.g. ox/oy or anchorX/anchorY) to avoid shadowing the px draw helper.
verify: Verified lines 5511-5517. px/py initialized via this.snap(x)/this.snap(y), then for door/runeDoor/valve/plug immediately reassigned with this.snap(x)/this.snap(y) recomputed — redundant snap calls on identical inputs. Module-level px helper confirmed at line 490, so the locals do shadow it. Trivial wasted work + readability nit; low/ai-slop is right.

title: editorLights array + objects reallocated every frame in author mode
desc: When the builder is open in author mode with light preview on and any lights present, the loop rebuilds state.editorLights every single frame via doc.lights.filter(...).map((l,n) => ({ ...toAuthoredLight(l, n), flicker: 0 })). This allocates a fresh array plus one new AuthoredLight object per light per frame, all consumed read-only by Lighting.ts. That is steady GC pressure in the editor's hot path even when nothing changed.
evidence: `state.editorLights = this.doc.lights.length > 0 ? this.doc.lights.filter((l) => !l.hidden && !this.mutedLightIds.has(l.id) && (this.soloLightId === null || l.id === this.soloLightId)).map((l, n) => ({ ...toAuthoredLight(l, n), flicker: 0 })) : null;` runs unconditionally each frame in the else branch. Lighting.ts only reads it (`if (ctx.state.editorLights && ctx.state.mode === 'build') this.seedAuthoredSet(...)`).
reco: Cache the computed array and only recompute when the inputs change (documentRevision / mutedLightIds / soloLightId / lightPreviewOn), reusing the existing dirty signals.
verify: Verified at lines 9246-9257: the else branch (author mode) rebuilds state.editorLights every frame via doc.lights.filter(...).map((l,n) => ({ ...toAuthoredLight(l, n), flicker: 0 })), allocating a fresh array + one object per light each frame. It runs unconditionally in that branch. Real GC pressure. However the guard is narrower than the finding's prose claims: it only fires when lightPreviewOn is true AND sessionMode !== 'live' (line 9238/9240 gate it), and the allocation scales with light count only. Author-mode-only, bounded by light count — downgrading medium to low.

title: Settle 'nothing moved' branch leaves documentRevision bumped, falsely marking the doc unsaved
desc: finishSettle calls this.markTerrainDirty() unconditionally before the result branches. markTerrainDirty bumps documentRevision (when not already paintDirty) and sets validationDirty/previewRuntimeDirty. The 'nothing moved' branch then restores only paintDirty (`this.paintDirty = this.settleWasDirty;`) but cannot un-bump documentRevision, so a settle run that changed zero cells still flips hasUnsavedChanges() to true and dirties validation/preview state. The comment ('restore, don't clear') shows the intent was to leave state untouched when nothing moved.
evidence: `this.markTerrainDirty();` then `} else { this.status('NOTHING MOVED'); this.paintDirty = this.settleWasDirty; }`. markTerrainDirty: `if (trackRevision && !this.paintDirty) this.documentRevision++;` plus validationDirty/previewRuntimeDirty = true.
reco: Only call markTerrainDirty() inside the branches that actually changed cells (overCap and before.idxs.length > 0), not before the no-op branch.
verify: Verified: finishSettle calls this.markTerrainDirty() unconditionally at line 8560, before the branch at 8561-8570. markTerrainDirty (lines 3935-3942) does `if (trackRevision && !this.paintDirty) this.documentRevision++` plus sets validationDirty/previewRuntimeDirty and clears doc.validation. The 'nothing moved' branch (8566-8569) only restores this.paintDirty = this.settleWasDirty and cannot un-bump documentRevision or undo the validation/preview invalidation. So a zero-cell settle flips hasUnsavedChanges true. The comment at 8568 confirms the intent was to leave state untouched. Real low-severity bug.

title: Inverted guard expressed as an empty if-body with a dangling else-for
desc: The link-wire draw guard is written as `if (this.layerHidden.has('links')) { /* skip */ } else for (const l of this.doc.links) { ... }`. An if with an empty body whose only content is a comment, followed by an unbraced `else for`, is confusing and fragile (any line inserted between the else and the for changes semantics). It reads as leftover scaffolding rather than a clean guard clause.
reco: Invert to a guard: `if (!this.layerHidden.has('links')) { for (const l of this.doc.links) { ... } }`, or early-continue at the top of the link section.
verify: Verified at lines 9431-9434: `if (this.layerHidden.has('links')) { /* comment only */ } else\n    for (const l of this.doc.links) {`. Exactly the empty-if-body-with-unbraced-else-for pattern described. Accurate, but it is purely a readability/maintainability nit — the logic is correct. Low severity / noise-adjacent is right.

title: Shadowed 'exported' binding in asset export bundle obscures intent
desc: runAssetBatchAction builds `const exported = records.map(...).filter(...)` (an array of {record, exported}) and then maps it with `({ record, exported }) => (...)`, where the destructured `exported` (an AssetStoreExport) shadows the outer `exported` array within the same expression. It is correct but the name collision makes the code harder to read and easy to mis-edit.
evidence: `const exported = records.map((record) => ({ record, exported: this.exportAssetRecord(record) })).filter(...); ... assets: exported.map(({ record, exported }) => ({ ... mime: exported.mime, text: exported.text }))`.
reco: Rename the outer array (e.g. `exportable`) or the inner field to avoid the same identifier referring to two different things in one expression.
verify: Verified at lines 11688-11708: `const exported = records.map((record) => ({ record, exported: this.exportAssetRecord(record) })).filter(...)` then `assets: exported.map(({ record, exported }) => ({ ... mime: exported.mime, text: exported.text }))`. The destructured inner `exported` (AssetStoreExport) shadows the outer `exported` array within the same map expression. Code is correct; this is a readability nit only. Low severity is appropriate.

title: Embedded sprite assets imported from share codes/JSON have no count cap
desc: Every other imported record family is `.slice(0, CAP)`'d before sanitization (objects 4096, links 8192, lights 1024, procedural 256, sparse pairs 80000). The embedded `assets.sprites` array is mapped through `sanitizeSpriteAsset` and filtered, but with no upper bound on the number of sprites. A hostile or accidentally-bloated share code (otherwise guarded only by the 12MB decompressed-JSON ceiling) could carry a very large sprite array that is fully materialized into the local library.
evidence: `const sprites = ((raw.assets as { sprites: unknown[] }).sprites).map(sanitizeSpriteAsset).filter((s): s is SpriteAsset => s !== null);` -- no `.slice(0, CAP)` unlike `objects`/`links`/`lights`/`proceduralHistory` above.
reco: Add a `DOC_SPRITE_CAP` and slice `raw.assets.sprites` before mapping, consistent with the other families.
verify: Verified document.ts:724-728: assets.sprites is mapped through sanitizeSpriteAsset and filtered with NO .slice(0,CAP), unlike objects (DOC_OBJECT_CAP), links, lights, proceduralHistory, and sparse pairs which all cap. The only size guard is SHARE_JSON_MAX_BYTES=12_000_000 enforced ONLY on the share-code path (shareCodeToDoc, line 777); the JSON-import and localStorage ingestion paths have no size ceiling at all, so the sprite array is effectively unbounded there. Consistent hardening/cap-convention gap; low severity appropriate.

title: PreviewRuntime sensor 'material' branch silently reads nothing when no filter is set, diverging from the validator
desc: In `senseZone`, a `sensorType === 'material'` sensor counts only `mechanism.materialFilter?.includes(type)`. If `materialFilter` is undefined (no filter chosen), the optional-chain yields undefined -> falsy -> counts nothing, so the sensor silently never reads in Logic Preview. The validator (validate.ts:890-894) instead treats a material sensor with no valid filter as a hard error ('material sensor needs a filter material'). The two should agree; as written the Logic Preview shows a dead sensor for a configuration the validator already flags, which is at least confusing and at worst masks the real cause during iteration.
evidence: `} else if (sensorType === 'material') { if (mechanism.materialFilter?.includes(type)) count++; }` -- no handling for the missing-filter case and no signal that the zone is being ignored.
reco: Minor consistency gap; the validator already errors so it rarely bites, but make the preview's no-filter material sensor behave predictably so preview and validation tell the same story.
verify: Verified PreviewRuntime.ts:661-662: the 'material' branch is `if (mechanism.materialFilter?.includes(type)) count++;` with no handling for undefined materialFilter -> silently counts nothing. validate.ts:890-894 pushes a hard error 'material sensor needs a filter material' for that config. Notably that error has no `code`, so playtestBlockingIssues (which filters by PLAYTEST_BLOCKING_CODES, validate.ts:97) does NOT block playtest/preview — so the dead sensor IS reachable in Logic Preview, matching the finding. Behavior divergence vs validator is real; low/maintainability is correct.

title: `rleDecode` relies on TypedArray.fill clamping with no bound check — fragile load-bearing invariant for every save/import path
desc: Foundation helper used by every builder save/import path. The decode loop never checks that the accumulated `pos` stays within `into.length`; it only works safely because `Uint8Array.fill(value, start, end)` clamps out-of-range start/end without throwing. This is an implicit assumption: any caller passing a smaller `into` than the encoded length gets silent truncation with zero feedback. Combined with the missing document-path length check (separate finding), corruption degrades silently instead of being detected.
evidence: `for (let i = 0; i + 2 < bin.length; i += 3) { const run = ...; into.fill(t, pos, pos + run); pos += run; }` -- no `if (pos >= into.length) break;` and no post-loop assertion that `pos === into.length`.
reco: Document the clamping contract at the call sites, or have callers (sanitizeWorldLayer, sanitizePrefab) verify `pos === expected` length after decode. The prefab path already does this via `rleLength`; the document path should too.
verify: Verified rle.ts:24-28: the decode loop does into.fill(t,pos,pos+run); pos+=run; with no bound check, relying on TypedArray.fill clamping; no post-loop assertion that pos===into.length. Accurate. Borderline by-design at the foundation layer (fill-clamping is documented JS behavior and disciplined callers like the prefab path pre-validate length via rleLength), so the truly actionable fix lives caller-side (finding 1); this is a defensive-hardening observation. Low severity is right; somewhat overlaps finding 1 but not a false positive.

title: PNG size cap is checked AFTER full decode, defeating its stated purpose
desc: PNG_DIM_CAP is documented as 'Refuse to decode anything larger before allocating buffers', but in pngBlobToRgba the dimension check (lines 43-45) runs only AFTER createImageBitmap(blob) has already fully decoded the image into a bitmap. A maliciously- or accidentally-crafted PNG (e.g. a 20000x20000 image, which is valid PNG and only a few KB compressed) is fully rasterized into memory before the cap rejects it, so the guard provides no protection against the very decompression-bomb case it claims to. There is also no file.size pre-check at the call sites in Builder.ts (importTerrainPng/importAsepritePair/importLonePng all call pngBlobToRgba directly on the user File).
evidence: Comment line 12: 'Refuse to decode anything larger before allocating buffers.' But createImageBitmap on line 38 decodes first; the `if (bitmap.width > PNG_DIM_CAP ...)` check is on line 43, inside the try AFTER the bitmap exists. createImageBitmap is the allocation.
reco: Either pass resize/quality limits to createImageBitmap, or (simpler and reliable) reject on blob.size before decoding, or read width/height from the IHDR (first ~24 bytes) and cap before decode. At minimum fix the comment so the next maintainer does not trust a guarantee that is not there.
verify: Verified png.ts: PNG_DIM_CAP comment line 12 says 'Refuse to decode anything larger before allocating buffers' but the width/height check (lines 43-45) runs AFTER createImageBitmap (line 38) has already decoded the bitmap. createImageBitmap is the decode/allocation, so the cap does not prevent the decompression-bomb case it documents. Confirmed no file.size pre-check at the three call sites in Builder.ts (lines 7784, 8004, 8022) via grep. However: there IS a post-decode 40000-cell cap at line 7789 for terrain, the 2048px dim cap does bound bitmap size, and this is a builder authoring tool consuming the user's OWN local files, not untrusted network input. The comment is misleading and the ordering is wrong, but the practical exposure is small. Downgraded medium -> low.

title: previewJsonImport collision detection diverges from the actual import commit
desc: previewJsonImport's findImportCollision (pipeline) excludes records whose contentSignature equals the incoming one (`record.contentSignature !== contentSignature`), so it reports 'New' for a same-id/same-content asset, whereas LocalStorageAssetStore.importJson's findImportCollision (AssetStore.ts 950-963) has NO such exclusion. The two collision predicates differ, so the preview shown to the user ('New X' / 'ID collision') can disagree with what importJson actually does (duplicate-skip / collision-reid). Same-content duplicates are caught earlier in both paths by the contentSignature dup check, so this is a UI-vs-commit consistency gap rather than data loss, but it is the kind of drift that produces 'the preview said New but it re-id'd' reports.
evidence: Pipeline findImportCollision filters `record.contentSignature !== contentSignature` (line 183); the store's findImportCollision (AssetStore.ts 957-962) matches on assetId/sourceId only with no contentSignature term. Two copies of essentially the same function with divergent logic.
reco: Extract a single shared findImportCollision used by both preview and commit, or document precisely why the predicates differ.
verify: Verified both predicates. Pipeline findImportCollision (AssetImportPipeline.ts 171-186) adds `record.contentSignature !== contentSignature` (line 183) so it skips same-content records; the store's findImportCollision (AssetStore.ts 950-963) has no such term and matches only on assetId/sourceId. The two genuinely diverge. As the finding itself acknowledges, same-content dups are caught earlier by the explicit contentSignature dup check in both paths (pipeline line 48-50, store line 286-288), so this is a preview-vs-commit UI consistency gap, not data loss. Real drift but low impact; medium is overstated -> low.

title: Duplicate payload-identify / collision / asset-id helpers across import modules
desc: parseImportPayload (AssetStore.ts 803-834) and identifyImportPayload (AssetImportPipeline.ts 129-164) are near-identical (the pipeline version just omits the `payload` field); stableImportedAssetId and findImportCollision are also duplicated verbatim between the two files. Because preview (pipeline) and commit (store) are meant to agree, keeping two copies guarantees they eventually drift (see the collision-predicate divergence finding).
evidence: AssetStore.ts parseImportPayload vs AssetImportPipeline.ts identifyImportPayload are the same doc/prefab/sprite sniff sequence with normalizeAssetToken on id; stableImportedAssetId is defined identically in both (AssetStore.ts 945-948, AssetImportPipeline.ts 166-169).
reco: Hoist payload identification, stableImportedAssetId, and collision detection into one shared helper that both preview and importJson call, so preview and commit can never disagree.
verify: Verified the duplication: parseImportPayload (AssetStore.ts 803-834) and identifyImportPayload (AssetImportPipeline.ts 129-164) are the same doc/prefab/sprite sniff sequence (pipeline omits the `payload` field). stableImportedAssetId is byte-identical in both (AssetStore.ts 945-948, AssetImportPipeline.ts 166-169). findImportCollision is duplicated and HAS already drifted (see index 1), which substantiates the reuse concern. Correctly rated low.

title: Bundle import rebuilds the entire asset database from localStorage per entry (O(entries x assets))
desc: importBundleJson loops over up to IMPORT_BUNDLE_MAX_ENTRIES (128) entries and, after each one, calls bundleWorkingDatabase -> localAssetDatabase, which reloads ALL documents, prefabs, sprites and import reports from localStorage and rebuilds a fresh AssetDatabase (including re-running dependency resolution and content-signature hashing over every stored asset). For a large library this is O(entries * total_assets) localStorage reads + JSON.parse on a user-blocking path. It is bounded (128 entries) so not catastrophic, but it is avoidable repeated full scans.
evidence: Line 408 `workingDatabase = this.bundleWorkingDatabase(workingDatabase);` runs every iteration; bundleWorkingDatabase (731) calls localAssetDatabase() (722) which loadDocLibrary()+loadPrefabs()+loadSprites()+listImportReports() and buildAssetDatabase() from scratch.
reco: Build the working database once before the loop and incrementally add each just-imported asset record to it instead of re-reading and re-indexing the whole store after every entry.
verify: Verified importBundleJson loop (AssetStore.ts 396-409): line 408 calls bundleWorkingDatabase every iteration; bundleWorkingDatabase (731-738) calls localAssetDatabase() (722-729) which runs loadDocLibrary()+loadPrefabs()+loadSprites()+listImportReports()+buildAssetDatabase() from scratch. So it is O(entries * total_assets) localStorage reads on the import path. Bounded by IMPORT_BUNDLE_MAX_ENTRIES (128) as stated. Real but minor; low is right.

title: mergeEmbeddedSprites re-ids document references before the save can fail
desc: On an id-collision-with-different-content, the code re-ids the incoming sprite, rewrites doc.objects[*].params.spriteId AND doc.assets.sprites[i] to the new id, increments reIded, THEN attempts saveSprite. If saveSprite returns false (quota full / storage unavailable), `added` is not incremented but the document has already been mutated to point at an id that exists only in the embedded doc copy, not the local library. Runtime resolveRuntimeSprite falls back to docSprites so visuals survive (fail-open holds), but the {added, reIded} return now reports a re-id with no corresponding library write, and the mutation-before-confirmed-persist ordering is fragile.
evidence: Lines 142-149: sprite re-id and `o.params.spriteId = sprite.id` happen, `reIded++`, then `if (saveSprite(sprite)) added++;` with no rollback of the doc mutation if saveSprite is false.
reco: Attempt saveSprite first; only rewrite doc references / bump reIded if the save succeeded, or explicitly document that the embedded copy is the source of truth on save failure and that {added,reIded} can disagree.
verify: Verified spritelib.ts 132-152: on id-collision-different-content the code re-ids the sprite, rewrites o.params.spriteId (line 145), and increments reIded (147) BEFORE `if (saveSprite(sprite)) added++` (149). If saveSprite fails, doc mutation persists with no library write and no rollback. As the finding notes, resolveRuntimeSprite falls back to docSprites so fail-open holds; the issue is the {added,reIded} accounting can report a re-id with no persisted write and the mutate-before-persist ordering is fragile. Accurately low.

title: ProjectAssetStore sanitizer enforces no upper bound on entry text size
desc: sanitizeProjectAssetEntry validates kind/origin/timestamp/signature and that sizeBytes (if present) matches byteLength(text), but applies no maximum to entry.text. The JSON import path enforces IMPORT_JSON_MAX_BYTES (12MB) and per-bundle-entry caps, but project assets read from IndexedDB / the File System Access folder bypass that ceiling entirely — listAssets/getAsset feed straight through sanitizeProjectAssetEntry, and makeProjectAssetRecords then JSON.parse's the blob again. Lower risk because it's the user's own project folder, but it is the one untrusted-input surface here with no size guard.
evidence: sanitizeProjectAssetEntry (86) checks `typeof entry.text !== 'string'` and the sizeBytes-consistency check (109) but never `entry.text.length > SOME_CAP`. Contrast AssetStore.importJson line 258 which does cap.
reco: Apply a sane per-entry byte cap (reuse IMPORT_BUNDLE_ENTRY_MAX_BYTES) inside sanitizeProjectAssetEntry so the project-folder/IndexedDB ingest path matches the import path's hardening.
verify: Verified sanitizeProjectAssetEntry (ProjectAssetStore.ts 86-127): it checks typeof entry.text==='string' (line 96) and the sizeBytes-consistency check (109) but applies no maximum to entry.text length. Contrast importJson (AssetStore.ts 258) which enforces IMPORT_JSON_MAX_BYTES. The project-asset path from IndexedDB/FileSystem bypasses that ceiling. As noted, it is the user's own project folder so low risk; correctly low.

title: IndexedDB request helper can resolve undefined and swallow a request-level error
desc: request() captures requestError in request.onerror but only ever rejects from tx.onerror/onabort. If a request fires onerror yet the surrounding transaction still reaches oncomplete (browsers can auto-commit if the error is not allowed to propagate), oncomplete does `resolve(result as T)` with result still undefined and the captured requestError is discarded. Callers like getAsset then treat undefined as 'not found' instead of surfacing the error. In normal operation a request error aborts the tx so this is an edge case, but the captured-but-unused requestError on the success path is a latent error-swallow.
evidence: Lines 372-374 set `requestError = request.error` but it is only consulted inside tx.onerror/onabort (379-386); tx.oncomplete (375-378) resolves unconditionally with possibly-undefined result.
reco: In oncomplete, if requestError is non-null reject with it; otherwise resolve, so a request-level failure is observable rather than collapsing to undefined.
verify: Verified request() helper (ProjectAssetStore.ts 350-388): request.onerror captures requestError (372-374) but tx.oncomplete (375-378) does `resolve(result as T)` unconditionally; requestError is only consulted in tx.onerror/onabort. If a request errors but the tx still completes, result stays undefined and the captured error is discarded, so getAsset treats it as not-found. As the finding states, a request error normally aborts the tx, so this is an edge case / latent error-swallow. Correctly low.

title: ProjectAssetEntry declares sizeBytes required but the sanitizer treats it as optional input
desc: ProjectAssetEntry declares `sizeBytes: number` (non-optional), but sanitizeProjectAssetEntry explicitly tolerates `entry.sizeBytes === undefined` (line 109) and then recomputes it. The runtime contract (sizeBytes may be absent on input) contradicts the compile-time type (always present). Harmless because the output always sets sizeBytes = expectedSize, but the `value as Partial<ProjectAssetEntry>` cast plus the optional-in-practice field means the type misrepresents the untrusted on-wire shape.
evidence: Interface line 32 `sizeBytes: number;` vs sanitizer line 109 treating undefined as valid input.
reco: Either make sizeBytes optional on a dedicated Raw/Partial on-wire input type, or require it in the sanitizer; today the type and the validator disagree.
verify: Verified: ProjectAssetEntry declares `sizeBytes: number` non-optional (line 32) but sanitizeProjectAssetEntry tolerates `entry.sizeBytes === undefined` (line 109) on input then recomputes expectedSize. The compile-time type says always-present while the runtime sanitizer treats it as optional-on-wire. Harmless (output always sets sizeBytes=expectedSize) as the finding concedes. Minor type-vs-wire mismatch; low is right.

title: escAttr is a no-op alias of esc that does not escape single quotes
desc: escAttr simply calls esc and adds nothing; both escape & < > and double-quote but not single-quote. The generated markup uses double-quoted attributes (style="...", title="...", data-asset-id="...") so double-quote escaping is sufficient, making escAttr functionally identical to esc. The separate name implies attribute-specific escaping that does not exist. Values flowing in (swatch, assetId, label) are derived from sanitized assets/hashes so this is not an injection finding, but the redundant aliased function is dead-weight indirection.
evidence: Line 232-234: `function escAttr(value: string): string { return esc(value); }` — identical body to esc.
reco: Delete escAttr and call esc directly, or make escAttr actually escape single quotes if attribute contexts could ever use single-quoted attributes.
verify: Verified AssetPreview.ts: escAttr (232-234) is `return esc(value)` with an identical body to esc (228-230); esc escapes & < > and double-quote but not single-quote. All generated attributes use double quotes (lines 94, 97, 100) so double-quote escaping suffices and escAttr is functionally identical to esc. As the finding states, inputs are sanitized assets/hashes so this is not injection. The redundant alias is dead-weight; correctly categorized as low ai-slop.

title: snapUnknown silently snaps semi-transparent pixels with no warning, unlike rgbaToCells
desc: rgbaToCells counts and reports semiTransparent pixels (0<a<255) so the importer can warn the artist that a soft brush was used. The 'snap all' path snapUnknown applies the same a<128 threshold but drops the semi-transparent accounting entirely, so when a user chooses Snap All on a PNG that also had antialiased edges, those edge pixels are force-snapped to materials with no surfaced warning, whereas the clean-import path warns. The two opaque-pixel decode paths give inconsistent feedback for the same image, undercutting the 'every mechanic needs visible feedback' rule for the snap branch.
evidence: rgbaToCells lines 59-60 track `semiTransparent`; snapUnknown (91-100) uses the same `< 128` threshold (line 95) but returns only `cells`, no warning surface. Builder.acceptTerrainPng-via-onSnapAll discards any such info.
reco: Have snapUnknown return (or the caller recompute) the semi-transparent count so the Snap All path can warn identically to the clean path.
verify: Verified pixmap.ts: rgbaToCells tracks semiTransparent (lines 56,60) using the a<128 threshold (61); snapUnknown (91-100) uses the same a<128 threshold (95) but returns only cells with no semiTransparent accounting. Confirmed in Builder.ts importTerrainPng (7793-7807): the clean path warns when semiTransparent>0 (7798-7800) but the onSnapAll path (7805) calls snapUnknown and surfaces no such warning. So an image with both unknown colors and antialiased edges gives inconsistent feedback when Snap All is chosen. Real but minor UX gap; low is right.

title: Beetle grazing indexes neighbor cells without a bounds check, wrapping to the wrong row at the world edge
desc: In the beetle graze branch, neighbor cells are read/written via `w.idx(xi + ddx, yi + ddy)` for offsets including [-1,0] and [c.facing,1] with no world.inBounds() guard (unlike the moth/light-shy scans elsewhere in the file which all guard with inBounds). World.idx performs no clamping (x + y*width), so when a beetle reaches xi===0 with a -1 dx offset, idx(-1, yi) resolves to yi*WIDTH-1 — the last column of the previous row — and if that cell happens to be Fungus/Moss it is overwritten to Empty and a particle is spawned at the wrong place. The outer loop only verifies inBounds(xi,yi), not the neighbor offsets.
evidence: Line 343: `const ti = w.idx(xi + ddx, yi + ddy);` then `const tt = w.types[ti]; if (tt === Cell.Fungus || tt === Cell.Moss) { w.types[ti] = Cell.Empty; ... }` with neighbor list `[[1,0],[-1,0],[0,-1],[c.facing,1]]` and no inBounds check on (xi+ddx, yi+ddy).
reco: Add `if (!w.inBounds(xi + ddx, yi + ddy)) continue;` before computing ti, consistent with every other neighbor scan in this file.
