verify: Verified Critters.ts:339-353: the beetle graze loop reads/writes w.idx(xi+ddx, yi+ddy) for offsets including [-1,0] with NO inBounds guard. World.idx (World.ts:52-54) is x + y*width with no clamping. The outer loop guards only inBounds(xi,yi) (line 209), and every other neighbor access in the file (lines 238,278,310,328,331,369,372,428,446,464,496) is inBounds-guarded — making this an inconsistent omission. At xi===0 with ddx=-1, idx(-1,yi) = yi*width-1 = last column of the previous row; if Fungus/Moss it is overwritten to Empty and a particle spawns at the wrong place. Real edge-wrap bug, but only triggers for a beetle at world x=0 on a 0.004/frame roll with adjacent fungus, and the effect is one mis-grazed cell. Low is correct.

title: Redundant liquid checks: isLiquid combined with an exact Water equality test
desc: Several conditions test both isLiquid(t) and t === Cell.Water, where one subsumes the other. Line 142 `if (isLiquid(t) && t === Cell.Water ...)` — isLiquid(t) is implied by t === Cell.Water, so the isLiquid call is dead. Lines 256 and 301 `here === Cell.Water || isLiquid(here)` are the inverse redundancy — isLiquid(here) already covers Water, so the explicit Water branch is dead. Harmless but confusing (suggests Water might not be a liquid).
evidence: Line 142: `if (isLiquid(t) && t === Cell.Water && counts.fish < CAPS.fish)`. Line 256: `const inWater = here === Cell.Water || isLiquid(here);`. Line 301: `if (here === Cell.Water || isLiquid(here)) {`.
reco: Pick one predicate per site: use `t === Cell.Water` where fish specifically need water, and `isLiquid(here)` where any liquid counts. Drop the redundant operand.
verify: Verified isLiquid (CellType.ts:112-114) includes Cell.Water. Line 142 'isLiquid(t) && t === Cell.Water' — second conjunct implies the first, so isLiquid is dead. Lines 256 and 301 'here === Cell.Water || isLiquid(here)' — isLiquid already covers Water, so the explicit Water disjunct is dead. Both redundancies real and confirmed at the cited lines. Harmless, purely cosmetic/clarity; correctly rated low ai-slop noise.

title: reviveSavedPickup is a misleading alias that just re-calls snapshotPickupForSave
desc: reviveSavedPickup(pickup) has an identical body to snapshotPickupForSave — it just `return snapshotPickupForSave(pickup)`. The name implies an inverse (save -> revive) transform, but it performs the same forward transform (clone data, force tome offerPending=false). For tomes this happens to be the correct restore behavior, but having two differently-named functions with the same body for the save and load directions obscures intent and invites future divergence bugs.
evidence: Lines 196-204: `function snapshotPickupForSave(pickup) { const data = {...pickup.data}; if (pickup.kind === 'tome') data.offerPending = false; return {...pickup, data}; }` immediately followed by `function reviveSavedPickup(pickup) { return snapshotPickupForSave(pickup); }`.
reco: Either inline reviveSavedPickup at its single call site (restoreLevel line 1588) and call snapshotPickupForSave directly, or give it a real revive-specific body if one is ever needed. Remove the redundant indirection.
verify: Verified Levels.ts:196-204: reviveSavedPickup(pickup) body is exactly 'return snapshotPickupForSave(pickup)' — identical forward transform (clone data, force tome offerPending=false). For tomes the forward transform happens to be the correct restore behavior, so there is no bug today; it is purely a misleading-name/duplication maintainability smell that invites future save/load divergence. Low maintainability is correct.

title: Legacy review saves are detected but never retired, leaving a permanently un-resumable save in localStorage
desc: tryResumeExpedition calls isLegacyReviewSave and, when true, returns false WITHOUT retiring the save (unlike the genVersion mismatch path two lines up, which calls retireSavedExpedition). hasSavedExpedition() therefore keeps reporting true for a save that can never resume; the run launcher's 'Continue' affordance may stay lit for a dead save. It is eventually overwritten when startDescent falls through to enterLevel(START_LEVEL) -> saveExpedition, but only after the player commits to a fresh run, and any UI that reads hasSavedExpedition before that point is misled.
evidence: Line 1464-1467 retires on genVersion mismatch via retireSavedExpedition. Line 1468 `if (this.isLegacyReviewSave(save)) return false;` — bare return, no retire, no toast.
reco: Mirror the genVersion path: call retireSavedExpedition(ctx, '...') before returning false for legacy review saves so hasSavedExpedition() reports honestly and the stale blob is dropped.
verify: Verified Levels.ts: genVersion mismatch (1464-1467) calls retireSavedExpedition (which abandonExpedition -> localStorage.removeItem at 818), but the legacy-review branch (1468) does a bare 'return false' with no retire. hasSavedExpedition (808-814) only checks the localStorage key exists, so it keeps returning true for an un-resumable legacy save. tryResumeExpedition returns false -> startDescent (515) falls through to enterLevel(START_LEVEL)+saveExpedition, overwriting it, but only after the player commits to a fresh run; any UI reading hasSavedExpedition before that is misled. The 'Continue affordance' impact is real but narrow — isLegacyReviewSave matches a hardcoded reviewer loadout (maxHp>=180, specific perks/wands, collection>=22), an extremely rare special case. Asymmetry is real; low is correct.

title: cell/fill paint helpers bypass World.replaceCellAt, leaving stale activeCharges/colorOverrides indices
desc: paintDisc and paintRect write world.types[i]/colors[i]/life[i]/charge[i] directly instead of calling world.replaceCellAt(i, type, color). World maintains two sparse indices that must stay in lockstep with the planes: activeCharges (driven by setChargeAt/clearChargeAt) and colorOverrides (a Set of indices whose color survives swaps). Writing charge[i] = 0 directly does NOT remove i from activeCharges, and nothing here deletes i from colorOverrides the way replaceCellAt/clearCellAt do. Painting a 'cell' or 'fill' disc/rect over previously charged or color-overridden cells therefore leaves stale entries. buildPlayground (line 490-495) correctly uses replaceCellAt/clearCellAt, so this is an inconsistency, not the house style.
evidence: paintDisc: `world.types[i] = type; world.colors[i] = fn ? fn() : EMPTY_COLOR; world.life[i] = 0; world.charge[i] = 0;` (lines 629-632). World.replaceCellAt does `this.clearChargeAt(i)` and `this.colorOverrides.delete(i)` (World.ts 88-94) which the hand-rolled writes skip. The electrical pass self-heals activeCharges (electrical.ts:18 deletes entries with charge<=0), but colorOverrides is never reconciled, so a painted cell that previously had an override keeps the flag and will preserve the wrong color through a later swap.
reco: Replace the four manual plane writes in paintDisc/paintRect with world.replaceCellAt(i, type, fn ? fn() : EMPTY_COLOR) (or clearCellAt when type === Cell.Empty). This both fixes the index drift and removes the duplicated plane-write logic.
verify: Verified in commands.ts: paintDisc (629-632) and paintRect (670-673) write world.charge[i]=0 directly and never touch colorOverrides, while replaceCellAt (World.ts 88-94) calls clearChargeAt + colorOverrides.delete. buildPlayground (490-494) correctly uses replaceCellAt/clearCellAt, confirming the inconsistency. The activeCharges self-heal claim checks out (electrical.ts 18-20 deletes entries with charge<=0), so colorOverrides is the genuinely-unreconciled index: World.swap (173-197) preserves an override flag through movement, so a painted-over previously-overridden cell keeps a wrong color through a later swap. Both cell (2031) and fill (2082) commands reach these. Real correctness inconsistency, but confined to the debug console and requires a rare precondition (paint over an overridden cell that then moves via swap); downgrading medium->low.

title: 'run' registered as both an exec alias and a top-level command — dead alias at prompt, divergent behavior inside scripts
desc: The exec command declares aliases: ['run'] (line 1588), and a separate top-level 'run' command (the expedition controller) is added later (line 1699). ConsoleCommandRegistry.add inserts aliases then names into the same byName map, and the later 'run' name overwrites the earlier 'run' alias, so at the prompt `run` always resolves to the expedition controller and the exec alias is dead. However, executeNamedScript intercepts lines whose name is 'exec' OR 'run' with exactly one arg (line 1138) and treats them as nested-script calls. The result: at the console `run foo` means 'expedition control', but inside a script `run foo` means 'execute script foo'. This is a confusing context-dependent meaning and a latent bug if anyone relies on the documented alias.
evidence: exec: `aliases: ['run']` (1588). run command: `name: 'run', aliases: ['expedition']` (1699-1700). Script interceptor: `parsed && (parsed.name === 'exec' || parsed.name === 'run') && parsed.args.length === 1 ? await executeNamedScript(...)` (1137-1139). registry.add overwrites: `this.byName.set(def.name.toLowerCase(), def)` runs for the later 'run' command, clobbering the earlier alias mapping (registry.ts 147).
reco: Drop 'run' from the exec aliases (keep only 'exec'), and remove 'run' from the script-nested-call interceptor so nested scripts use only 'exec'. This makes `run` unambiguously the expedition command everywhere.
verify: Confirmed: exec declares aliases:['run'] (1588); a later top-level run command (1699, aliases:['expedition']) is added. registry.add (registry.ts 145-148) sets byName by name then aliases, and the later run name overwrites the earlier run alias, so registry.exec ('run ...', 95-97) resolves run to the expedition controller — the exec alias is dead at the prompt. Inside scripts, executeNamedScript intercepts parsed.name==='exec'||'run' with 1 arg (1137-1139) and treats it as a nested-script call. So 'run foo' means expedition-control at the prompt but execute-script-foo inside a script. Behavior is real and context-dependent, but it is a debug console with a dead documented alias; substance is a maintainability/footgun nit -> low rather than medium.

title: give gold path duplicates grantGold logic but omits the tainted flag and uses inline score mutation
desc: The `give gold` branch re-implements grantGold inline (taintIfNeeded + score += amount + scoreChanged emit) instead of calling the existing grantGold helper used by the dedicated `gold` command. The two paths also diverge in their result data: grantGold includes `tainted: ctx.state.debugGodMode` (line 780) while the give-gold result omits it. This is copy-paste duplication that can drift.
evidence: give: `const taint = taintIfNeeded(ctx, 'give', target.target); ctx.state.score += amount; ctx.events.emit('scoreChanged', { score: ctx.state.score }); return result(true, ..., { target, kind, amount, score: ctx.state.score });` (1949-1952) vs grantGold which returns the same shape plus `tainted` (776-781).
reco: Have the `give gold` branch delegate to grantGold(ctx, amount, target.target) (merging any extra fields like kind), eliminating the duplicate mutation and aligning the result payload.
verify: give gold branch (1949-1952) re-implements grantGold inline: taintIfNeeded + score+=amount + scoreChanged emit, and its result omits the tainted field that grantGold includes (780). Minor wrinkle: grantGold taints under command 'gold' while give taints under 'give', so they aren't byte-identical, but the structural duplication and the missing tainted field in the result data are accurately described. Low ai-slop/drift nit confirmed.

title: Single-flask spec parsing duplicated between parseRunFlaskSpec and the inline --flask handler
desc: parseRunFlaskSpec parses a `material[:count]`/empty spec (used for the --flasks list), and the --flask handler parses the same grammar inline with slightly different control flow and defaulting. Two copies of the same material:count parsing rules invite drift (e.g. the empty/none handling and 0-600 clamp are written twice).
evidence: parseRunFlaskSpec: `const [rawMaterial, rawCount] = raw.split(':', 2); ... if (rawMaterial.toLowerCase() === 'empty' || ... 'none') material = null; else parseCellType(...)` (1263-1272). Inline --flask: `const [rawMaterial, rawCount] = parsed.value.split(':', 2); if (rawMaterial.toLowerCase() === 'empty' || ... 'none') flaskMaterial = null; else { const material = parseCellType(...) }` (1425-1432).
reco: Reuse parseRunFlaskSpec inside the --flask handler (it already returns a FlaskSlotConfig) and map its result into flaskMaterial/flaskCount, removing the inline duplicate.
verify: parseRunFlaskSpec (1262-1281) parses material[:count] with empty/none -> null and a 0-600 clamp; the inline --flask handler (1422-1440) re-parses the same grammar with separate flaskMaterial/flaskCount vars and a different control flow (and notably skips the 'empty flask cannot have cells' validation parseRunFlaskSpec has). Genuine duplication of the same parsing rules; the divergence already present reinforces the drift risk. Low maintainability nit confirmed.

title: requireNoBuilderPlaytestPersistentState is a pure pass-through wrapper around blockBuilderPlaytestPersistentState
desc: requireNoBuilderPlaytestPersistentState just returns blockBuilderPlaytestPersistentState(command, target) verbatim. The codebase uses both names for the same purpose — give/god call blockBuilderPlaytestPersistentState directly, while gold/heal go through the wrapper — so there is no abstraction benefit, only an extra indirection and an inconsistent call pattern.
evidence: `function requireNoBuilderPlaytestPersistentState(command: string, target: ConsoleTarget): CommandResult | null { return blockBuilderPlaytestPersistentState(command, target); }` (369-371); callers: gold (2360) and heal (2376) use the wrapper, give (1941) and god (1784) call the underlying function.
reco: Delete the wrapper and have gold/heal call blockBuilderPlaytestPersistentState directly for a single consistent name.
verify: requireNoBuilderPlaytestPersistentState (369-371) just returns blockBuilderPlaytestPersistentState(command,target) verbatim. Grep confirms inconsistent call pattern: god (1784) and give (1941) call the underlying function; gold (2360) and heal (2376) go through the wrapper. Pure pass-through with no abstraction benefit. Low ai-slop confirmed.

title: watch list / bind list resave the loaded value as a side effect of a read-only subcommand
desc: Both `watch list` and `bind list` call the save function with the just-loaded value (saveConsoleWatches(watches) / saveConsoleBinds(binds)) even though listing should be read-only. It is presumably a deliberate normalize-on-read, but it is unobvious, performs an extra localStorage write on every list, and is not commented, so it reads as an accidental write.
evidence: watch: `const watches = loadConsoleWatches(); saveConsoleWatches(watches); return result(true, ...)` (1631-1633). bind: `const binds = loadConsoleBinds(); saveConsoleBinds(binds); ...` (1664-1666).
reco: If normalize-on-read is intended, add a one-line comment; otherwise drop the redundant save from the list paths.
verify: watch list (1631-1632) and bind list (1664-1665) call saveConsole*(loaded value) on a read-only listing. Reading prefs.ts: loadConsoleWatches/saveConsoleWatches both apply the same normalize/dedupe/sort/clamp (35-51), so the resave is a deliberate normalize-on-read that persists the cleaned-up canonical form (migrates legacy/dirty stored values). Harmless and arguably intentional; the only genuine defect is that it's an unobvious, uncommented extra localStorage write on every list. Low, leans toward intended behavior.

title: Watch HUD polls every 500ms via the full command pipeline regardless of console visibility
desc: A 500ms setInterval drives refreshWatchHud, which for each watched path runs ctx.console.exec('get ' + path) — a full tokenize/parse/dispatch/resolveParamPath round-trip — even when the console overlay is closed. It early-outs only when there are zero watches. With the cap of 12 watches this is bounded, but it is continuous background work (string parsing + promise fan-out) tied to wall-clock rather than to whether the HUD is actually shown.
evidence: `window.setInterval(() => void this.refreshWatchHud(), 500)` (162); refreshWatchHud maps paths to `await this.ctx.console.exec('get ' + path)` (528) with no guard on this.openState; only `paths.length === 0` short-circuits (520).
reco: Skip the refresh (or read resolveParamPath directly without the exec string round-trip) when the console is closed, or resolve watch values via a lightweight direct getter instead of constructing and re-parsing a `get <path>` command each tick.
verify: ConsoleOverlay.ts line 162 window.setInterval(()=>void this.refreshWatchHud(),500) has no guard on openState; refreshWatchHud (516-524) early-outs only when paths.length===0. For each watched path it runs await this.ctx.console.exec('get '+path) (528) — a full tokenize/parse/dispatch round-trip — wall-clock-driven regardless of overlay visibility. Bounded to <=12 watches (CONSOLE_WATCH_LIMIT) and only when watches exist, so genuinely minor, but the continuous-background-work claim is accurate. Low performance nit confirmed.

title: refreshWatchHud permanently prunes any watch whose 'get' returns ok:false
desc: After fetching each watch value, the HUD filters to only ok rows and, if the count differs, calls saveConsoleWatches(validPaths), permanently dropping any path whose `get` failed. resolveParamPath failure is deterministic for a given path, so this is usually fine, but it means a transient resolution failure (e.g. a path under ctx.params.spells before a subsystem is initialized) would silently and permanently delete a user's pinned watch with no feedback.
evidence: `const validPaths = rows.filter((row) => row.ok).map((row) => row.path); if (validPaths.length !== paths.length) saveConsoleWatches(validPaths);` (539-540).
reco: Only prune watches that fail with a definitively-unknown-path error code (e.g. data.code === 'parse-param-path'), not any ok:false result, to avoid deleting watches on transient resolution failures.
verify: ConsoleOverlay.ts 539-540: validPaths keeps only ok rows and resaves via saveConsoleWatches when the count drops, permanently pruning any watch whose 'get' returned ok:false with no feedback. Behavior is real. The 'get' command (2285-2293) fails only when resolveParamPath returns a CommandResult (unresolvable path); watch paths are prefix-restricted by normalizeWatchPath, so the transient-failure-during-init scenario is plausible but speculative — the auto-pruning data-loss edge itself is definitely real. The category 'type-safety' is misapplied (this is a correctness/UX issue), but I judge on substance. Low confirmed.

title: `let vx`/`let vy` never reassigned in tickKnock (lint error, prefer-const)
desc: `tickKnock` declares `let vx = (e.knockVx ?? 0) * KNOCK_DRAG;` and `let vy = ((e.knockVy ?? 0) + KNOCK_GRAV) * KNOCK_DRAG;`. Neither is ever reassigned in the function body — they are only read (Math.hypot, added to e.fx/e.fy) and copied back to `e.knockVx`/`e.knockVy`. `npx eslint` flags both as `prefer-const`. These are not load-bearing port constants, so the fix is safe and the repo's hard invariant #7 requires `tsc`/lint to be clean.
evidence: eslint output: `282:9 error 'vx' is never reassigned. Use 'const' instead prefer-const` and `283:9 ... 'vy' ...`. Bodies at 284-339 read but never write `vx`/`vy`.
reco: Change both to `const`.
verify: Ran `npx eslint src/entities/Enemies.ts`: exactly two errors, `282:9 'vx' is never reassigned. Use 'const' instead prefer-const` and `283:9 'vy' ...`. Read lines 282-339: vx/vy are only read (Math.hypot, e.fx+=vx, e.knockVx=vx) and never reassigned in tickKnock. Not load-bearing port constants. Invariant #7 requires clean lint. Low is correct (cosmetic).

title: Leviathan damage-resistance reads a stale `submerged` flag recomputed only every 4th frame and persisted across save/load
desc: `damage()` glances hits off the leviathan when `e.submerged === true`, but `submerged` is only recomputed in the AI update on `e.timer % 4 === 0` (line 1269-1279) and is persisted in expedition saves (Levels.ts:249/292). Consequences: (1) for up to 3 frames after the pool is drained the leviathan still shrugs off hits (and vice-versa after it re-enters water); (2) on save/resume the flag is restored to whatever it was when saved, so a leviathan saved while submerged stays damage-immune for up to 4 frames after load even if the basin was drained between sessions, until the next `timer%4` recompute. The damage census is `world` cells, so the fix is cheap. Impact is small (a few frames) but it is a real correctness gap in a deliberately water-gated boss, and the persisted flag is the kind of stale-state the review brief calls out.
evidence: Enemies.ts:206 `if (e.kind === 'leviathan' && e.submerged === true) { amount *= 0.25; ... }`; submerged set only at 1269 `if (e.timer % 4 === 0) { ... e.submerged = waterN >= 8; }`. Levels.ts:249 `if (e.submerged === true) saved.submerged = true;` and 292 restore.
reco: Either recompute the submersion census inside `damage()` (it is a small fixed-stride scan) when the target is a leviathan, or don't persist `submerged` in saves so it is recomputed from real cells on the first post-load AI tick.
verify: Code is as described: damage() at Enemies.ts:206 glances hits while e.submerged===true; submerged is recomputed only at Enemies.ts:1269 `if (e.timer % 4 === 0)`; Levels.ts:249 saves it and 292 restores it (timer is also persisted/restored at 266). The within-session ≤3-frame lag is an intended load-bearing `%4` cadence throttle (~50ms at 60Hz, negligible). The save/resume stale-flag window is real but self-corrects within ≤4 frames once the recompute runs, and requires a contrived basin-vs-flag mismatch. Accurately described, not a hallucination, but genuinely trivial. Low is correct.

title: RigidBodies.spawn may evict a long-lived tagged body (e.g. player corpse) on the next spawn
desc: `spawn` enforces `MAX_DYNAMIC_BODIES` by calling `evictOldestDynamic()` which removes the oldest un-held dynamic body. Callers that hold a long-lived reference to a spawned body — notably `PlayerControl.kill()` which stores the spawned corpse in `this.corpse` and later reads `corpse.sleeping` every frame in `tickCorpse` — can have that body silently evicted out from under them if 80+ dynamic bodies pile up (a chain of detonations/shatters right at death). The corpse is not `held`, so `evictOldestDynamic` is free to pick it; after eviction `tickCorpse` keeps polling a body Rapier no longer owns (its `sleeping` mirror is frozen, so the settle path relies on the 240-frame timeout fallback, and `clearCorpse`'s `remove` becomes a no-op). The fallback timeout means it is not a hard lock, but the corpse-settle feedback can be stranded.
evidence: RigidBodies.ts:117 `if (dyn >= MAX_DYNAMIC_BODIES) this.evictOldestDynamic();`; evictOldestDynamic (184-191) only protects `this.held`, not arbitrary tagged bodies. Player.ts:832 stores `this.corpse = ctx.rigidBodies.spawn({...}, ... tag:'player-corpse')` and tickCorpse(863) polls `corpse.sleeping` each frame.
reco: Exempt tagged/long-lived bodies (e.g. `tag === 'player-corpse'`) from eviction, or have eviction skip any body in a registered protect-set; alternatively have `tickCorpse` detect that the corpse is no longer in `ctx.rigidBodies.bodies` and settle immediately.
verify: Verified: evictOldestDynamic (RigidBodies.ts:184-191) only protects this.held, not tagged bodies; spawn() calls it at line 117 when dyn>=MAX_DYNAMIC_BODIES (=80). The player corpse (Player.ts:832, tag 'player-corpse') is NOT set as held, so it is eligible for eviction. tickCorpse (863) polls corpse.sleeping each frame. However the corpse is the NEWEST dynamic body, so eviction only reaches it after ~80 newer dynamic bodies spawn post-death (each evicting one older first) — an extreme flood scenario. And tickCorpse's `corpseT > 240` timeout (867) always settles the UI, so no hard lock (fail-open holds). Real but low-impact architectural fragility, accurately characterized as non-locking. Low is correct.

title: Play-mode bomb-charge mouseup path and number-key spell select are dead in play
desc: The number-key spell selector that sets ctx.player.spell is inside the `if (ctx.state.mode !== 'play')` branch, so in play mode player.spell never changes from its default 'bolt'. The charged-bomb release on mouseup (InputManager 257-282) is gated by `ctx.player.spell === 'bomb'`, which can never be true in play. So the entire Worms-style bomb-charge flow (Spells.firePlayerSpell bomb branch + this mouseup release) is unreachable in the live game; it only runs in build/gallery. This is leftover scaffolding from the pre-wand spell system.
evidence: InputManager.ts:431 digit handler sits under the `mode !== 'play'` block opened at 418. InputManager.ts:262 release requires `ctx.player.spell === 'bomb'`. Spells.ts:215-219 bomb branch only charges input.bombCharge, never spawns.
reco: Either remove the dead play-mode bomb-charge path or document it as build/gallery-only. Leaving two contradictory firing systems (player.spell vs wand program) co-resident in the play input path is a maintenance trap.
verify: Verified. The only assignment to player.spell (other than the 'bolt' default at Player.ts:154) is InputManager.ts:434, which sits inside the `if (ctx.state.mode !== 'play')` block opened at line 418 (returns at 438). So in play mode player.spell is permanently 'bolt'. The mouseup charged-bomb release (InputManager.ts:257-283) requires both `mode === 'play'` AND `player.spell === 'bomb'`, which can never both hold. Spells.ts:215-219 bomb branch only increments input.bombCharge. The entire bomb-charge flow is unreachable in live play. Cited line ranges are slightly off (actual mouseup 257-288, digit handler 431-437) but substance is correct. Low is right — leftover pre-wand scaffolding, no player-facing bug.

title: loadLoadout/restoreRuntimeState trust unvalidated card ids; a bad id throws in compileWand
desc: Both restore methods do `w.cards.push(...saved.cards)` with no validation, unlike cardDiscovery.ts which filters via isCardId. compileWand later does `const def = CARD_DEFS[id]; if (def.kind === ...)` (compiler.ts:103-104) — a bogus/removed card id yields def===undefined and throws a TypeError at first cast/compile. The expedition resume path wraps this in a catch-and-retire (Levels.ts:1471-1508), so corrupted expedition saves degrade gracefully, but the WandBench and Builder restore paths do not have that net, and the contract that 'callers must validate' is implicit and fragile.
evidence: WandSystem.ts:724 `w.cards.push(...saved.cards);` (raw). compiler.ts:103 `const def = CARD_DEFS[id];` then 104 `if (def.kind === 'modifier')` with no undefined guard. Contrast cardDiscovery.ts:19-23 which filters with isCardId.
reco: Filter saved.cards through an isCardId guard (slotting null for unknown ids) inside loadLoadout/restoreRuntimeState, or have compileWand skip ids not present in CARD_DEFS. This makes the wand engine robust to any caller, not just the one wrapped in try/catch.
verify: Verified. loadLoadout (WandSystem.ts:724) and restoreRuntimeState (764) both do `w.cards.push(...saved.cards)` with no isCardId filtering, unlike cardDiscovery.ts:20 which filters. compiler.ts:103-104 does `const def = CARD_DEFS[id]; if (def.kind === 'modifier')` with no undefined guard, so a bogus id yields def===undefined → TypeError on compile/cast. Save types declare cards as (CardId|null)[] so it is statically sound; the risk is hand-edited/cross-version JSON at runtime. Levels.ts wraps expedition resume in catch-and-retire but WandBench/Builder restore paths lack that net. Real defensive-coding gap, correctly rated low.

title: Bounce-card reflection axis variable names are inverted relative to what they test
desc: In the terrain bounce handler, `hitVertical = solidAt(world, gx, prevGy)` actually tests the X-axis step (new gx, previous gy), and `hitHorizontal = solidAt(world, prevGx, gy)` tests the Y-axis step. The names suggest the opposite of the axis they probe. The resulting reflection logic happens to be correct (x-step hit flips vx, y-step hit flips vy, corner flips both), but the misleading names make this corner-reflection logic very hard to verify and easy to break on edit.
evidence: Projectiles.ts:835 `const hitVertical = solidAt(world, gx, prevGy); // the x-step alone hits` then `if (hitVertical || !hitHorizontal) p.vx *= -0.55;` — a variable named 'vertical' driving the vx flip.
reco: Rename to hitFromXStep/hitFromYStep (or xBlocked/yBlocked) so the names match the axis being probed; the inline comments already describe the correct semantics.
verify: Verified Projectiles.ts:835-838. `hitVertical = solidAt(world, gx, prevGy)` tests new-x with previous-y (the X-axis step), and drives the vx flip — the name 'vertical' is inverted relative to the horizontal motion it actually probes. The reflection logic is functionally correct (the author even added an inline comment '// the x-step alone hits') and the corner case works, but the names contradict the axis. Genuine naming-clarity issue, low severity bordering on noise given the clarifying comment already present.

title: Per-frame closure allocation in the projectile update hot path
desc: update() allocates a fresh `removeAt` arrow closure (capturing `projectiles`) every frame. This is once per frame, not per projectile, so impact is small, but it is avoidable GC pressure in the tick path and the same pattern repeated across systems adds up. The function does not capture per-call state and could be a private method or module function.
evidence: Projectiles.ts:521 `const removeAt = (idx: number): void => { const last = projectiles.length - 1; if (idx !== last) projectiles[idx] = projectiles[last]; projectiles.pop(); };` declared inside update().
reco: Hoist to a private method `private removeAt(projectiles, idx)` or a module-level helper taking the array, eliminating the per-frame closure allocation.
verify: Verified Projectiles.ts:521-525: the `removeAt` arrow closure capturing `projectiles` is declared inside update(), so one allocation per frame (not per projectile). The finding itself correctly characterizes impact as small/avoidable. Technically accurate but trivial — borderline noise. Low is the ceiling.

title: electricCharge mod is permanent and never pruned, keeping the WeakMap entry alive for the projectile's life
desc: pruneProjectileMods keeps a ProjectileModState alive while electricCharge===true, and electricCharge is set once and never cleared. Combined with the per-frame `chargeNearby` (frameCount % 4) this is intended, but it means an electric projectile re-scans a disk of cells every 4 frames for its whole life and the mod state is retained until the projectile object is GC'd. Not a leak (WeakMap), but worth noting the cost scales with electric-projectile lifetime and there is no decay/budget like the trail mods have.
evidence: Projectiles.ts:602-604 `if (mods.electricCharge && ctx.state.frameCount % 4 === 0) chargeNearby(ctx, Math.floor(p.x), Math.floor(p.y), 2, 8);` with no countdown; pruneProjectileMods (191-202) explicitly never removes an electricCharge mod.
reco: If indefinite passive charging is intended, leave as is but add a comment; otherwise give electricCharge a frame budget consistent with the trail/homing mods so long-lived projectiles (wisp life 240, meteor 300) don't charge terrain forever.
verify: Verified. pruneProjectileMods (Projectiles.ts:191-201) returns early (retains the mod) whenever mods.electricCharge===true, and electricCharge is set once in markProjectile and never cleared (no countdown, unlike shortHomingFrames). chargeNearby runs every 4 frames (602-604) for the projectile's whole life. The finding explicitly states this is intended and not a leak (WeakMap-backed). Accurate maintainability note about per-life scan cost, correctly low.

title: Wisp with Short-Homing applies two independent steering passes the same frame
desc: A wisp projectile has built-in homing in the per-type steering block AND, if launched with the shorthoming modifier, also runs applyShortHoming. Both nudge p.vx/p.vy toward the nearest enemy and both apply their own speed caps in the same frame, so a short-homing wisp steers roughly twice as hard as intended and the two caps (5.2 vs max(3.5, initialSpeed*1.2)) fight each other. Likely unintended stacking rather than a designed synergy.
evidence: Projectiles.ts:561 wisp branch adds `p.vx += ((best.x - p.x) / d) * 0.42` and caps at 5.2; then 599-601 `const mods = PROJECTILE_MODS.get(p); if (mods) { this.applyShortHoming(ctx, p, mods); ...}` adds another `p.vx += (...) * 0.36` with a different cap. Both run for a wisp carrying shorthoming.
reco: Skip applyShortHoming for p.type==='wisp' (which already homes), or have the wisp branch early-out when a shorthoming mod is present, so steering isn't double-applied with conflicting caps.
verify: Verified the stacking is real. wisp built-in seek (Projectiles.ts:561-584) nudges vx/vy by 0.42 toward nearest enemy capped at 5.2; then applyShortHoming (599-601 → 348-384) adds another 0.36 nudge capped at max(3.5, initialSpeed*1.2). Both run unconditionally for any projectile with the shortHoming mod. cards.ts confirms wisp (projectile) and shorthoming (modifier) are both valid, non-exclusive cards, and WandSystem.ts:444-453 produces type:'wisp' then calls markProjectile which applies shortHoming regardless of type. So a shorthoming-wisp double-steers with two competing caps. Plausibly unintended stacking; low severity, niche card combo.

title: frostshard/iceshard and wisp projectile lifetimes differ between the wand path and the legacy paths
desc: The same logical spells get different `life` values depending on which spawner runs: wand frostshard→iceshard uses life 180 while Spells.firePlayerSpell/castBuildSpell use 140; wand wisp uses 240 while the legacy paths use 260. Because impact behavior is otherwise identical, these divergent magic literals are accidental drift, not load-bearing port constants, and make the effective range of a 'Frost Shard' depend on which subsystem fired it.
evidence: WandSystem.ts:433 `type: 'iceshard', life: 180`; Spells.ts:241 `type: 'iceshard', life: 140`; WandSystem.ts:450 `type: 'wisp', life: 240`; Spells.ts:249 `type: 'wisp', life: 260`.
reco: Centralize per-type projectile lifetimes (and velocity/gravity) in one shared table consumed by both WandSystem.castActionAt and Spells, so a spell behaves identically regardless of spawner.
verify: Verified the literals diverge: WandSystem.ts:433 iceshard life:180 vs Spells.ts:241 iceshard life:140 (frostshard branch); WandSystem.ts:450 wisp life:240 vs Spells.ts:249 wisp life:260. Not load-bearing port constants (no docs/PORTING.md justification; behavior is otherwise identical), so genuine accidental drift. Nuance lowering severity: per finding 1, the Spells path is gallery/builder-preview only and the WandSystem path is live play, so the divergence affects play-vs-preview consistency rather than gameplay balance directly. Correctly rated low.

title: Workspace.resizePanel hardcodes a 120..720 clamp that overrides per-panel registry min/max
desc: DockHost.resizePanel first clamps the requested size to the panel spec's [minSize, maxSize] (e.g. builder-virtual-world allows up to 760), then delegates to Workspace.resizePanel which re-clamps unconditionally to Math.max(120, Math.min(720, size)). The second clamp is narrower than some specs and silently wins, so panels whose registry maxSize exceeds 720 (builder-virtual-world maxSize 760) can never actually be resized past 720, and panels with minSize > 120 are not enforced by this path (only by the caller). The two clamps encode the same concern in two places with different magic numbers.
evidence: DockHost.resizePanel: resizePanel(this.layout, id, Math.max(spec.minSize ?? 120, Math.min(spec.maxSize ?? 720, size))). Workspace.resizePanel: if (panel && Number.isFinite(size)) panel.size = Math.max(120, Math.min(720, size)). builder-virtual-world spec sets maxSize: 760.
reco: Make Workspace.resizePanel a pure setter (store the already-clamped size, or accept min/max params) and keep clamping authority solely in DockHost/registry, so per-panel limits are honored and the 120/720 magic numbers live in one place.
verify: Verified Workspace.resizePanel (line 234) hardcodes Math.max(120, Math.min(720, size)) while DockHost.resizePanel (DockHost.ts:79) first clamps to spec [minSize..maxSize], and builder-virtual-world spec maxSize is 760 (PanelRegistry.ts:139). So the inner 720 cap does silently override the 760 spec — real two-clamp inconsistency. BUT the practical 'can never resize past 720' effect does not manifest in production: DockHost.resizePanel is never called by Builder.ts (only by tests/editor-ui.test.ts:1165); the real splitter-drag path is Builder.resizeDock (Builder.ts:2618) which caps at dock max 620 before spec.maxSize even applies. So it is a latent code-smell, not a user-visible bug. Downgraded medium->low.

title: Sanctum.close() unconditionally clears ctx.state.paused, breaking the pause-ownership pattern
desc: Sanctum.open/openShop set ctx.state.paused = true and close() sets it back to false unconditionally, without remembering the prior pause state. This diverges from the deliberate pattern elsewhere: CardOfferOverlay saves this.wasPaused and restores it, and PauseOverlay only releases a pause that ESC itself took (guarded by this.active). If the Sanctum is ever opened while the game is already paused by another source, closing it force-resumes the game. In current flows the portal/refuge interaction happens during active play so it is not hit today, but it is a latent state-clobber and an inconsistency with the two adjacent overlays.
evidence: open(): ctx.state.paused = true; close(): this.ctx.state.paused = false; (no wasPaused capture). Compare CardOfferOverlay.open: this.wasPaused = this.ctx.state.paused; ... choose(): this.ctx.state.paused = this.wasPaused.
reco: Capture wasPaused on open/openShop and restore it on close, matching CardOfferOverlay, so Sanctum cannot un-pause a pause it did not create.
verify: Verified Sanctum.open/openShop set ctx.state.paused = true (lines 67, 122) and close() sets it = false unconditionally (line 258) with no wasPaused capture. CardOfferOverlay does save/restore (CardOfferOverlay.ts:28-29, 111) and PauseOverlay guards with this.active ('someone else paused', PauseOverlay.ts:23). The pattern divergence is real. However the finding itself admits it is latent/unreachable in current flows (portal interaction happens during active play). For a not-currently-reachable state clobber, medium overstates it; downgraded to low.

title: Context-inspector sliders never resync on paramsChanged, contradicting the panel's own doc
desc: generateContextInspector wires per-material/per-spell sliders with one-way input listeners and no resync hook. Only the four global sliders subscribe to paramsChanged (line 137). So if a material/spell param is changed elsewhere while that material is selected (console `param`, Builder mirror, a reset), the context sliders display stale values until the tool is re-selected. The class doc at lines 119-121 asserts the panel 'can never show a stale number', which is only true for the global sliders, not the context inspector it also owns.
evidence: wireGlobalControls: ctx.events.on('paramsChanged', () => bindings.forEach((b) => b.resync())). generateContextInspector adds dyn-input listeners but registers no paramsChanged subscription and exposes no resync, yet the surrounding doc claims staleness is impossible.
reco: Either subscribe the context inspector to paramsChanged (re-read fields[propKey] into each dyn-input/dyn-val on event) or narrow the doc comment to the global sliders so it no longer over-promises.
verify: Verified generateContextInspector (Inspector.ts:108-115) wires dyn-input listeners with no paramsChanged subscription/resync, while wireGlobalControls (line 137) does subscribe its bindings. So context sliders can go stale if a material/spell param is changed elsewhere while selected — real. BUT the finding misattributes the doc: the 'panel can never show a stale number' text is a METHOD-LOCAL comment inside wireGlobalControls (lines 121-123) explicitly scoped to 'these very sliders', not a class doc covering the context inspector. The class doc is at lines 53-55. The 'contradicts its own doc' framing is weak/overstated; the underlying staleness gap is a minor maintainability nit. Downgraded medium->low.

title: bindSelect builds <option> markup via unescaped innerHTML interpolation
desc: bindSelect populates the select with `opts.options.map(o => `<option value="${o.value}">${o.label}</option>`).join('')` and assigns it to innerHTML without escaping value or label. The only current caller (Toolbar biome select) passes trusted BIOMES-derived strings, so there is no live XSS, but the helper is a shared, reusable binding primitive and the codebase already has escapeAttr/escapeHtml in editor/Fields.ts for exactly this. A future caller feeding user-supplied option text would inject markup.
evidence: select.innerHTML = opts.options.map((o) => `<option value="${o.value}">${o.label}</option>`).join('');
reco: Escape value/label with escapeAttr/escapeHtml (re-exported from editor/Fields), or build options with document.createElement('option') + textContent to make the helper injection-safe by construction.
verify: Verified domBind.ts:55 builds <option> via unescaped template-string innerHTML. Confirmed the sole caller is Toolbar.ts:134, passing trusted Object.keys(BIOMES) ids and BIOMES[id].name (static data) — so no live XSS. escapeHtml/escapeAttr do exist (Fields.ts:189,198). Accurate low-severity defensive-coding nit on a shared primitive.

title: PopoverHost registers document-level capture listeners with no dispose(), across 4 long-lived instances
desc: The PopoverHost constructor adds capture-phase keydown and scroll listeners on document plus a window resize listener, but the class exposes no dispose()/teardown. Four instances are created (Toolbar, Minimap, Builder, prefabPanel), so every document scroll/resize fires four handlers, each iterating that host's node map to hide popovers. All four owners are app-lifetime singletons so this is not a growing leak, but it is an asymmetry: MenuHost and Tabs.wireTabStrip both provide dispose()/disconnect, and a capture-phase document scroll listener that always runs is a small steady cost and a refactor hazard if PopoverHost ever becomes per-view.
evidence: constructor: this.doc.addEventListener('keydown', this.onKeyDown, true); this.doc.addEventListener('scroll', this.onScroll, true); this.doc.defaultView?.addEventListener('resize', this.onResize, ...). No removeEventListener anywhere; grep shows 4 `new PopoverHost()` sites.
reco: Add a dispose() that removes the three listeners and clears the node map, mirroring MenuHost.dispose(), even if owners are currently permanent.
verify: Verified PopoverHost constructor adds document keydown(capture)/scroll(capture) + window resize listeners (lines 60-62) with no dispose/removeEventListener anywhere in the class. Confirmed exactly 4 `new PopoverHost()` sites (Builder.ts:767, prefabPanel.ts:41, Minimap.ts:692, Toolbar.ts:20), all app-lifetime singleton fields. Factually correct, but as the finding itself states this is not a growing leak — an asymmetry/refactor-hazard only. Borderline noise; low is right.

title: LevelStore load-from-localStorage path lacks try/catch and structural validation
desc: applySave calls rleDecode(save.rle, ...) (atob throws on malformed base64) and iterates `for (const [i, v] of save.life)` / save.charge without checking they are arrays. The import path (level-import change handler) wraps applySave in try/catch and validates `save.v === 1 && typeof save.rle === 'string'`, so a crafted file throwing inside applySave is caught and shows 'Not a valid level file'. The btn-level-load path, however, calls applySave with no try/catch and no validation beyond `if (!save)`; a tampered localStorage entry (missing life array, or non-base64 rle) throws an uncaught error / unhandled rejection in the click handler. No data corruption occurs (TypedArray.fill clamps OOB writes), but the failure is unhandled rather than producing the 'Load Failed' alert the code intends.
evidence: btn-level-load: const save = loadLibrary()[name]; ... if (!this.applySave(save)) await appDialog.alert(...). applySave: rleDecode(save.rle, w.types); for (const [i, v] of save.life) ... — no Array.isArray guards; the import handler is the only path with try/catch.
reco: Validate in applySave (Array.isArray(save.life)/charge, typeof save.rle === 'string') returning false on failure, and/or wrap the load handler in try/catch so tampered storage degrades to the existing 'Load Failed' alert.
verify: Verified btn-level-load handler (LevelStore.ts:123-139) calls applySave with only `if (!save) return`, no try/catch. applySave calls rleDecode(save.rle,...) which does atob(rle) (rle.ts:22) — throws DOMException on malformed base64 — and iterates `for (const [i,v] of save.life)/save.charge` with no Array.isArray guards (lines 92-93). The import handler (169-194) is the only path with try/catch + `save.v===1 && typeof save.rle==='string'` validation. So a tampered localStorage entry throws an unhandled rejection instead of the intended 'Load Failed' alert. No data corruption. Accurate low.

title: Dead `case 'section'` branch in renderInspectorItem
desc: renderInspectorItems intercepts every item with kind === 'section' (flush + continue) before ever calling renderInspectorItem, and renderInspectorItem is private with that single call site. The `case 'section'` arm in renderInspectorItem (which renders a bare <div class="bi-head">) is therefore unreachable. It also duplicates section markup that the real section path produces via editorSectionHtml, so it is misleading dead code.
evidence: Loop: if (item.kind === 'section') { flush(); current = item; ...; continue; } body.push(renderInspectorItem(item)); — renderInspectorItem is only invoked here, after sections are already removed, yet defines case 'section': return `<div class="bi-head"...`.
reco: Remove the unreachable case 'section' arm (and narrow renderInspectorItem's parameter type to the non-section union) so the switch reflects reality.
verify: Verified the render loop (InspectorSchema.ts:156-164) intercepts `item.kind === 'section'` with flush()+continue before calling renderInspectorItem, which is the only call site (line 163). The `case 'section'` arm in renderInspectorItem (lines 181-184) is therefore unreachable dead code, and it duplicates section markup the real path produces via editorSectionHtml. Confirmed dead/misleading code, low.

title: DEFAULT_BUILDER_LAYOUT and workspacePresetLayout omit the registered builder-runtime panel
desc: BUILDER_PANEL_SPECS registers builder-runtime, but the hand-maintained DEFAULT_BUILDER_LAYOUT panel list (and therefore cloneDefaultBuilderLayout and every workspacePresetLayout result, which sanitize against DEFAULT_BUILDER_LAYOUT.panels) omit it. The Builder's actual DockHost re-sanitizes through PanelRegistry.sanitizeLayout (whose `known` map includes builder-runtime), so the panel is recovered there; but the two parallel default sources have drifted and any consumer that uses loadWorkspaceLayout/preset output without the registry pass would silently lack builder-runtime. This is exactly the dual-source-of-truth drift PanelRegistry was meant to eliminate.
evidence: DEFAULT_BUILDER_LAYOUT.panels lists 15 ids ending at dev-console with no builder-runtime; BUILDER_PANEL_SPECS includes { id: 'builder-runtime', ... }. workspacePresetLayout calls sanitizeWorkspaceLayout(layout) with the default DEFAULT_BUILDER_LAYOUT.panels.
reco: Derive DEFAULT_BUILDER_LAYOUT (or at least the preset base) from BUILDER_PANEL_SPECS, or add builder-runtime to the literal, so the registry and the literal default cannot diverge.
verify: Verified DEFAULT_BUILDER_LAYOUT.panels (Workspace.ts:46-62) lists 15 ids ending at dev-console with no builder-runtime, while BUILDER_PANEL_SPECS includes builder-runtime (PanelRegistry.ts:288). workspacePresetLayout sanitizes against DEFAULT_BUILDER_LAYOUT.panels. In production this is recovered: DockHost constructor calls registry.sanitizeLayout (DockHost.ts:24) and the registry knows builder-runtime, so the live Builder always restores it. Real dual-source drift but recovered in practice; low (maintainability).

title: Hud.update re-resolves DOM nodes via getElementById every frame in the 60Hz tick path
desc: Hud.update runs every game tick (60Hz) and calls el(...) (document.getElementById) for many fixed nodes on each call, several of them twice (e.g. el('hp-fill') at 322 and 330, el('mana-fill') at 325 and 333, el('hud-gold') twice). The HUD already caches flask/hotbar slot element references in arrays, so the pattern exists; the vitals/gold/key/cards nodes are static and could be cached identically. Plus per-frame string concatenation and nextCastSlots()/nextWandSentence() work. This is the tick path, not the per-cell sim loop, so impact is modest, but it is avoidable repeated DOM lookups and minor GC churn on the hottest UI function.
evidence: el('hp-fill') called at lines 322 and 330; el('mana-fill') at 325 and 333; el('hud-gold') at 342 and 345; all inside update(ctx) which is the per-frame HUD hook.
reco: Cache the static vitals/gold/key/cards element references in the constructor (as already done for flaskSlots/hotbarSlots) and reuse them in update().
verify: Verified Hud.update re-resolves static nodes via el()=getElementById repeatedly — hp-fill at 322 & 330, mana-fill at 325 & 333, hud-gold at 342 & 345, plus key/cards — while flask/hotbar slots are already cached in arrays. One inaccuracy: update runs at ~30Hz not 60Hz — Game.ts:454 gates it with `frameCount % 2 === 0`. Substance (avoidable repeated DOM lookups on the hottest UI fn) holds. Low is appropriate; tick path, not sim loop.

title: Dead play-mode bomb-charge release branch in InputManager.onMouseUp (pre-wand-system vestige)
desc: The mouse-up handler has a block, gated on ctx.state.mode === 'play', that releases a charged bomb throw when ctx.input.bombCharge >= 0 && ctx.player.spell === 'bomb'. In PLAY mode neither precondition can ever be true: player.firing routes through ctx.wands.fire(ctx) (Player.ts:1766), not Spells.firePlayerSpell(), so bombCharge is never incremented in play; and player.spell is only ever reassigned in BUILD mode (InputManager.ts:434, defaults to 'bolt' at Player.ts:154), so it is never 'bomb' during play. The whole branch (manaCost spend, projectile push, charge tone) is unreachable.
evidence: onMouseUp guard: `if (ctx.input.bombCharge >= 0 && ctx.state.mode === 'play' && !ctx.player.dead && !ctx.player.climbing && ctx.player.spell === 'bomb')`. firePlayerSpell (the only writer of bombCharge) is only invoked from builder/gallery.ts; the real play loop calls ctx.wands.fire(ctx). player.spell is assigned only at InputManager.ts:434 inside the `mode !== 'play'` branch.
reco: Remove the dead bomb-charge release block from onMouseUp (and the matching bombCharge handling now only meaningful to the gallery preview), or, if the legacy fixed-spell path is still intended to be reachable, route it explicitly. Leaving it implies a play feature that does not exist.
verify: Verified the chain: in play mode firing routes through ctx.wands.fire(ctx) at Player.ts:1766 (not firePlayerSpell), and WandSystem.ts never references player.spell. bombCharge is only set >=0 by Spells.firePlayerSpell (Spells.ts:217), which in production is called only from builder/gallery.ts playtest, never the live play loop. player.spell is assigned only at InputManager.ts:434 inside the mode!=='play' branch (default 'bolt' at Player.ts:154). transients.ts:50 also resets bombCharge=-1 on mode transition. So the onMouseUp branch (256-285) requiring bombCharge>=0 AND spell==='bomb' in play is unreachable. Real dead/vestige code, but pure maintainability noise with zero user impact, so downgraded medium->low.

title: Config comments contradict their current values (ambient, goreBlood)
desc: Two GLOBAL_PARAMS comments describe values that no longer match the literals. `ambient: 0.36` is preceded by a comment saying it was 'Raised from the original 0.14' and that '0.18 keeps the caves moody' — the value is 0.36, not 0.18. `goreBlood: 2.3` is preceded by 'Blood-specific gore dialed up (1.0 -> 1.3)' — the value is 2.3, not 1.3. The comments were not updated when the numbers were retuned, which is misleading for anyone tuning lighting/gore.
evidence: `// 0.18 keeps the caves moody ...` then `ambient: 0.36,`; `// Blood-specific gore dialed up (1.0 -> 1.3) ...` then `goreBlood: 2.3,`.
reco: Update the two comments to reflect the shipped values (0.36 ambient, 2.3 goreBlood), or restate them as ratios/intent rather than concrete numbers that drift.
verify: params.ts:24-26 comment says '0.18 keeps the caves moody' but ambient is 0.36; params.ts:28-30 comment says 'gore dialed up (1.0 -> 1.3)' but goreBlood is actually 4.0 (the finding mis-states it as 2.3, but the substantive claim — comment contradicts value — is correct for both). Stale comments, low severity.

title: Exported populationForDepth is dead code (superseded by populationForLevel)
desc: populationForDepth is exported but never imported anywhere in src/ (only referenced in docs/UPGRADE-DELTA.md). The live population path is populationForLevel (used by content/registry.ts and the levels system). The two functions encode subtly different depth/kind curves, so keeping the unused one around invites accidental use of the stale formula.
evidence: grep for populationForDepth across src/**/*.ts returns only its own definition at worldgraph.ts:50; all callers use populationForLevel.
reco: Delete populationForDepth (and its doc references) or, if it is intentionally kept for reference, mark it clearly as unused/legacy. Don't leave two divergent population formulas exported.
verify: Confirmed populationForDepth (worldgraph.ts:50) is referenced nowhere in src/ except its own definition (grep only hits docs/UPGRADE-DELTA.md otherwise). The live path is populationForLevel (worldgraph.ts:66, used by registry.ts and Levels.ts), which encodes a different curve (total=24+depth*6, biome-weighted) than the dead one (slime 12+depth*3, etc). Genuinely unused dead export. Low.

title: Eyedropper writes currentElement with an `as never` cast
desc: In the sandbox eyedropper fallback, a raw number from world.types is assigned to ctx.state.currentElement (typed Cell) via `t as never`. `as never` defeats all type checking on the assignment (it would accept literally any value), where the intended and far safer narrowing is `t as Cell`. CLAUDE.md invariant #7 prohibits unsafe casts; `as never` is the most unsafe form available.
evidence: `ctx.state.currentElement = t as never;` where `const t = ctx.world.types[ctx.world.idx(coords.x, coords.y)]` (a number).
reco: Use `t as Cell` (the value already came from the cell-type plane), or validate it is a known Cell id before assigning. Avoid `as never`.
verify: InputManager.ts:203 does `ctx.state.currentElement = t as never;` where t is a raw number from ctx.world.types (Uint8Array) and currentElement is typed Cell (types.ts:772). `as never` is an unsafe double-assertion that bypasses narrowing; `as Cell` is the intended form. The finding's 'accepts any value' phrasing is imprecise (number->never->Cell works because never is assignable to Cell), but it is a real unsafe-cast smell against invariant #7's spirit. Low.

title: rleDecode / base64ToBytes trust external save input without structural validation
desc: rleDecode/base64ToBytes are the codecs for save data (and share codes, per the module header). rleDecode silently ignores a trailing partial triple (loop bound `i + 2 < bin.length`) and never checks that the decoded run total matches `into.length` — a truncated or malformed payload decodes into a partially-filled grid with no error, leaving stale/zero cells. atob() also throws on invalid base64; callers must wrap it. For a save/share-code path this is unvalidated external input.
evidence: `for (let i = 0; i + 2 < bin.length; i += 3) { ... into.fill(t, pos, pos + run); pos += run; }` with no final assertion that pos === into.length, and atob() not guarded inside the function.
reco: After decoding, verify the filled length equals the expected cell count (or return a success flag) and ensure every caller wraps the atob path in try/catch with a clear fallback. Treat decode failure as a corrupt-save signal rather than silently proceeding.
verify: rle.ts:24 loops `i + 2 < bin.length` (silently drops a trailing partial triple) and never asserts pos===into.length, so a truncated payload partial-fills with no error; atob() at line 22 is unguarded and throws on malformed base64. Verified the share/import call site applyWorldLayer (document.ts:289) does not wrap rleDecode in try/catch. Real robustness gap on the save/share-code path, but impact is limited to local authoring (partial grid or uncaught throw), so low is right.

title: Redundant always-true `mode === 'play'` guard after an early return in onKeyDown
desc: onKeyDown does `if (ctx.state.mode !== 'play') { ...; return; }` and then `if (ctx.state.mode === 'play') { ... }`. After the first block's unconditional return, the second condition is always true, so the wrapping `if` is dead scaffolding that obscures control flow (and pairs with the duplicated movement-key matching split across both blocks).
evidence: Line 418 returns for all non-play modes; line 441 re-tests `=== 'play'` which can no longer be false.
reco: Drop the redundant `if (ctx.state.mode === 'play')` wrapper (keep the body) and consider consolidating the movement-key matching that is duplicated between the two mode branches.
verify: Verified onKeyDown: the `if (ctx.state.mode !== 'play') { ... }` block ends with an unconditional `return;` at InputManager.ts:438, so the following `if (ctx.state.mode === 'play')` at line 441 can never be false. Redundant always-true guard / dead scaffolding. Pure maintainability, low.

title: Three dead exported functions in heavily-modified config/backdrop.ts
desc: cloneBackdropSettings, copyBackdropSettingsInto, and resolveBackdropLayersForRuntime are each exported but appear exactly once in the repo (their own declaration). backdrop.ts is a currently-modified file, so this is freshly-introduced over-export / dead API rather than legacy cruft. Sibling exports in the same file (resolveBackdropProfile, sanitizeBackdropGrade) ARE used internally, so the module is live — just these three are not.
evidence: `grep -rc` per name: cloneBackdropSettings, copyBackdropSettingsInto, resolveBackdropLayersForRuntime each = 1 occurrence (declaration) in backdrop.ts and 0 elsewhere.
reco: Remove the three unused functions (or unexport+use them if they were meant to back the runtime layer resolution / settings copy paths). Confirm during the backdrop change that the runtime path uses whatever replaced resolveBackdropLayersForRuntime.
verify: cloneBackdropSettings (118), copyBackdropSettingsInto (122), resolveBackdropLayersForRuntime (153) each have exactly one occurrence (their declaration). The finding correctly notes resolveBackdropProfile/sanitizeBackdropGrade ARE used internally (verified: resolveBackdropProfile called at 142/150, used by ComposeShader/FrameComposer/WebGpuLiveCompose via resolveBackdropProfileForRuntime). Freshly-introduced dead exports in a modified file; low fair.

title: Cluster of unused speculative API in world/virtual (coords.ts, hash.ts, HerringboneTiles.ts) hidden behind barrel export *
desc: The world/virtual subsystem ships a barrel (index.ts does `export *` for every submodule), which masks that a sizeable set of helpers are never consumed: in hash.ts, fnv1aBytes/hashCoord3/signedUnitHash/hash3i; in coords.ts, worldToChunk/localInChunk/chunksForRect; in HerringboneTiles.ts, tileCoordForWorld/tileOrigin/orientationForTile. Each occurs exactly once (its declaration) across src+tests+scripts. The live code uses the integer-hash variants (hash2i/unitHash2i) and chunkRect/biomeAtWorld, so these are parallel/earlier helpers kept warm by the barrel. ChunkGenerator.getWorldgenWasmBackend() is similarly dead (internal reads use the module var directly; setter is used by tests, getter by no one).
evidence: Each name returns a single occurrence from `grep -rc "\bNAME\b" src tests scripts`. ChunkGenerator.ts:343,643 read `worldgenWasmBackend` directly, never the getter at line 41.
reco: Prune the unused helpers (or stop `export *` and export only the live surface). A barrel that re-exports everything turns dead internals into apparent public API and defeats unused-export detection.
verify: Mostly correct but partially overstated. Genuinely dead (single occurrence): hash.ts fnv1aBytes/hashCoord3/signedUnitHash/hash3i, coords.ts worldToChunk/localInChunk/chunksForRect, HerringboneTiles.ts tileCoordForWorld, and ChunkGenerator getWorldgenWasmBackend (reads use module var directly at 343/643). CORRECTION: the finding wrongly lists tileOrigin and orientationForTile as dead — both ARE used internally in HerringboneTiles.ts (tileOrigin at 71/75/95, orientationForTile at 46). The barrel-masking thesis still holds for the remainder; low fair.

title: Four dead field-builder wrappers in ui/editor/Fields.ts (partial-dead helper family)
desc: Fields.ts exports a family of one-line wrappers that each just call fieldRow({...field, kind:'X'}). Of these, numberField/checkboxField/selectField/vec2Field are used in other files, but sliderField, colorField, textField, and swatchField each appear only in Fields.ts itself (declaration only). They are an over-built API: a complete set of typed constructors was written but half are unconsumed.
evidence: Per-name file count: sliderField, colorField, textField, swatchField each match in exactly 1 file (Fields.ts); the other four match 2 files.
reco: Drop the four unused wrappers; re-add only when a caller needs them. The 'kind' field can be passed inline at the rare call site.
verify: Fields.ts:71-93: sliderField/colorField/textField/swatchField each match only 1 file (Fields.ts declaration) while numberField/checkboxField/selectField/vec2Field match 2 files (used elsewhere). Over-built typed-constructor family with half unconsumed. Confirmed; low fair.

lines: core/math.ts:1 (clamp). Local redefs: src/ui/editor/PopoverHost.ts:238, src/builder/runtimeOverlay.ts:300, src/world/fortress.ts:6 (clamp); src/render/Camera.ts:10 + src/world/virtual/ChunkGenerator.ts:1577,1581 (smoothStep/smoothstep/lerp)
title: Small math helpers (clamp, smoothstep, lerp) re-implemented locally instead of imported from core/math
desc: clamp(v,a,b) is exported from core/math.ts (a foundation module imported by ~18 files) yet is re-defined locally three times (PopoverHost, runtimeOverlay, fortress). smoothstep and lerp are not in core/math but are re-implemented in Camera.ts and ChunkGenerator.ts (and the assembly worldgen). These are trivial 1-liners, so the cost is low, but it is inconsistent: Camera.ts already imports clamp from core/math while defining its own smoothStep. Note: ChunkGenerator's value-noise/lerp intentionally differs from core/math.valueNoise (different lattice/seed mixing for the virtual world), so do NOT blindly merge those — only the literal clamp/smoothstep/lerp 1-liners are safe to share.
evidence: PopoverHost.ts:238 `function clamp(v,min,max){return Math.max(min,Math.min(max,v));}`; runtimeOverlay.ts:300 identical; fortress.ts:6 arrow form. Camera.ts:10 `function smoothStep(t){return t*t*(3-2*t);}` duplicated at ChunkGenerator.ts:1577.
reco: Import clamp from @/core/math in PopoverHost, runtimeOverlay, fortress (core/ is importable from anywhere). Add smoothstep/lerp to core/math and reuse in Camera; leave ChunkGenerator's noise math alone (deliberately distinct).
verify: core/math.ts:1 exports clamp; PopoverHost.ts:238, runtimeOverlay.ts:300, fortress.ts:6 each redefine clamp locally. core/math does NOT export smoothstep/lerp; Camera.ts:10 defines its own smoothStep (while importing clamp from core/math), duplicated by ChunkGenerator.ts:1577/1581. The caveat that ChunkGenerator's value-noise/lerp intentionally differs and must not be blindly merged is correct and respects invariant 4. Low fair.

lines: ProjectAssetStore.ts: requestProjectAssetDirectory (single-occurrence) and sanitizeProjectAssetEntry; AssetDatabase.ts: assetRecordSourceKey; AssetStore.ts: sanitizeImportReport; sprites.ts: SPRITE_DIM_CAP/SPRITE_FRAME_CAP/SPRITE_ENCODED_CAP-style caps; pixmap.ts: UNKNOWN_REPORT_CAP; png.ts: PNG_DIM_CAP
title: Dead constants and sanitizer helpers scattered in asset/import subsystem
desc: Several validation/sanitizer helpers and cap constants in the builder asset-import pipeline are exported but referenced only in their declaring file (or not at all). assetRecordSourceKey and requestProjectAssetDirectory each occur exactly once (declaration). These look like input-validation utilities written ahead of (or after) the wiring that would use them. Because this is the path that ingests external PNG/JSON/asset bundles, leaving half-wired sanitizers around is a maintainability hazard — a reviewer can't tell which guards are actually enforced on imported data.
evidence: assetRecordSourceKey, requestProjectAssetDirectory => 1 occurrence each across src+tests+scripts. Caps like SPRITE_DIM_CAP exported but used only within sprites.ts.
reco: Audit the asset-import validation surface: unexport caps that are file-internal, and either wire the sanitizers (assetRecordSourceKey, requestProjectAssetDirectory, sanitizeProjectAssetEntry) into the import path with a test or delete them so the enforced-guard set is unambiguous.
verify: Partially substantiated. Verified genuinely-dead single-occurrence exports: assetRecordSourceKey (AssetDatabase.ts:262) and requestProjectAssetDirectory (ProjectAssetStore.ts:639 — the actually-called function is the distinct requestProjectAssetDirectoryResult). The other bundled items are over-exported-but-USED internally, not dead (sanitizeProjectAssetEntry 10 occ, sanitizeImportReport 3, SPRITE_*_CAP 4-6, PNG_DIM_CAP 3, UNKNOWN_REPORT_CAP 2) — the finding's own text concedes these are 'used only within' their file. The maintainability-hazard framing is mild; low fair.

title: FNV-1a-32 base constants hand-inlined in domain hashes that could share a primitive
desc: Beyond the verbatim string-FNV copies, two more places open-code FNV-1a-32 with the 2166136261/16777619 constants for domain-specific hashing (terrain region hash; screenshot pixel-sample hash). These are not pure copy-paste (different mixing per element), so they are more defensible — but combined with the four string copies it shows the codebase has no single shared 32-bit hash primitive, so the magic constants are sprinkled across ~8 sites. Low severity because each variant's mixing genuinely differs.
evidence: Builder.ts:8058 `let hash = 2166136261 >>> 0; ... hash = Math.imul(hash, 16777619) >>> 0;`; commands.ts:896 same constants with different per-pixel mix.
reco: Provide a tiny shared `fnv1a32` accumulator primitive (init/step/finish) in core so domain hashers compose it instead of re-typing the constants. Lower priority than the string-FNV consolidation.
verify: Verified Builder.ts:8058 prefabPlacementTerrainHash and commands.ts:896 screenshot sampleHash both open-code FNV-1a-32 (2166136261 / 16777619) with element-specific mixing that differs from the string fold. The finding correctly classifies these as more defensible (genuinely different per-element mix) and the observation that the codebase lacks a single shared 32-bit hash primitive is valid. Low fair.

title: life/charge save restore skips the bounds guard that colorOverrides applies
desc: In restoreLevel, the colorOverrides restore explicitly guards each saved index `if (i < 0 || i >= world.colors.length ...) continue` (1557-1558), but the immediately-preceding life and charge restores write blob.life/blob.charge pairs with no index validation (`world.life[i] = v`, `world.setChargeAt(i, v)`). For the typed-array writes an OOB index is silently ignored (no throw), but setChargeAt also mutates world.activeCharges membership, so an out-of-range index from a tampered/corrupt save can poison the active-charge set. The author clearly knew to validate indices (colorOverrides does) and the inconsistency is an easy correctness hazard.
evidence: `for (const [i, v] of blob.life) world.life[i] = v;` and `for (const [i, v] of blob.charge) world.setChargeAt(i, v);` (1555-1556) — no `i` range check, unlike the colorOverrides loop two lines below.
reco: Apply the same `i >= 0 && i < world.life.length` guard to the life and charge restore loops for consistency and to keep setChargeAt's activeCharges set well-formed.
verify: Confirmed. Lines 1555-1556: `for (const [i,v] of blob.life) world.life[i]=v` and `for (const [i,v] of blob.charge) world.setChargeAt(i,v)` have no index guard, while the colorOverrides loop immediately below (1557-1558) explicitly does `if (i<0||i>=world.colors.length...) continue`. setChargeAt (World.ts:97-101) does activeCharges.add(i) when q>0, so an OOB index from a tampered save inserts a junk index into the sparse active-charge set (the typed-array write itself is a silent no-op for OOB). Real inconsistency / minor correctness hazard from untrusted save data; low severity is right.

title: Redundant moved-epoch re-stamp after world.swap in element handlers
desc: After calling w.swap(x,y,tx,ty) (which already stamps moved[a]=moved[b]=movedTick for both cells — see World.swap, World.ts:187-188), the ember handler immediately re-writes `w.moved[w.idx(tx,ty)] = w.movedTick`. These are dead no-op writes. The same redundant pattern appears in newMaterials.ts (162, 199) and vines.ts (56, 83). Harmless but it muddies the invariant (it reads as if swap does NOT stamp moved, which is false) and adds an extra idx() call per move in a sim hot path.
evidence: thermal.ts: `w.swap(x, y, tx, ty); w.moved[w.idx(tx, ty)] = w.movedTick;` — World.swap already sets both endpoints' moved to movedTick.
reco: Delete the redundant `w.moved[...] = w.movedTick` lines that immediately follow a w.swap (keep only the ones that follow an in-place transmutation, e.g. thermal.ts:78, which are legitimate).
verify: Partially correct — confirmed only for thermal.ts. World.swap stamps moved[a]=moved[b]=movedTick (World.ts:187-188), and thermal.ts 104-105/109-110 immediately re-write moved[idx(tx,ty)] (the swap destination b) — a genuine redundant no-op after swap. BUT the claim that newMaterials.ts:162/199 and vines.ts:56/83 are 'the same redundant pattern' is FALSE: those lines stamp moved AFTER a DIRECT cell write (w.types[ni]=Cell.Moss/Fungus/Vines), with NO preceding swap, so they are the necessary/correct way to flag a newly-grown cell as moved — not redundant. The finding mischaracterizes 4 of its 6 cited lines. The thermal-only issue is trivial ai-slop noise; keeping at low.

title: combat modules import world/entities helpers outside the foundation allowlist
desc: Projectiles.ts concretely imports `probeHollow` from '@/world/secrets' (16) and `EnemySpatialIndex` from '@/entities/enemySpatial' (12); Lightning.ts similarly imports '@/entities/enemySpatial' and '@/entities/bodyMaterials'. The Ctx contract limits concrete cross-system imports to the foundation allowlist (config/, core/, sim/CellType, sim/colors, sim/World, sim/stains, sim/brush, render/pixels); world/ and entities/ are not on it. In practice these targets are stateless helpers (a spatial-index data structure, a material registry, a grid-query function), so the coupling is benign, but `world/secrets.probeHollow` in particular reaches a worldgen/secrets module from combat, which is the kind of feature->feature edge the contract is meant to prevent.
evidence: Projectiles.ts:16 `import { probeHollow } from '@/world/secrets';` used at 809; Projectiles.ts:12 / Lightning.ts:4-5 import @/entities/enemySpatial + @/entities/bodyMaterials.
reco: Either expose these as small Ctx surfaces (e.g. ctx.physics.probeHollow / a shared materials accessor) or promote enemySpatial/bodyMaterials to an explicitly-documented shared foundation module so the allowlist matches reality. Low priority — these are pure helpers, not stateful systems.
verify: Facts verified. Projectiles.ts:12 imports EnemySpatialIndex from @/entities/enemySpatial and :16 imports probeHollow from @/world/secrets (used at 809); Lightning.ts:4-5 import @/entities/enemySpatial + @/entities/bodyMaterials. Neither entities/ nor world/ is on the foundation allowlist (config/, core/, sim/CellType, sim/colors, sim/World, sim/stains, sim/brush, render/pixels), so these are concrete cross-system imports outside the Ctx contract. The targets are stateless helpers so coupling is benign; the world/secrets.probeHollow combat->worldgen edge is the most contract-relevant. Low is appropriate.

title: Per-substep full-radius gold-magnet disk scan with no early-out
desc: runHarvesterField runs first in every sim substep (Simulation.processFrame:54). In play mode it unconditionally walks the entire disk of radius 30 (~2800 cells), or 48 with the goldmagnet perk (~7200 cells), around the player every substep checking for Cell.Gold — even when no gold exists in the level. The offset list is cached (good), but the scan itself has no presence check / early-out and is tied to substep cadence; at the default simSpeed=1.0 it is one scan/frame (modest), but simSpeed is a live tuning dial and at higher values this becomes a 6x-per-frame redundant scan. Cheap to bound.
evidence: `for (const { dx, dy } of diskOffsets(rad))` over rad=30/48 every call (48-65); called per substep at Simulation.ts:54 with no gating on whether any gold is nearby.
reco: Gate the magnet scan to once per render frame (e.g. only on the first substep, frameCount-based) and/or skip it when the level has no gold cells; both keep behavior visually identical while removing redundant per-substep scans.
verify: Verified. runHarvesterField (harvester.ts:39) is called at Simulation.processFrame:54, which runs 0-6 substeps per frame (Simulation.ts:31-36, types.ts:1238). In play mode it unconditionally iterates diskOffsets(rad) with rad=30 (~2800 cells) or 48 with goldmagnet (~7200), checking Cell.Gold, with no presence/early-out gate (48-65). Offset list is cached (good) but the per-substep scan is not bounded by whether gold exists, so at simSpeed>1 it's up to 6 full scans/frame. Real but minor; bounded array iteration with a cheap branch. Low is correct.

title: composeTerrainCpu rebuilds the backdrop-layer descriptor array (and N object literals) every frame
desc: On the CPU compose path (the default unless postFx.gpuCompose is enabled), composeTerrainCpu() builds a fresh `activeBackdropLayers` Array and pushes a new `{ pixels, width, opacity, xSamples, ySamples }` object literal per visible backdrop layer, every single frame. This runs in the per-frame render path (compose is called once per tick from Game.tick). The xSamples/ySamples Int32Arrays themselves are already preallocated (backdropSampleX/Y), so only the wrapper array and a handful of small objects churn — but it is steady GC pressure on the hottest path, and the inner Empty-cell loop then does `for (const active of activeBackdropLayers)` for up to VIEW_W*VIEW_H air cells (iterator over a freshly-built array).
evidence: `const activeBackdropLayers: Array<{...}> = [];` then a per-layer `activeBackdropLayers.push({ pixels: layer.pixels, width: layer.width, opacity: setting.opacity, xSamples, ySamples });` inside composeTerrainCpu, with no caching across frames.
reco: Hoist a reusable array field (e.g. `private readonly activeBackdropLayers` with a `length` reset) and a small pool of reusable descriptor objects, or store the per-layer scalars in parallel preallocated arrays indexed by layer slot, so the per-frame compose allocates nothing. The xSamples/ySamples pattern is already done this way — extend it to the wrapper.
verify: Verified FrameComposer.ts:241-274. composeTerrainCpu() does build a fresh activeBackdropLayers array plus one {pixels,width,opacity,xSamples,ySamples} literal per visible backdrop layer every frame, and composeTerrainCpu runs on the per-frame compose path (compose->composeTerrainCpu at line 170, the default branch when gpuCompose is off). xSamples/ySamples ARE preallocated (this.backdropSampleX[i]/backdropSampleY[i]), so only the wrapper array and a handful of small objects churn. The inner air-cell loop later iterates this array. Real but minor: bounded to the few visible backdrop layers per frame, so I downgrade medium->low.

title: compose() allocates a lenses array (and per-blackhole objects) every frame even when no singularities exist
desc: Every frame, compose() does `const lenses: CompositorLens[] = []` then loops all projectiles pushing a `{ cx, cy, R, K }` literal for each blackhole. In the overwhelmingly common case (no black holes) this still allocates a throwaway empty array that is then passed through composeTerrainCpu/beginGpuCompose and read via `lensLen`. It is a tiny allocation, but it is unconditional on the per-frame compose path and could be a reused field cleared to length 0.
evidence: `const lenses: CompositorLens[] = []; for (const pr of ctx.projectiles) { if (pr.type === 'blackhole') { lenses.push({ cx: pr.x, cy: pr.y, R: pr.vortexRad! * 2.1, K: 4 + pr.vortexRad! * 0.16 }); } }`
reco: Make `lenses` a reused instance field (`this.lenses.length = 0`) and reuse pooled lens objects, mirroring how shockwaves are read directly. Low impact given black holes are rare, but trivially removable GC churn.
verify: Verified FrameComposer.ts:144-149. const lenses: CompositorLens[] = [] is allocated unconditionally every frame in compose(), then projectiles are scanned for blackholes. In the common no-blackhole case it is a throwaway empty array passed to composeTerrainCpu/beginGpuCompose. Tiny allocation; low severity is correct.

title: Duplicated per-frame linear scan + closure allocation to find the player-corpse body in three systems
desc: While the player is dead, FrameComposer.drawPlayerRagdoll, Lighting.build, and Camera.update each independently run `ctx.rigidBodies.bodies.find((b) => b.tag === 'player-corpse')` — three separate linear scans over the rigid-body set plus a fresh arrow-function closure allocation each, every frame, all looking up the same single body. The body set is bounded (<=80) so cost is small, but it is redundant work and three copies of the same lookup that can silently diverge.
evidence: Identical `bodies.find((b) => b.tag === 'player-corpse')` appears in FrameComposer.ts:594, render/Lighting.ts:638, and render/Camera.ts:62 (confirmed via grep).
reco: Cache the corpse reference once on death (e.g. expose `ctx.rigidBodies.playerCorpse` set when the corpse body is spawned in Player.kill and cleared on level change), and have all three readers use it. Removes the per-frame scans and the closure allocations, and centralizes the tag contract.
