verify: Grep confirms identical bodies.find((b)=>b.tag==='player-corpse') at FrameComposer.ts:594, render/Lighting.ts:638, render/Camera.ts:62. Each runs only while ctx.player.dead (drawPlayerRagdoll guards mode==='play', Lighting/Camera guard player.dead), so it is three redundant linear scans + closure allocs per frame during death, not universally. Body set bounded. Genuinely redundant/maintainability concern; low is right.

title: unpack01() allocates a fresh {r,g,b} object on every call inside per-frame draw loops
desc: `unpack01(c)` returns `{ r, g, b }` — a new object literal each call. It is invoked in per-frame overlay draws: drawVineStrands calls it once per strand (cold-ish), but drawCauldronBowl calls it once per brewable basin cell scanned every frame the cauldron is on-screen, and drawFlaskEffects calls it per frame. None of these are large counts, so the GC impact is minor, but the helper hands out a fresh object where the three scalars could be returned via out-params or a reused struct (the codebase already does exactly this for Lighting.sample's LightSample).
evidence: `private unpack01(c: number): { r: number; g: number; b: number } { return { r: unpackR(c) / 255, g: unpackG(c) / 255, b: unpackB(c) / 255 }; }` called in drawVineStrands (line 701), drawCauldronBowl (line 921, inside a per-cell loop), drawFlaskEffects (lines 1005, 1045).
reco: Follow the Lighting.sample pattern: return a single reused private struct, or split into unpackR01/unpackG01/unpackB01 scalar helpers and assign locals. Especially worth it for the drawCauldronBowl per-cell loop.
verify: Verified unpack01 at FrameComposer.ts:106-108 returns a fresh {r,g,b} literal. Confirmed call sites: drawVineStrands (701, per strand), drawCauldronBowl (921, inside the per-cell basin scan loop), drawFlaskEffects (1005 and 1045, per frame). Counts are small/bounded so GC impact is minor; low severity accurate. Note drawCauldronBowl/drawFlaskEffects only run when those overlays are active, so it is not truly unconditional, but the finding acknowledges this.

title: Bat predation does a full critter-list scan per bat per frame with no spatial pruning
desc: When a bat loses the player (pDist > 120 or target dead), it scans the entire `ctx.critters.list` every frame looking for a nearby moth (`for (let ci2 = 0; ci2 < critters.length; ci2++)`), with the distance test applied after the loop body. Critters are capped low (moth cap 6, total a few dozen) so this is bounded, but it is an O(bats * critters) scan each frame that runs only because there is no shared spatial structure for critters (enemies already have EnemySpatialIndex). It will not show up until there are many bats and critters simultaneously.
evidence: Inside the bat branch: `const critters = ctx.critters.list; for (let ci2 = 0; ci2 < critters.length; ci2++) { const cr = critters[ci2]; if (cr.kind !== 'moth') continue; ... if (cdx*cdx+cdy*cdy < 70*70) { prey = cr; break; } }` runs every frame each non-hunting bat is awake.
reco: Acceptable for shipped caps; if bat/critter counts grow, gate the scan on a cadence (e.g. only re-seek every Nth frame and cache the prey reference) or reuse a lightweight spatial bucket for critters. Flagging as a latent scaling cost, not a current hotspot.
verify: Verified Enemies.ts:891-917. In the bat branch, when !targetAlive || pDist > 120 it scans the full ctx.critters.list every frame (for ci2=0..critters.length) filtering kind==='moth' and distance-testing with break-on-first-hit. O(bats * critters) with no spatial pruning while enemies have EnemySpatialIndex. Critters are capped low so bounded; low severity is correct. The break on first prey caps inner cost somewhat.

title: Untrusted RLE cell ids are never range-checked; ids >=128 corrupt the GPU compose charge bit
desc: rleDecode fills world.types with raw bytes 0-255. sanitizeWorldLayer only verifies the RLE decodes without throwing — it never checks decoded cell ids are within [0, CELL_COUNT). So an imported document or share code can inject out-of-range materials. The sim treats unknown ids as inert (the isLiquid/isSolid predicates return false, no array is indexed by type), so no crash — but the GPU compose path packs the type into a texture alpha byte as `types[ci] | (charged?0x80:0)` and reads it back as `type = a & 0x7F, charged = (a & 0x80)`. An id with bit 7 set (>=128) is silently reinterpreted as a different, charge-flagged material, and ids overflow the byte. Cell ids are a save ABI capped at 127 by this very packing (documented at CellType.ts:63-69), but nothing on the untrusted load path enforces it.
evidence: ComposeShader lines 342/803/841: `(types[ci] | (charge[ci] !== 0 ? 0x80 : 0)) << 24`; shader line 224-225 `int type = int(cell.a & 0x7Fu); bool charged = (cell.a & 0x80u) != 0u`. sanitizeWorldLayer (document.ts) decodes into a scratch buffer but does not validate ids; restoreLevel (Levels.ts 1545-1550) copies savedTypes[i] into world.types[i] unchanged.
reco: In the RLE-acceptance path (sanitizeWorldLayer, and the expedition restore overlay), clamp/drop any decoded id >= CELL_COUNT to Cell.Empty before it reaches world.types, so off-palette ids cannot masquerade as real charged materials.
verify: Verified sanitizeWorldLayer (document.ts:563-580) only calls rleDecode into a scratch buffer to confirm it doesn't throw, then stores the ORIGINAL layer.rle unchanged — it never checks decoded ids are in [0, CELL_COUNT). It carefully validates biome/seed/paintSeed and routes life/charge/colorOverrides through sanitizeSparsePairs, so the world-id gap is a real asymmetry. There IS an untrusted path: importDocumentFile (Builder.ts:4386) -> sanitizeDocument -> sanitizeWorldLayer (711). ComposeShader packs `types[ci] | (charged?0x80:0)` (342/803/841) and the shader reads `type = a&0x7F, charged = a&0x80` (224-225); CellType.ts:63-69 documents the ids<=127 cap. So an imported id>=128 is reinterpreted as id-128 + charged. Confirmed as a real validation gap on an untrusted input path. Severity reduced to low: the consequence is purely cosmetic/inert (sim treats unknown ids as inert, no crash, no save-ABI corruption — the bad id never round-trips back out as a real material), not the medium-grade type-safety hazard the others are.

title: reviveSavedEnemy copies x/y/hp/maxHp without finite guards while guarding lesser fields
desc: reviveSavedEnemy carefully runs dmgK/timer/attackCd/bobPhase/calmT/recoil/etc through finiteNumber()/nonNegativeInt(), but copies the load-bearing fields x, y, hp, maxHp directly from the SavedEnemyState. A corrupt save with a NaN/Infinity enemy position or hp produces a ghost enemy with broken physics and AI that can wedge the level (and the same blob feeds straight back into the next save). The inconsistency suggests the guard was applied field-by-field and the most important ones were missed.
evidence: Lines 254-262: `x: se.x, y: se.y, ... hp: se.hp, maxHp: se.maxHp,` raw, versus `dmgK: finiteNumber(se.dmgK, 1), timer: nonNegativeInt(se.timer, 0)` immediately below.
reco: Wrap x/y with finiteNumber and hp/maxHp with a positive-finite guard (e.g. maxHp = Math.max(1, finiteNumber(...)); hp clamped to [0, maxHp]).
verify: Verified reviveSavedEnemy (Levels.ts:253-294) copies x:se.x, y:se.y, hp:se.hp, maxHp:se.maxHp raw (256-263) while running dmgK/timer/attackCd/bobPhase/calmT/recoil/fusing/etc through finiteNumber()/nonNegativeInt() immediately below (264-291). The inconsistency is real and the load-bearing fields are the unguarded ones. Reachable via Infinity through JSON (`1e999`) since the top-level save-shape guard doesn't inspect blob.enemies. A NaN/Infinity enemy position breaks physics/AI. Low is right — a wedged ghost enemy is a degraded-but-not-locking failure, and enemies outside the sim window freeze anyway.

title: Expedition life/charge restore writes unvalidated indices, unlike the sanitized Builder path
desc: restoreLevel and LevelStore.applySave iterate blob.life/blob.charge as `for (const [i, v] of ...) world.life[i] = v` / `world.setChargeAt(i, v)` with no bounds or type check on i, whereas the Builder document path routes the same data through sanitizeSparsePairs which clamps index to [0, WIDTH*HEIGHT), dedups, caps count, and clamps the value. Typed-array OOB writes are silent no-ops so this does not crash today, but it is an inconsistent contract: colorOverrides in the very same function (line 1557-1558) DOES bounds-check i, so the asymmetry is an accident, not a decision. An uncapped sparse array could also balloon load time.
evidence: restoreLevel: `for (const [i, v] of blob.life) world.life[i] = v;` and `for (const [i, v] of blob.charge) world.setChargeAt(i, v);` — no guard. Contrast colorOverrides loop directly below: `if (i < 0 || i >= world.colors.length ...) continue;`. document.ts sanitizeSparsePairs is the validated counterpart.
reco: Route expedition blob.life/charge through the same index-clamp + cap logic as sanitizeSparsePairs (or share that helper), matching the colorOverrides guard already present.
verify: Verified restoreLevel (Levels.ts:1555-1556) does `for (const [i,v] of blob.life) world.life[i]=v` and `world.setChargeAt(i,v)` with no bounds check on i, while colorOverrides directly below (1557-1558) DOES guard `if (i<0 || i>=world.colors.length ...) continue`. LevelStore.applySave (92-93) repeats the unguarded pattern. setChargeAt (World.ts:97-102) clamps the VALUE but not the index, and worse, on an OOB i it still calls activeCharges.add(i) (101), polluting the sparse charge index with a bogus key (the typed-array write itself is a silent no-op). The Builder path routes the same data through sanitizeSparsePairs (clamp index, dedup, cap). Real asymmetry, no crash today. Low is correct.

title: reviveSavedPickup is a misleading no-op wrapper around the save-side normalizer
desc: reviveSavedPickup(pickup) just returns snapshotPickupForSave(pickup) — the SAVE-side normalizer — so the RESTORE path performs no validation of the loaded pickup's kind/data (contrast the virtual-scene path which builds pickups through makePickup with kind whitelisting). The name 'revive' implies a load-time sanitize step that does not exist, and the indirection obscures that gap. A corrupt pickup kind/data in a save flows directly into runtime.pickups (only caught if it happens to throw, which it generally won't).
evidence: `function reviveSavedPickup(pickup: Pickup): Pickup { return snapshotPickupForSave(pickup); }` then `pickups: blob.pickups.map(reviveSavedPickup)` at restore. snapshotPickupForSave only clears tome offerPending; it validates nothing.
reco: Either delete reviveSavedPickup and call snapshotPickupForSave directly at the restore site (honest about doing nothing), or make it validate pickup.kind against PickupKind and clamp data fields, matching the rigor of the rest of the resume path.
verify: Verified reviveSavedPickup (Levels.ts:202-204) is literally `return snapshotPickupForSave(pickup)` — the SAVE-side normalizer (196-200) that only clears tome offerPending and validates nothing about kind/data. Restore at 1588 (`blob.pickups.map(reviveSavedPickup)`) thus performs no load-time validation, and the 'revive' name implies a sanitize step that doesn't exist. This is a maintainability/clarity finding (a corrupt pickup kind/data flows into runtime.pickups), correctly rated low — generally won't crash, just misleading naming masking a gap.
